text: We need to create a comprehensive water monitoring service, similar to a weather service. However, without a collective demand for accountability, there won't be sufficient motivation to finance such an initiative. The first time I spoke at a conference was here at TED, eight years ago. As a recent graduate, I wasn't aware that those few moments on stage would shape the questions I would face over the next decade. Like many people in their twenties, I was eager to tackle global issues, particularly those related to water, with my technological solutions. I had much to learn. It was tempting to believe that our major water quality challenges persisted mainly because they were difficult to detect. I assumed all we needed were simpler, quicker, and more affordable sensors. I was mistaken.

While not perfect, this game has been highly enjoyable. The save system and the possibility of sudden death can lead to some frustration. I'm on my first playthrough, and I lost my main character around hour 10 of 20ish. I would have really appreciated a save to reload at that point. I've since picked up a third-party mod/save tool to avoid catastrophic failures. Plus, the aiming reticule is on the small side, and the zoom is underpowered. With all that said, it's been a blast. I'm not usually a zombie fan, but the combination of strategy, missions, first-person shooter, and beat 'em up is great. I've been playing it as often as I can get away... Just one more mission, just one more supply run—or I'm running away and want to get safe before shutting the game down.

T. Donoho (non-privileged).pst This has been a tough couple of weeks. I hope that these daily communications on new developments are helping you understand what's happening with our company. Here's a rundown of events from last week: we executed a commitment letter with JPMorgan and Salomon Smith Barney that will provide us with additional secured credit lines worth $1 billion. Our Northern Natural Gas Company and Transwestern Pipeline Company will support these assets. We now have a total of $4 billion in credit lines, which we will use to pay off short-term commercial debt and maintain liquidity. Our Board of Directors elected a new member to the board - William Powers, Jr., the Dean of the University of Texas School of Law in Austin, Texas.

Yes, this is a nostalgic journey for thirty-somethings who remember (especially in the UK) watching the ever-irritating Timmy Mallett just to catch the five-minute Transformers cartoon. The voice actors for Optimus, Megatron, Bumblebee, and many others have returned and are instantly recognizable. The distinctive cartoon transformation sound is present. Graphically, the cel-shaded presentation is perfect and very reminiscent of Generation One. Beyond that, it's actually a very fun game. The combat mechanics are extremely well done. Transforming mid-fight often becomes necessary and is seamlessly woven into combos, making it quite distinct from High Moon's excellent War for/Fall of Cybertron games. Another distinction is the heavy focus on melee combat, although some enemies (e.g. Seekers) require switching to a ranged blaster.

I'm not sure if Dahl will be attending the rehearsal dinner with me, but I think he'll probably hook up with Jones and the crew instead. I'll likely hook up with you and the group afterwards, and we'll be in touch. You can reach me on my cell at 206-660-6248. I'll have that number with me. As for me, I'll be quoting Mark Guzman, and I work during the day on Fridays. Is Dahl going with you to the rehearsal dinner and all that? If not, I'm sure I'll be grabbing some brews with people after work on Friday, and you're more than welcome to come. Either way, let's hang out after you're done with all your stuff.

Khyatt (non-privileged).pst El Paso tests market for mainline expansion. El Paso Natural Gas apparently received the message when its last open season for 1.2 Bcf/d of firm transportation capacity attracted bids for 14.4 Bcf/d. The pipeline has announced an open season to test market interest in a mainline expansion and to address regulatory concerns that something needs to be done to alleviate the apparent capacity constraint at the California border. El Paso has been under significant pressure from regulators and shippers to expand due to enormous demand and skyrocketing prices in California. In a Jan. 3 letter to the pipeline, Daniel M. Adamson, Director of FERC's Office of Energy Projects, urged El Paso to consider modifying its Line 2000 project to "assist the difficult situation" now facing the California gas market.

1. Everyone is obsessed with one movie or series of movies - what's yours? The Evil Dead series, if it wasn't obvious. I have one of the largest collections of items from that movie series. 2. Can you play an instrument? What is it? I play the bass guitar - oh yes, I rock out. 3. What's your biggest money-wasting vice? Either my compulsive buying of DVDs or any new technology. 4. Do you have any phobias, allergies, or compulsive behaviors? No, no, and I have a habit of organizing the money in my wallet in order from least to greatest at all times. 5. How much wood could a woodchuck chuck if a woodchuck could chuck wood? Because I said so.

I know this may seem unusual, but I believe robots can inspire us to become better humans. Growing up in Bethlehem, Pennsylvania, the birthplace of Bethlehem Steel, I had a unique upbringing. My father, an engineer, would teach me about the inner workings of things. We would collaborate on projects, such as model rockets and slot cars. Here's a photo of the go-kart we built together. That's me behind the wheel, accompanied by my sister and my best friend at the time. And one day, when I was around 10 years old, he came home, and at the dinner table, he announced that for our next project, we were going to build a robot, a robot, a machine that would learn and adapt to its environment, a true marvel of engineering and innovation.

Extreme mass ratio inspirals (EMRIs) can be classified as dry EMRIs and wet EMRIs based on their formation mechanisms. Dry (or "loss-cone") EMRIs, previously considered the main EMRI sources for the Laser Interferometer Space Antenna, are primarily produced by multi-body scattering in the nuclear star cluster and gravitational capture. In this letter, we highlight an alternative EMRI formation channel: wet EMRI formation assisted by the accretion flow around accreting galactic-center massive black holes (MBHs). In this channel, the accretion disk captures stellar-mass black holes that are initially moving on inclined orbits and subsequently drives them to migrate towards the MBH—this process boosts the formation rate of EMRIs in such galaxies by orders of magnitude.

Metal ion implantation into ceramics has emerged as a highly effective and controllable method for tailoring the surface electrical conductivity of ceramic pieces, with a wide range of applications. Notably, this approach enables the grading of voltage drops across high-voltage insulators, thereby significantly increasing the maximum operational voltage that can be applied across the insulator without surface flashover. However, a critical consideration is the long-term stability of the implantation-induced conductivity, particularly when the implanted metal species is prone to oxidation. In this study, we investigate the long-term behavior of the surface conductivity of titanium-implanted alumina, revealing that after an initial decline of approximately 40% in the first few weeks following implantation, the conductivity exhibits a remarkably slow decrease of around 10% over the subsequent year. These findings have significant implications for the development of high-reliability, high-voltage devices and systems that require precise control over the surface electrical properties of ceramic components.

They have effectively resolved the Shadow Warrior (2013) issue, i.e., the boss fights are impressive, weapons feel very nice (albeit not as exceptional as D44M's, but still solid), enemies aren't too spongy (if you're not too reckless with handling elemental and upgrade items). The upgrade system is well-balanced and once you master it, it's not overly tedious. The elemental elements and limited ammo encourage you to use a variety of weapons. Many unique, or rather, cool weapons that remain viable throughout most of the game. The music is excellent, particularly the main theme and, of course, Stan Bush's work.

Deadline looms for Fiat-GM deal; Fiat and General Motors (GM) have until midnight on February 1 to settle a disagreement over a potential takeover. The deadline marks the point at which Fiat will gain the right to sell its car division to GM, as part of an alliance agreed upon in 2000. However, GM, whose European operations are currently losing money, no longer wishes to own the unprofitable Fiat unit. Reports of deadlocked talks sent Fiat shares down 1.2% on Tuesday, after Monday's 4% gain on hopes of a resolution. The US firm is believed to be offering around $2 billion (£1.06 billion) to extricate itself from the arrangement. GM has argued that the deal was voided by Fiat's decision to sell off its finance arm and halve GM's stake through a capital-raising effort.

Warframe is a fun game for the first few hours of play. After those initial enjoyable hours, you'll begin to notice that the game's offerings become repetitive in its combat, enemies, and gameplay. One major highlight is that the developers are working hard and frequently release updates that generally include a lot of content, from sweeping changes to some of the game's systems, to new warframes and other tweaks/features. It's a good game for casual play, though I doubt it will hold your attention beyond a few months unless you really enjoy grinding. The upside to all of this is that the game is free, so I'd recommend giving it a try. If you're looking for a quick fix, try it out.

Our research examines how difference-in-differences (DiD) analyses can be compromised when control and treatment groups exhibit significant disparities, as such mismatches may introduce bias from historical events affecting groups differently. Through simulation studies, we demonstrate that creating a more precisely matched control group—by selecting a subset from the broader control pool—can effectively mitigate this bias.  

In our application, we investigate the mortality impacts of mountaintop removal mining (MRM) using a DiD framework, leveraging the expansion of MRM activities after the 1990 Clean Air Act Amendments. By refining our control group to closely align with the treated group, our analysis yields a 95% confidence interval that encompasses a range of outcomes: from substantial negative effects to no effect and even minor positive effects. This approach underscores the importance of careful control group construction in strengthening causal inference.

The assembly history of the Milky Way is currently being unveiled thanks to the data provided by the Gaia mission. It is likely that the globular cluster system of our galaxy has followed a similarly intricate formation path. To constrain this formation path, we explore the link between the globular clusters and the known merging events that the Milky Way has experienced. To this end, we combined the kinematic information provided by Gaia for almost all galactic clusters with the largest sample of cluster ages available, having carefully corrected for systematic errors. To identify clusters with a common origin, we analyzed their dynamical properties, particularly in the space of integrals of motion. We find that about 40% of the clusters likely formed in situ.

In the summer of 1903, a pivotal moment in American conservation history unfolded in California's breathtaking Yosemite Valley, as President Theodore Roosevelt embarked on a three-day camping excursion. Under the majestic canopy of towering sequoia trees, Roosevelt immersed himself in the unspoiled beauty of nature, braving a snowstorm and engaging in profound conversations with his host, the renowned conservationist John Muir, around the warm glow of the campfire. While Roosevelt's affinity for the outdoors was well-documented, Muir's invitation extended far beyond a leisurely camping trip – it was a call to action, as Yosemite teetered on the brink of destruction. Despite being designated as protected land in 1864, the valley remained vulnerable to the threats of overdevelopment in 1903, caught in the midst of a decades-long struggle to strike a delicate balance between preservation and public access – a challenge that proved far more daunting than it seemed. The seeds of this contentious battle were sown during the 1849 gold rush, when a surge of prospectors flocked to the Sierra Nevada foothills, setting in motion a chain of events that would ultimately determine the fate of Yosemite.

Entanglement is a crucial resource for quantum information processing. A commonly used tool for detecting entanglement is the entanglement witness, where the measurement of the witness operator is guaranteed to be positive for all separable states and may be negative for certain entangled states. In practice, due to the exponentially increasing Hilbert-space dimension with respect to system size, constructing an efficient entanglement witness for general multipartite entangled states is very challenging. For $n$-partite Greenberger-Horne-Zeilinger (GHZ)-like states, the most robust witness scheme requires $n+1$ local measurement settings and can tolerate up to $1/2$ white noise. In comparison, the most efficient witness for GHZ-like states only needs two local measurement settings and can tolerate up to $1/3$ white noise.

Who wouldn’t love wearing a fez? :D This charming puzzle-platformer features a small character in a fez, exploring a vast, branching map while collecting fragments to assemble a cube. The game is delightfully entertaining, and progressing through it is genuinely enjoyable. ^_^ However, it can get a bit frustrating if you aim to collect every cube and unlock all achievements. To find them all, you’ll need to decode various puzzles—some may take mere seconds, while others might push you to consult a guide (*raises hand*—I admit, I gave up. -_-). Speaking of which, "A Complete Guide to FEZ!!!" can be a lifesaver.

Ever have one of those days when you just don't know what the... What the... Anything? No? Well then you should skip the rest of this because it won't make any sense to you. To those of you choosing to continue on, I offer the following observation: "Sometimes I wish I'd been given the manual." Manuals, if done correctly at least, let you know: what things are for, what they can do, how they do the things they do, what happens if you push button X or Y, what to do if things aren't doing what they're supposed to, the phone number and email address of the omniscient guru who can solve every problem—very useful information. Unfortunately, I didn't get a manual with me.

World leaders are gathering in the Swiss mountain resort of Davos for the annual World Economic Forum (WEF). Over 2,000 business and political leaders from around the globe are arriving for a five-day meeting. The agenda will cover issues such as China's economic power and Iraq's future after this Sunday's elections. UK Prime Minister Tony Blair and South African President Thabo Mbeki are among the more than 20 government leaders and heads of state attending the meeting. Unlike previous years, protests against the WE Forum are expected to be muted. Anti-globalization campaigners have called off a demonstration planned for the weekend. The Brazilian city of Porto Alegre will host the rival World Social Forum, scheduled to run in parallel with the WE Forum's more exclusive event in Davos.

Well, things can get incredibly busy here at times...but there’s always room for stress-relieving activities. I’ve discovered that I’m really not great at 8-ball or ping pong! Rick (the Canuck) regularly beats me—we have a ping pong table in the front lobby of our building, so there’s always time for a 'quick round.' Last night, I joined an 8-ball tournament at the Hot Spot (our recreation tent, complete with a popcorn machine, multiple TVs, a jukebox, DVD rentals, a pool table, dartboard, and the ever-present ping pong table). I was actually doing pretty well throughout my entire game—until I scratched on the eight ball. Still, I had a great time—the highlight was the two Dutch soldiers who joined the tournament.

The Weak Gravity Conjecture (WGC) posits that the existence of super-extremal particles is a necessary condition for any consistent quantum theory of gravity. The standard lore suggests that these particles are introduced to ensure that extremal black holes are either unstable or marginally stable, but the implications of this assumption are unclear if it does not occur. This note reveals that, for a generic Einsteinian quantum theory of gravity in Anti-de Sitter space, the stability of extremal black branes is in tension with rigorously proven quantum information theorems about entanglement entropy. To avoid this contradiction, a non-perturbative version of the WGC is required, which reduces to the usual statement at weak coupling. The argument is general and does not rely on either supersymmetry or a particular UV completion, assuming only the validity of Einstein's gravity, effective field theory, and holography.

I have two important questions for you. First, whose legacy do you honor? And second, what principles do you uphold? I always begin my poetry workshops by asking students these questions because poetry can sometimes appear like an antiquated art form associated with old white men, seemingly born into old age, reminiscent of Benjamin Button. I pose these questions to my students and then reveal my answers in the form of three sentences: I am the daughter of Black writers, who descended from freedom fighters that broke their chains and transformed the world. They inspire me. I repeat these words as a mantra before every poetry performance. In fact, just a moment ago, I was over there in the corner, reciting them and making faces.

A groundbreaking study reveals that reducing the legal blood alcohol limit for drivers could save significantly more lives than previously estimated. According to research conducted by University College London, lowering the current limit from 80mg to 50mg of alcohol per 100ml of blood would prevent approximately 65 fatalities annually.  

The findings, published by the Parliamentary Advisory Council for Transport Safety, coincide with upcoming parliamentary debates on the government’s Road Safety Bill. While the proposed legislation addresses penalties for speeding offenses, it does not currently include revisions to drink-driving limits.  

Transport expert Professor Richard Allsop, who led the study, highlights that adopting the stricter limit would also result in 230 fewer serious road injuries each year compared to 2003 data. This change is projected to yield annual economic savings of £119 million for the UK.  

Earlier assessments, such as a 1998 Department of Transport report, had suggested a potential annual reduction of 50 lives under similar measures—underscoring the new study’s more substantial projected benefits.

The novel concepts, perspectives, and challenges in measuring and controlling an open quantum system via sequential schemes are showcased. We demonstrate how similar protocols, relying on repeated quantum measurements and dynamic decoupling control pulses, can enable us to  (i) confine and protect quantum dynamics from decoherence in accordance with the zero-phononics. (ii) analytically predict the probability that a quantum system is transferred into a target quantum state by means of stochastic sequential measurements. (iii) optimally reconstruct the spectral density of environmental noise sources by orthogonalizing in the frequency domain the filter functions driving the designed quantum-sensor. The achievement of these tasks will significantly enhance our capability to observe and manipulate open quantum systems, thereby driving advances in quantum science and technology.

What is a parent? It's not an easy question. Today, we have adoption, stepfamilies, and surrogate mothers. Many parents face tough questions and tough decisions. Should we tell our child about the sperm donation? If so, when? What words should we use? Sperm donors are often referred to as "biological fathers," but should we really be using the word "father"? As a philosopher and social scientist, I have been studying these questions about the concept of parenthood. But today, I will talk to you about what I learned from talking to parents and children. I will show you that they know what matters most in a family, even though their family may look a little different.

I've been overwhelmed with an inexplicable exhaustion for the past two days. The fatigue has been relentless, leaving me feeling utterly drained. Yesterday evening, I returned home, collapsed onto my bed, and slept through the night, only rousing when my mother called me around 8 30 pm. As I slowly came to, it was pitch dark, and I hadn't even bothered to turn on the lights, so for a few disorienting moments, it was just my mother's soothing voice in the darkness, and me struggling to regain my bearings. I couldn't help but wonder why I'm feeling so utterly exhausted... The morning was surprisingly pleasant, with a gentle drizzle outside, a welcome respite from the torrential rain that, I'm told, has been relentless in Kolkata for the past three days.

The orientational dynamics in the isotropic phase of a comb-shaped nematic polymer featuring mesogenic and functional side groups were investigated using Kerr effect and dielectric spectroscopy. For the first time, it was observed that, unlike low-molecular-weight mesogens, the relaxation of electric birefringence in a mesogenic polymer melt above the nematic-isotropic phase transition temperature can be described by a superposition of multiple exponential processes, with two of them being dominant. These primary relaxation mechanisms alternate within a temperature range of approximately fifty degrees. Additionally, dielectric spectroscopy revealed two distinct orientational relaxation processes: the first arising from the rotation of the side mesogenic groups, and the second linked to the motion of the main chain segments.

Someone once said that politics is, of course, "showbiz for ugly people." So, on that basis, I feel like I've really arrived. The other thing to consider is what an honor it is, as a politician, to give a TED Talk, particularly here in the U.K., where the reputation of politics—with the expenses scandal—has sunk so low. There was even a story recently that scientists had considered replacing rats in their experiments with politicians. And someone asked, "Why?" They said, "Well, there's no shortage of politicians, no one really minds what happens to them, and, after all, there are some things that rats just won't do." (Laughter) Now, I know you all love data, so I'm starting with a data-rich slide.

Monopoly has finally worn out its welcome in my life, and I hereby vow never to touch that dreaded iron game piece again. This evening, Andy narrowly defeated me by a few dollars and claimed victory in the game. Perhaps I let him win, considering he's leaving in just a few hours and I wanted him to enjoy his last night here. Whatever the case, it pales in comparison to the massive headache I’m experiencing right now. I've taken some Aleve, which seems to be doing its job; all I need now is some well-earned rest and relaxation. It was your phone that helped me wrap the cord around your neck... At the drive-in.

Attitude: bored. Mood: indifferent, but looking forward to tonight. It’s been a long, hard day in the trenches here today, folks—almost no time to write anything for you readers. Still, it’s good to see Tigger (now Akcron) taking up the reins herself, making an appearance, and talking about things. Not much to say otherwise. Yesterday was Shakespeare on the Green—a lovely play, though I had to leave before the closing curtain, which kinda sucked. Hopefully someday I’ll see an entire Shakespeare play. But I digress. Akcron is right about the shower problems—it sucks. As for laughing at her in the shower, you have to admit, the look people get when committing self-torture can be pretty entertaining.

The reboot of the Devil May Cry franchise has brought about numerous changes for the series, particularly for its protagonist, Dante. While longtime fans of the original series may be displeased with the alterations to their beloved character, it is undeniable that the changes are for the better. In fact, this new game by Ninja Theory showcases Dante's origins as an arrogant and cynical young man, and chronicles his transformative journey towards selflessness. Throughout, the game masterfully crafts a compelling narrative arc for the new Dante, making him an engaging character to follow. The story depicts Dante's introduction into a corrupt world he willfully chose to ignore, offering a fresh perspective on his character.

The processing of spontaneous conversations in real-world environments, as exemplified by child-centered recordings, poses a significant challenge in the field of audio analysis. However, the development of speech processing models capable of handling diverse acoustic conditions would greatly benefit language acquisition research, where the quantity and quality of speech exposure and production in children are of paramount interest. Furthermore, such models would also facilitate early diagnosis and the assessment of remediation outcomes. This paper introduces our approach to designing an open-source neural network architecture for classifying audio segments into distinct vocalization categories, including those produced by the child wearing the recording device, other children, adult males, and adult females. Our methodology involves the compilation of a diverse, multilingual dataset comprising 260 hours of child-centered recordings across 10 languages, providing a comprehensive foundation for model development and training.

More women turn to net security. Older people and women are increasingly taking charge of protecting home computers against malicious net attacks, according to a two-year study. The number of women buying programs to protect PCs from virus, spam, and spyware attacks rose by 11.2% each year between 2002 and 2004. The study, for net security firm Preventon, shows that security messages are reaching a diverse range of surfers. It is thought that 40% of those buying home net security programs are retired. For the last three years, that has gone up by an average of 13.2%. However, more retired women (53%) were buying security software than retired men. The research reflects the changing stereotype and demographics of web users, as well as growing awareness of the greater risks that high-speed broadband net connections can pose to surfers.

One simple vitamin can significantly reduce your risk of heart disease. Eating chocolate can reduce stress levels in students, and a new drug has been shown to prolong the lives of patients with rare diseases. Headlines like these are published daily, often presenting opposite claims from one another. There can be a disconnect between the attention-grabbing headlines and the specific, incremental results of the medical research they cover. To avoid being misled by sensationalized headlines, it's essential to assess a headline's credibility by examining the original research it reports on. We've developed a hypothetical research scenario for each of these three headlines. Keep in mind the explanation of the first example, and pause at the headline to answer the question. These are simplified scenarios, but understanding the context is crucial to making informed decisions about the validity of the claims being made.

This study presents a novel extension of existing human gait models, drawing inspiration from the works of Keller and Kockshenev. By integrating a metabolic energy minimization strategy with a non-linear description of the mass center's oscillatory motion, the hybrid model aims to accurately replicate the walk-run transition phenomenon. The analysis of experimental data across various speed regimes reveals that the trajectory perimeter of the mass center is a crucial parameter in quantifying this dynamic behavior. This research was conducted in collaboration with the biomedical engineering, basic sciences, and laboratory research groups at the Manuela Beltrán University in Bogotá, Colombia, utilizing a specially designed experimental protocol.

We study the phenomenology of a hypercharge-zero triplet scalar, motivated by two-step electroweak symmetry-breaking. We consider both the possibility that the triplets are stable and contribute to the dark matter density, or that they decay via mixing with the standard model Higgs boson. The former is constrained by disappearing charged track searches at the LHC and by dark matter direct detection experiments, while the latter is constrained by existing multi-lepton collider searches. We find that a two-step electroweak phase transition involving a stable triplet with a negative quadratic term is ruled out by direct detection searches, and an unstable triplet with a mass less than 230 GeV is excluded at 95% confidence level.

Let me start off by saying I did not purchase EU when it first came out and that I got it on sale, which included EW, so I never experienced the issues that some people claim to have had with that game. I loved that game; however, it was unforgiving in the early stages but rewarding when you did things right. As for XCOM2, I love it. The game plays pretty well for me on medium, and I am running a GTX560 with 2GB of VRAM. At first, the game set it to high, and it was running a bit sluggishly but not to the point of being unplayable. When I dropped it down to medium, it smoothed out. I'm not sure if I am just lucky when it comes to having issues with games or what, but it seems that most of the negative reviews sound like they are coming from some really entitled people.

Hands down, my all-time favorite first-person shooter. This game isn't for run-and-gun Rambo types. Recoil and ballistics are remarkably realistic, with single hits to the chest, neck, or head from rifle-caliber weapons almost always resulting in death, unless the shot merely grazes you. Rounds drop significantly over distance, and short, controlled bursts are necessary to stay on target. There's no infinite ammunition pool; if you constantly reload mid-magazine, you'll end up with a pouch full of partially used magazines. Endless sprinting isn't an option either, as prolonged running will leave you gasping for breath, severely impacting your aim until you've caught your breath. To steady your aim, rest your weapon on your elbows while prone or on a windowsill. Accuracy also suffers when standing, making it essential to adapt your shooting stance.

Leeds vs. Saracens (Friday) at Headingley - Friday, 25 February, 20:00 GMT. The Tykes have secured Newcastle prop Ed Kalman and Tom McGee from the Borders on loan, while fly-half Craig McMullen has joined from Narbonne. Raphael Ibanez is set to play as hooker for Saracens, marking one of four changes. Simon Raiwalui and Ben Russell have also been added to the pack, with Kevin Sorrell stepping in at outside centre.  Friday's match at Headingley was confirmed after successfully passing an early pitch inspection. 

Leeds lineup: Balshaw; Rees, Christophers, Bell, Doherty; McMullen, Dickens; McGee, Rawlinson, Gerber; Murphy, Palmer (captain), Morgan, Parks, Popham. Replacements: Kalman, Regan, Hyde, Rigney, McMillan, Rock, Vickerman.

We study the phenomenology of a hypercharge-zero SU(2) triplet scalar whose existence is motivated by two-step electroweak symmetry breaking. We consider both the possibility that the triplets are stable and contribute to the dark matter density, or that they decay via mixing with the Standard Model Higgs boson. The former is constrained by disappearing charged track searches at the LHC and by dark matter direct detection experiments, while the latter is constrained by existing multilepton collider searches. We find that a two-step electroweak phase transition involving a stable triplet with a negative quadratic term is ruled out by direct detection searches, while an unstable triplet with a mass less than $230\ \mathrm{GeV}$ is excluded at $95\%$ confidence level.

If you can overlook the bugs (I personally encountered only two—one minor and the other quite annoying—*edit: both have since been resolved, with one fix boosting my FPS from 60-90 to 80-120*) and are looking for a space exploration game, I can wholeheartedly recommend this. Since most reviews focus on specs, here are mine:  

- **CPU:** i5 6600k (~€200)  
- **GPU:** Radeon RX 480 (~€200)  
- **Motherboard:** Z170 Pro-Gaming (~€150)  
- **RAM:** 16GB DDR4 (~€70)  

Totaling around €650, this setup falls squarely in the mid-range price bracket—though admittedly, it’s fairly new. I maintain a near-constant 60+ FPS on high settings with V-Sync enabled. Frankly, I’m baffled by the 90% of comments claiming their overkill rigs struggle to hit 20 FPS.

While not perfect, this game has been a thoroughly enjoyable experience. The save system and possibility of sudden death can lead to some frustration. I'm currently on my first playthrough and I lost my main character around hour 10 of the 20-hour mark. I would have greatly appreciated the ability to reload at that point. I've since acquired a third-party mod/save tool to avoid catastrophic failures. Additionally, the aiming reticle is a bit small and the zoom could be more powerful. With all that said, it's been a blast. I'm not typically a fan of zombies, but the combination of strategy, missions, and first-person shooter/beat 'em up is a great combination. I've been playing it as often as I can get away... Just one more mission, just one more supply run - or I'm running away and want to get to a safe location before shutting the game down.

Deep hashing techniques have demonstrated exceptional performance in large-scale image retrieval systems, achieving remarkable accuracy while maintaining computational efficiency. A central challenge in these methods lies in the effective optimization of discrete binary codes. Current approaches typically employ activation functions such as sigmoid(·) or tanh(·), coupled with quantization loss minimization to approximate discrete values. Unfortunately, this conventional framework presents a critical limitation: an increasing number of hash bits become trapped in suboptimal saturation regions of the activation functions, unable to recover. We identify this phenomenon as the "dead bits problem" (DBP). Moreover, standard quantization losses inadvertently exacerbate this issue. To address this challenge, we introduce an innovative yet straightforward gradient amplification mechanism that operates prior to activation functions, effectively mitigating DBP while preserving model performance.

El Paso Natural Gas Pipeline to Test Market Interest in Mainline ExpansionEl Paso Natural Gas Pipeline to Test Market Interest in Mainline Expansion
The El Paso Natural Gas Pipeline has announced an open season to test market interest in a mainline expansion and to satisfy regulatory concerns that something needs to be done to relieve the apparent capacity constraint at the California border. El Paso has been under significant pressure from regulators and shippers to expand due to enormous demand and skyrocketing prices in California.In a January 3 letter to the pipeline, Daniel M. Adamson, Director of FERC's Office of Energy Projects, urged El Paso to consider modifying its Line 2000 project to "assist the difficult situation" now facing the California gas market."

El Paso Natural Gas Pipeline

The El Paso Natural Gas Pipeline is a major pipeline that transports natural gas from the Permian Basin in West Texas to the Southwest Power Pool (SPP) market in California. The pipeline has a capacity of 1.2 Bcf/d and has been in operation since 1999. However, due to increasing demand and limited pipeline capacity, the pipeline has been facing constraints at the California border

I'm sorry to say that I didn't find this game to be particularly enjoyable. As someone who is supportive of indie games and puzzle/exploration titles, I was intrigued by the premise of this game. However, I found the execution to be unintuitive and lacking in certain features. Specifically, I struggled to understand how to progress through the game without a clear pause button, as many of the sequences are timed. While I appreciate the attention to detail in the game's atmosphere and visuals, I found myself simply tapping my fingers impatiently rather than actively engaging with the gameplay. Additionally, I feel that the game could benefit from a more balanced approach to pacing, as there were times when the game felt more like a cutscene than an interactive experience. Despite my disappointment, I recognize that this game has its merits and I appreciate the effort that went into its creation. However, I do not feel that it is a game that I would personally enjoy playing again.

February 7, 2014 - It's unfortunate that I can't wholeheartedly recommend or enjoy this game. From the brief time I've played, I can tell I'd be hooked if it weren't for two major issues. First, the absence of Australian servers is a significant problem. Playing with a single red connection bar puts you at a severe disadvantage and results in unbearable lag. Incredibly, I even experienced stuttering and lag during the offline training mode (how does that even happen?). Combine this with poor ping, and it completely ruins the gameplay experience. Secondly, there's a severe issue with graphical artifacts affecting everything, from menus and backgrounds to training and in-game visuals. I've never encountered this problem with my current graphics card setup. I initially thought it might be due to my overclocked settings, but reverting my GPU to its default state made no difference.

Density-based clustering relies on the idea of linking groups to specific features of the probability distribution underlying the data. The reference to a true, yet unknown, population structure allows framing the clustering problem in a standard inferential setting, where the concept of ideal population clustering is defined as the partition induced by the true density function. The nonparametric formulation of this approach, known as modal clustering, draws a correspondence between the groups and the domains of attraction of the density modes. Operationally, a nonparametric density estimate is required, and a proper selection of the amount of smoothing—governing the shape of the density and hence possibly the modal structure—is crucial to identify the final partition. In this work, we address the issue of density estimation for modal clustering from an asymptotic perspective.

I've been overcome with an overwhelming sense of exhaustion for the past two days. I've been plagued by debilitating fatigue, feeling as though I'm running on empty. Last night, I returned home and collapsed onto my bed, only stirring when my mother's voice pierced the darkness around 8 30 pm. As I slowly came to, I was disoriented and disoriented, struggling to discern the time or date. Kevin Spacey's new film had its UK debut with a star-studded premiere attended by Hollywood actors Kevin Spacey and Kate Bosworth. The event took place on Thursday at Leicester Square in London. Spacey, aged 45, not only wrote and directed "Beyond the Sea" but also played the lead role, bringing to life the story of 1950s crooner Bobby Darin. "This film is my homage to an extraordinary talent," said Spacey, who performed all 18 songs on the movie's soundtrack. Bosworth, aged 21, portrayed Darin's wife, the real-life actress Sandra Dee. "Before working on this project, I knew nothing about Bobby Darin, but I've since become a fervent admirer," remarked Bosworth, who attended the premiere with her British boyfriend, Orlando Bloom.

According to a two-year study, more women are taking charge of protecting their home computers from malicious net attacks, as the number of women buying programs to shield PCs from virus, spam, and spyware attacks rose by 11.2% each year between 2002 and 2004. The study, conducted by net security firm Preventon, reveals that security messages are reaching a diverse range of surfers. It is believed that 40% of those buying home net security programs are retired, a figure that has increased by an average of 13.2% over the last three years. Interestingly, more retired women (53%) are buying security software than retired men. The research reflects the evolving demographics of web users and growing awareness of the increased risks associated with high-speed broadband net connections for surfers.

As I stand before you, I wonder what thoughts cross your mind. Do you see a woman of unwavering faith, a knowledgeable expert, or perhaps a kindred spirit? Or do you perceive me as oppressed, brainwashed, or even a threat to society? Maybe, in a more mundane sense, you associate me with the frustration of a delayed airport security line – a notion that, I must confess, holds a grain of truth. If some of your initial impressions were less than favorable, I don't hold it against you. The media has, after all, played a significant role in shaping these perceptions, with a staggering 80 percent of news coverage about Islam and Muslims being decidedly negative. It's no surprise, then, that many Americans admit to not knowing a Muslim personally. I often joke that perhaps they just don't engage in conversation with their Uber drivers. To those of you who have never had the opportunity to meet a Muslim, I'm delighted to make your acquaintance.

In a groundbreaking move, the US singles chart has been revamped to include digital music downloads for the first time, revolutionizing the way music popularity is measured. Billboard's iconic Hot 100 chart now seamlessly integrates data from digital music sales, previously relegated to a separate download chart, to provide a more comprehensive picture of an artist's success. This shift is exemplified by Green Day's "Boulevard of Broken Dreams," which currently holds the number two spot on Billboard's pop chart and reigns supreme on the digital chart. A similar transformation is set to take place in the UK singles chart later this year. In the US, digital sales have long been a key component of Billboard's Hot Digital Sales chart, but they will now be combined with physical single sales and airplay data to create a more nuanced Hot 100 chart. Additionally, Billboard has introduced the Pop 100 chart, which aggregates airplay, digital, and physical sales, with a focus on US radio stations that specialize in chart-topping music, offering a more targeted snapshot of the music landscape.

Metal ion implantation into ceramics has been demonstrated to be an effective and controllable technique for tailoring the surface electrical conductivity of a ceramic piece, and this approach has been used in a number of applications. Importantly, it provides a method for grading the voltage drop across high-voltage insulators, and thereby increasing the maximum operational voltage that can be applied across the insulator without surface flashover. However, a concern for the use of the method is the long-term stability of the implantation-induced conductivity, especially if the implanted metal species is readily oxidized. Here, we report on our examination of the long-term behavior of the surface conductivity of titanium-implanted alumina. The results indicate that after an initial drop of as much as 40% in the first few weeks after implantation, the conductivity shows only a very slow decrease of about 10% over the following year.

Deep hashing methods have demonstrated exceptional retrieval accuracy and efficiency in large-scale image retrieval. The primary focus in these methods is on optimizing discrete hash bits. A common strategy in these methods is to adopt an activation function, for instance, the sigmoid or tanh, and minimize a quantization loss to approximate discrete values. However, this paradigm may lead to more and more hash bits being stuck in the wrong region of the activation functions, failing to escape. We refer to this issue as the 'dead bits problem'. Moreover, the existing quantization loss will exacerbate dbp. In this paper, we propose a simple yet effective gradient amplifier that acts before activation functions to mitigate dbp.

The world's largest and most impactful environmental and industrial undertaking is located in the heart of Canada's boreal forest, the most extensive and pristine forest on the planet. This vast forest extends across northern Canada, including Labrador, where the George River Caribou Herd resides—the largest remaining wild caribou herd in the world, with approximately 400,000 animals. Unfortunately, during my visit, I was unable to spot any caribou, though the antlers bear testament to their presence. Throughout the boreal region, we are fortunate to have an incredible abundance of wetlands. Globally, wetlands are among the most threatened ecosystems, yet they are vital for cleaning air and water, sequestering significant amounts of greenhouse gases, and providing a home to a wide array of species.

Last night, I finished this game. The story is not particularly deep or captivating, but the storytelling is what makes it stand out. The atmosphere is rich and immersive, and the many collectibles do an excellent job of fleshing it out. I learned a lot about the world of Batman, so it was a bit disappointing that there were so few boss battles. However, there are enough future games to rectify that. The combat is the right amount of challenge, leaning towards the easier side. Leveling up Batman wasn't very satisfying. It's the gadgets you acquire and upgrade during the campaign that add nice new gameplay mechanics. Having access to previously unreachable places makes for a sense of progression.

You’ve been accused of a crime you did not commit. It’s impossible to prove your innocence. If you insist that you’re innocent anyway, you’ll likely be found guilty and executed. But if you confess, apologize, and implicate others for good measure, you’ll go free. Do you give a false confession—or risk a public hanging? This was the choice facing those accused of witchcraft in the village of Salem, Massachusetts, between February 1692 and May 1693. They were the victims of paranoia about the supernatural, misdirected religious fervor—and a justice system that valued repentance over truth. Salem was settled in 1626 by Puritans, a group of English Protestants. Life was strict and isolated for the people of Salem. Battles with their Native American neighbors and groups of French settlers were commonplace.

I encountered no performance issues even at launch, and with all the subsequent patches, the game now runs exceptionally well. Once you get the hang of it, cruising through Gotham is an absolute delight!

In the context of Calabi-Yau orbifolds, we investigate the relationship between two distinct methods for constructing their mirror partners. Specifically, we consider an orbifold defined as the quotient of a hypersurface, represented by a quasi-homogeneous polynomial, in a weighted projective space, under the action of a suitable subgroup of phase symmetries. Two approaches have been proposed to construct the mirror partner of this orbifold. The first, known as the Berglund-Hübsch-Krawitz (BHK) construction, involves a new weighted projective space and a hypersurface within it, quotiented by a dual group. In contrast, the Batyrev construction defines the mirror partner as a hypersurface in a toric variety, which is itself determined by a reflexive polytope dual to the polytope associated with the original orbifold. We provide a straightforward demonstration of the equivalence between these two seemingly disparate constructions.

The spreadsheet below illustrates the implicit commodity charge based on the effective date of four different rates for the same rate schedule (Schedule DR). Notably, the rate schedule in effect from August 2000 through September 2000 results in a significantly higher commodity charge, as SDG&E customers will receive a lump sum reimbursement from SDG&E during these two months, leading to lower expected electric bills. Excluding the lump sum reimbursement from the implicit energy component, the average implicit commodity charge from June 2000 through January 2001 is 6.2 cents, while the average from February 2001 through December 2001 is 7.3 cents. Overall, the average implicit commodity charge from June 2000 through December 2001 remains at 6.9 cents, which would still be the case for deliveries starting on September 1, 2000.

When a new user signs up on a website, we typically have limited information about them, i.e., no interaction with items, no user profile, and no social links with other users. In such circumstances, it is crucial for our recommender systems to effectively attract users at the first point of contact, so that the users decide to stay on the website and become active users. This problem falls under the category of new user cold-start, which is a crucial aspect of the development and survival of a company. Existing approaches on user cold-start recommendation often require additional user efforts, such as setting up an interview process or leveraging side information, such as user demographics, locations, social relations, and so on. However, users may not be willing to provide the required information, and side information on cold-start users is often not available, making it a challenging problem to solve.

Attention, Magic: The Gathering enthusiasts—prepare for disappointment. Wizards of the Coast has failed to deliver a truly engaging digital experience for its beloved card game. At its heart, Magic thrives on social interaction—the camaraderie of friends (or rivals), the banter, the shared excitement of game-changing plays. Yet Wizards appears to have lost sight of this essence.  

The current system actively discourages playing with friends by denying gold rewards, the game's essential currency. While the daily gold cap makes sense—given that gold unlocks nearly all in-game content—it forces players into a tedious, isolating grind. You're left with two bleak options: mindless 1v1 matches against AI or silent, impersonal battles with strangers.  

For free-to-play users, the grind is especially punishing. Expect to invest roughly four hours daily for over a month—solo—just to build a viable collection. Is this really how Magic should be experienced? The answer is a resounding no.

These are grim economic times, fellow Tedsters, grim economic times indeed. And so, I would like to lift your spirits with one of the great, albeit largely unknown, commercial success stories of the past 20 years. Comparable, in its own very peculiar way, to the achievements of Microsoft or Google. And it's an industry that has bucked the current recession with equanimity. I refer to organized crime.

Now, organized crime has been around for a very long time, I hear you say, and these would be wise words, indeed. But in the last two decades, it has experienced an unprecedented expansion, now accounting for roughly 15 percent of the world's GDP. I like to call it the global shadow economy, or McMafia, for short.

So, what triggered this extraordinary growth in cross-border crime?

Urllink invites everyone to welcome the latest Atom/RDF/RSS reader on the scene: Urllink PulpFiction 1.0, developed by Urllink Freshly Squeezed Software. This paper shows that the eccentric debris rings seen around stars Fomalhaut and HD 202628 are narrower than expected in the standard eccentric planet perturbation scenario, sometimes referred to as "pericenter glow." The standard scenario posits an initially circular and narrow belt of planetesimals at semi-major axis $a$, whose eccentricity is increased to $e_f$ after the gas disc has dispersed by secular perturbations from an eccentric planet, resulting in a belt of width $2ae_f$. In a minor modification of this scenario, narrower belts can arise if the planetesimals are initially eccentric, which could result from earlier planet perturbations during the gas-rich protoplanetary disc phase. Alternatively, a primordial eccentricity could be caused by instabilities that increase the disc eccentricity without the need for any planets.

*(Inhales deeply, exhales slowly)* So, I haven’t always made my living through music. For roughly five years after graduating from a respected liberal arts university, this was my daily routine. *(Chuckles)* I worked as a self-employed living statue—the "Eight-Foot Bride"—and I adore sharing this part of my past because, inevitably, people always wonder: *Who are these peculiar souls in real life?* *(Laughs softly)*  

Here’s how it went: I’d paint myself white, step onto a pedestal, place a hat or a can at my feet, and whenever someone dropped in a coin, I’d offer them a flower—along with a piercing gaze. And if they declined the flower? I’d linger with a wistful, longing gesture as they walked away.

Here is the polished text:
Two twin domes, two radically opposed design cultures. One is made of thousands of steel parts, the other of a single silk thread. One is synthetic, the other organic. One is imposed on the environment, the other creates it. One is designed for nature, the other is designed by her. Michelangelo said that when he looked at raw marble, he saw a figure struggling to be free. The chisel was Michelangelo's only tool. But living things are not chiseled. They grow. And in our smallest units of life, our cells, we carry all the information that's required for every other cell to function and to replicate. Tools also have consequences. At least since the industrial revolution, the world of design has been dominated by the rigors of manufacturing and mass production.

In a surprising turn of events, Jurgen Melzer defeated second-seed Andre Agassi in the quarter-finals of the SAP Open. The Austrian's effective use of drop shots left Agassi struggling, resulting in a 6-3, 6-1 loss for the American in San Jose. Meanwhile, defending champion and top seed Andy Roddick made a comeback to overcome Sweden's Thomas Enqvist with scores of 3-6, 7-6 (8-6), 7-5. Additionally, unseeded Cyril Saulnier triumphed over fourth-seed Vincent Spadea, winning 6-2, 6-4, while Tommy Haas edged out eighth-seed Max Mirnyi with scores of 6-7 (2-7), 7-6 (7-3), 6-2. This marks the second victory for Melzer over Agassi in their three encounters. "I had a solid game plan and executed it flawlessly," Melzer commented. "Facing Andre is always challenging. I aimed to disrupt his style because he can make you chase the ball all over the court like a dog.

Downloads enter US singles chart. Digital music downloads are being included in the main US singles chart for the first time. Billboard's Hot 100 chart now incorporates data from sales of music downloads, previously only assigned to a separate download chart. Green Day's 'Boulevard of Broken Dreams' is currently number two in Billboard's pop chart, and tops its digital chart. Download sales are due to be incorporated into the UK singles chart later this year. Digital sales in the US are already used to compile Billboard's Hot Digital Sales chart. They will now be tallied with sales of physical singles and airplay information to make up its new Hot 100 chart. Its second new chart - the Pop 100 - also combines airplay, digital and physical sales, but confines its airplay information to US radio stations that play chart music.

One of the most pressing unsolved challenges in condensed matter physics is understanding how two-dimensional systems can exhibit unconventional superconductivity at high critical temperatures. Ultracold Fermi gases have emerged as ideal model systems to investigate the intricate interplay between strong correlations and reduced dimensionality, yet direct evidence of superfluidity in these systems has been lacking. Here, we provide conclusive evidence of superfluidity in an ultracold Fermi gas by inducing a periodic potential and observing the absence of dissipation below a critical velocity, v_c. Our measurement of v_c as a function of interaction strength reveals a maximum in the crossover regime between bosonic and fermionic superfluidity. This achievement establishes ultracold Fermi gases as a powerful platform for exploring the impact of reduced dimensionality on strongly correlated superfluids and sheds new light on the fundamental physics governing these systems.

Voters are flocking to the blog awards website, where the annual Bloggies ceremony is underway, recognizing the best online weblogs that showcase the year's most thought-provoking and engaging content. The nominations were announced on Sunday, but the heavy traffic to the official site caused the website to temporarily shut down due to the overwhelming number of visitors. This year, weblogs have been nominated in 30 categories, ranging from the top regional blog to the best-kept-secret blog. The blogosphere has had a remarkable year, with the term "blog" being named as the Word of the Year in 2004 by a leading dictionary. According to Technorati, a prominent blog search engine, there are approximately six million blogs online, with over 12,000 new ones being added daily. Interestingly, a new blog is created every 5.8 seconds, according to research by the Pew Internet and American Life project. However, only less than 40% of these blogs are updated at least once every two months, highlighting the dynamic nature of this ever-evolving online landscape.

Remember the age of mythology? Indeed, it has been a long time since there has been an RTS game of such exceptional quality. Divinity  Dragon Commander comes close to my personal favorite RTS game. Hearts 2-1 Livingston Hearts wrapped up their Scottish Cup quarter-final tie against Livingston with two goals in the first 10 minutes. Lee Miller scored inside the opening 60 seconds, heading over Colin Meldrum and into the net from Jamie McAllister's free kick. McAllister himself scored the second when he met Saul Mikoliunas' cross at the back post to put the game beyond the visitors. Craig Easton headed in a controversial goal after an hour but Hearts held on. The opening goal came after Gus Bahoken had clumsily fouled Miller 35 yards from goal. McAllister floated the free kick into the Livi box and Miller outjumped the static visiting defense to loop his header from 10 yards over Meldrum. With just 10 minutes on the clock, the home side doubled their lead. Mikoliunas sprinted down the right, crossed to the back post where McAllister raced in to head past Meldrum.

The continuous-time quantum walk framework has emerged as a powerful paradigm for designing a wide range of quantum algorithms, with the quantum hitting time serving as a key metric for evaluating their efficiency. This metric represents the time required for a quantum walk to locate a target vertex with high probability. This article presents significant advancements in the field by establishing improved upper bounds for the quantum hitting time, thereby enhancing the performance of various quantum algorithms based on continuous-time quantum walks. Notably, our techniques yield a substantial improvement in the hitting time upper bound for the glued-trees problem, reducing it from a quintic to a nearly quadratic dependence on the problem size, specifically from O(n^5) to O(n^2 log n). Moreover, our methods also enable an exponential reduction in the precision dependence of the continuous-time quantum walk-based algorithm for finding a marked node on any ergodic, reversible Markov chain, as originally proposed by Chakraborty et al.

Carbon is a vital element that is present in all living things, including soil, oceans, and rocks. When plants and other living things die, their stored carbon is released back into the atmosphere, but some of it becomes fossil fuels like coal, oil, and natural gas. Natural events like volcanic eruptions release some of the carbon trapped in rocks, while human activities, such as burning fossil fuels, release much more than all the volcanoes on Earth combined. Today, humans release around 60 times more carbon than all the volcanoes on the planet each year.

Optomechanics and electromechanics have made it possible to prepare macroscopic mechanical oscillators in their quantum ground states, quadrature squeezed states, and entangled states of motion. In addition to coaxing larger and more tangible objects into a regime of quantum behavior, this new capability has encouraged the use of mechanical oscillators in processing and communicating quantum information and as precision force sensors operating beyond the standard quantum limit. However, the effectively linear interaction between motion and light or electricity precludes access to the broader class of quantum states of motion, such as cat states or energy squeezed states. Early optomechanical proposals noted the possibility of overcoming this restriction by creating strong quadratic coupling of motion to light. Although there have been experimental demonstrations of quadratically coupled optomechanical systems, these have not yet accessed nonclassical states of motion.

The Cabinet is expressing anger at the proposal to use Whitehall's cash to keep council tax bills down, with local government minister Nick Raynsford acknowledging the plans. In his pre-budget report on Thursday, Gordon Brown reallocated £512m from central to local government budgets. Mr. Raynsford stated that he had engaged in "pretty frank discussions" with fellow ministers over the plans. The central government's cash is part of a £1bn package to assist local authorities in England in keeping next year's council tax rises below 5%, likely in the context of a general election. We introduce a novel Skyrme-like conserved current in the effective theory of pions and vector mesons, founded on the notion of hidden local symmetry. The associated charge is equivalent to the Skyrmion charge for any smooth configuration. Additionally, there are singular configurations that can be identified as \(n_f=1\) baryons that are charged under the new symmetry. With this identification, the vector mesons act as the Chern-Simons vector fields residing on the quantum Hall droplet that forms the \(n_f=1\) baryon. We propose that this current is the correct effective expression for the baryon current at low energies. This proposal provides a unified perspective for the two types of baryons and allows them to naturally transform into one another. Furthermore, Chern-Simons dualities on the droplet can be interpreted as a result of Seiberg-like duality between gluons and vector mesons.

**The Silent Burden of Menstruation**  

A single word—menstruation—encapsulates a universal biological process, yet it remains shrouded in centuries of stigma, reduced to a hushed conversation. Why? At this very moment, over 800 million women worldwide are menstruating. This cycle is the very reason humanity exists, yet society treats it as a source of shame.  

The exhaustion is real. It’s in the furtive glances as you slip a pad from its brown paper wrapper, tuck it into your palm, and hurry to the restroom, praying no one notices. It’s in the forced composure during lectures or meetings, masking the searing pain of cramps with a practiced smile. This is the silent burden we carry—a natural process made unnatural by silence.

Yukos, heading back to US courts, Russian oil and gas company Yukos is due in a US court on Thursday as it continues to fight for its survival. The firm is in the process of being broken up by Russian authorities in order to pay a $27 billion (£14 billion) tax bill. Yukos has filed for bankruptcy in the US, hoping to use international business law to halt the forced sale of its key oil production unit, Yugasnkneftegas. The unit was, however, sold for $9.4 billion to state oil firm Rosneft, but only after the state auction had been disrupted. Yukos lawyers now say the auction violated US bankruptcy law. The company and its main shareholders have vowed to go after any company that buys its assets, using all and every legal means.

Routing controllability of autonomous vehicles (AVs) has been shown to reduce the impact of selfish routing on network efficiency. However, the assumption that AVs would readily allow themselves to be controlled externally by a central agency is unrealistic. In this paper, we propose a joint routing and pricing control scheme that aims to incentivize AVs to seek centrally controlled system optimal (SO) routing by saving on tolls, while user equilibrium (UE) seeking AVs and human-driven vehicles (HVs) are subject to a congestion charge. The problem is formulated as a bi-level optimization, in which dynamic tolls are optimized in the upper level, whereas the lower level is a mixed equilibrium simulation-based dynamic traffic assignment model considering a mixed fleet of AVs and HVs.

Sure, I'd be happy to explain the Yap and their unique system of money!

The Yap is a small island nation located in the Pacific Ocean, known for its unique culture and traditions. One of these traditions is their form of money, which is based on large limestone discs called "rai stones."

The Yapese people have been using rai stones as their form of currency for centuries, and the tradition is still alive and well today. The stones are made from limestone and are cut into different shapes and sizes, depending on their intended use. The largest rai stones are called "mwang," and they can weigh up to four tons and measure up to 12 feet in diameter.
The Yapese people have a complex system for tracking who owns which part of each stone. The stones are divided into different sections, and each section is assigned to a specific family or clan. When a stone is created, it is divided into these sections, and the ownership is passed down from generation to generation.
The Yapese people have a deep respect for these rai stones, and they are an integral part of their culture and identity. They are used in many ceremonies and rituals, and they are also used as a symbol of wealth and status.

In our recent class discussion, we delved into the assigned reading, 'Toward a Renewed Study of the Place-World' by Edward S. Casey. The first chapter, 'Implacement,' and the second, 'Displacement,' offered a fascinating exploration of the concepts that shape our understanding of the world. Casey posits that our contemporary culture and society are dominated by the force of time, which he argues is a situating force that drives our daily lives. We are constantly racing against time, trying to make the most of the hours we have, and yet, paradoxically, we are often stuck in the present moment. Casey's central argument is that by placing place at the forefront of our cultural considerations, we can cultivate a more grounded sense of community and belonging. This book has had a profound impact on me, as it is the first reading to have truly resonated with me and sparked a deeper appreciation for the importance of place in shaping our experiences.

Jamieson issues warning to bigots. Scotland's Justice Minister has warned bigoted soccer fans that she wants to hit them "where it hurts most" by banning them from matches. Cathy Jamieson said exclusion orders are one of a series of measures being considered in the Scottish Executive's campaign against sectarianism. She praised Celtic and Rangers for their work in tackling the problem. However, the minister said stopping sectarian abuse associated with Old Firm matches is a key objective. Ms. Jamieson was speaking ahead of the third-round Scottish Cup clash between the Glasgow clubs at Parkhead on Sunday. The sectarianism long associated with sections of the support from both clubs has become a significant target for the Executive. Last week, Ms. Jamieson and First Minister Jack McConnell met supporters' representatives from both clubs to discuss the issue.

Ordinary differential equations (ODEs) are mathematical models used in many application areas such as climatology, bioinformatics, and chemical engineering due to their intuitive appeal for modeling. Despite ODEs' wide usage in modeling, the frequent absence of analytic solutions makes it difficult to estimate ODE parameters from data, especially when the model has many variables and parameters. This paper proposes a Bayesian ODE parameter estimation algorithm that is fast and accurate even for models with many parameters. The proposed method approximates an ODE model with a state-space model based on the equations of a numerical solver. It enables fast estimation by avoiding computations of a full numerical solution in the likelihood. The posterior is obtained by a variational Bayes method, more specifically, the approximate Riemannian conjugate gradient method (Honkela et al.).

I loved you immensely, and to this day the feeling carries an echo through my heart. Or a kiss under the rain. Whenever I get the feeling of a dark glass puncturing my heart, you will be there. That piece of you... Bleeding the eternal love that once lived in me... And still remains... For her.

It's a shame I can't thoroughly recommend or enjoy this game, as from the short time I've played it, I know I'd be addicted if it didn't have the following two game-breakers. Unfortunately, the game is marred by two major issues  the lack of Aussie servers and poor performance. The lack of servers is particularly egregious, as one red bar puts you at a severe disadvantage, causing unbearable lag. I've played through the offline training mode, and it was equally plagued by stuttering and lag (it's astonishing that this could happen). Combine this with the poor ping, and it utterly ruins the game. Furthermore, the game's artifacts, such as the menus, backgrounds, and in-game elements, are laughable. I've never experienced this level of poor performance with this graphics card and thought it might be an issue with my overclocked processor. However, I set my GPU back to stock settings, and, sure enough, it didn't make a difference.

Wales 'must learn health lessons'. The new health minister for Wales says there are lessons to learn from England in tackling waiting lists. Dr Brian Gibbons, on his first full day in the job after Jane Hutt was sacked, admitted "big challenges" but insisted the "essentials" were in place. However, both Plaid Cymru and the Liberal Democrats said Dr Gibbons needed to change policy. Meanwhile, Ms Hutt defended her record, saying waiting times and lists were "only 10% of the health agenda". Dr Gibbons, a former GP in Blaengwynfi, in the Upper Afan Valley, before becoming AM for Aberavon, said NHS staff wanted a period of consolidation after "tremendous change and reform". One of the biggest problems that had faced Ms Hutt during her five-and-a-half years as the assembly's first health minister was the length of waiting lists in Wales.

I am a cultural omnivore, whose daily commute is made possible by an iPod filled with a diverse range of music - from Wagner and Mozart to pop diva Christina Aguilera, country singer Josh Turner, and gangsta rap artist Kirk Franklin, as well as concerti and symphonies. I'm a voracious reader, devouring works by authors ranging from Ian McEwan to Stephanie Meyer, including the Twilight tetralogy. I also live for my home theater, where I indulge in DVDs, video on demand, and a lot of television, with favorites like "Law & Order: SVU," Tina Fey's "30 Rock," and "Judge Judy," where "the people are real, the cases are real, the rulings are final.

Where does all the carbon dioxide we release go? Carbon plays a natural cycle, present in all living things  in the soil, in the oceans, in the atmosphere, and in rocks deep underground. When plants and other living organisms die, much of their stored carbon is released back to the atmosphere, where it's reabsorbed by new plants and organisms. However, over millions of years, some of the carbon stored in ancient trees and sea life becomes rock, and some becomes fossil fuels  coal, oil, and natural gas. Natural events like volcanic eruptions release some of the carbon trapped in rock. Human activities, such as burning fossil fuels, also release some of that prehistoric carbon. Today, humans release approximately 60 times more carbon dioxide than all the volcanoes on the planet each year.
