Spontaneous conversations in real-world scenarios, particularly those captured in child-centered recordings, have proven to be some of the most difficult audio files to process. Despite these challenges, developing speech processing models that can handle a broad range of conditions is crucial for language acquisition research. Such models are essential for researchers interested in analyzing the quantity and quality of speech that children hear and produce, as well as for early diagnosis and assessing the effects of remediation. In this paper, we introduce our method for creating an open-source neural network designed to classify audio segments. These segments include vocalizations made by the child wearing the recording device, other children's vocalizations, adult male speech, and adult female speech. To achieve this, we compiled diverse child-centered corpora totaling 260 hours of recordings across 10 different languages.

A warning has been issued to internet users regarding a fraudulent website purporting to collect donations on behalf of tsunami victims. The site's authenticity is compromised by its use of an outdated version of the official Disasters Emergency Committee webpage, despite having no affiliation with the legitimate organization. Authorities have been notified, and an investigation is underway. This incident is the latest in a series of scams attempting to exploit the public's generosity in response to the tsunami disaster. The fake website is being promoted through a spam email campaign, identifiable by its subject line "Urgent Tsunami Earthquake Appeal" and characteristic poor grammar and spelling indicative of phishing attempts.

In 1903, the President of the United States embarked on a three-day camping trip in California's Yosemite Valley. President Theodore Roosevelt slept among a grove of towering sequoia trees, braved a snowstorm, and spent countless hours engaging in lively conversation around the campfire with his host and guide, conservationist John Muir. Roosevelt, an ardent lover of the great outdoors, was also drawn to Yosemite for a more pressing reason: the valley was in peril. Although Yosemite had been protected land since 1864, the valley was still at risk of overdevelopment in 1903, the result of a decades-old struggle to strike a balance between preservation and public use. The battle for Yosemite's future began in the midst of the 1849 gold rush, when throngs of miners flocked west in search of gold in the Sierra Nevada foothills.

Yesterday, my colleagues from the bank and I went on an outing to Guintubdan, La Carlota. For most of us, it was a new experience. We embarked on a trek to the falls, which took about 30 minutes. The uphill walk left us breathless, but we persevered and reached our destination. The water was icy cold, enough to make our bodies feel numb. We didn't linger for long, less than an hour, before setting off to explore another nearby waterfall, involving another 30-minute hike. Although challenging, the journey was truly rewarding. The falls exuded a sense of tranquility, and the entire scene was breathtaking. It felt as though we were in harmony with nature. Immersing myself in the chilly water provided a profound connection to nature, and the cool air and water were soothing enough to lull anyone into a tranquil state.

Extreme mass ratio inspirals (EMRIs) can be broadly categorized into two distinct types based on their formation mechanisms: dry EMRIs and wet EMRIs. Traditionally, dry EMRIs, also known as "loss-cone" EMRIs, were considered the primary source of EMRIs detectable by the Laser Interferometer Space Antenna. These dry EMRIs are primarily formed through the complex interplay of multi-body scattering within the nuclear star cluster and gravitational capture. However, this letter sheds light on an alternative, yet significant, EMRI formation pathway: the assisted formation of wet EMRIs facilitated by the accretion flow surrounding accreting massive black holes (MBHs) at galactic centers. In this scenario, the accretion disk captures stellar-mass black holes that are initially on inclined orbits, and subsequently, through a process of orbital migration, drives them towards the MBH, thereby dramatically enhancing the formation rate of EMRIs in these galaxies by several orders of magnitude.

This strategy game is truly exceptional! Having played every other war game, including European Escalation and ALB, I can confidently say this one fits in seamlessly. The sheer number of units available is impressive, and while I can't pinpoint the exact count, there are a vast amount to choose from! The graphics are stunning, the audio is immersive, and overall, the gameplay is nothing short of epic. Even the in-game chat box at the main menu is surprisingly entertaining. However, for those who expect an 'easy' experience or are new to the strategy genre, be prepared for a learning curve. Don't be discouraged by losses; you will lose many units. Just ensure you plan strategically, and always include anti-air and anti-tank defenses.

At a glance: Tory health checks  
The UK's opposition Conservative Party has announced plans to implement mandatory health screenings for immigrants if they win the upcoming general election. Here’s an overview of the proposal:  

- Individuals relocating to Britain from outside the EU for work or residence would be subject to health checks.  
- Those intending to stay for six months or longer, originating from countries with high tuberculosis (TB) rates, would require a chest X-ray and additional tests if necessary.  
- All non-EU nationals seeking to remain in the UK for a year or more would undergo a comprehensive medical examination, covering conditions such as tuberculosis, hepatitis B, and HIV.  
- A positive TB test would result in an automatic visa rejection.  
- Other medical conditions would be assessed individually on a case-by-case basis.

In 2004, Prince was named the top-earning music artist, surpassing icons like Madonna and Elton John according to Rolling Stone's annual list. He amassed $56.5 million (£30.4 million) from his Musicology tour and album, outperforming Madonna, who came in second with $54.9 million (£29.5 million) from her global Re-Invention Tour. Meanwhile, Simon and Garfunkel ranked tenth, earning $24.9 million (£13.4 million) with their comeback tour. Rolling Stone highlighted Prince's triumphant return to the spotlight after a decade away from the commercial mainstream. His 2004 tour alone generated $90.3 million (£48.5 million) in ticket sales and he sold 1.9 million copies of his album "Musicology.

Research indicates that high-speed internet access is significantly boosting online self-expression. According to a report by the UK think tank Demos, a substantial segment of broadband users in Britain, approximately one-quarter, routinely upload content and manage personal websites. The report highlights how an always-on, fast internet connection is transforming internet usage patterns. Currently, over five million UK households have broadband, and this number continues to rise swiftly. The Demos report analyzed the influence of broadband on internet habits, revealing that more than half of the broadband users access the web before breakfast. Remarkably, one in five confessed to browsing the internet in the middle of the night. Most notably, the report asserts that broadband is propelling individuals toward more active online engagement.

A woman from the United States has filed a lawsuit against Hewlett-Packard (HP), alleging that its printer ink cartridges are designed to expire on a specific date. This woman, hailing from Georgia and choosing not to reveal her identity, claims that a chip inside these cartridges signals the printer to require a refill even when it's not necessary. The lawsuit aims to include all individuals in the United States who have bought an HP inkjet printer since February 2001. HP, known as the largest printer manufacturer globally, has not provided any comments on this legal action. It is known that HP ink cartridges use chip technology to detect low ink levels and alert users to replace them. However, the lawsuit contends that these chips also deactivate the cartridges after a set period, regardless of whether or not they are actually empty.

While I personally can't recommend this game, it wasn't completely awful. The gunplay is decent, provided you have the funds to buy gun parts—which brings me to my primary criticism: it's pay-to-win. If I could overlook that aspect, the game would actually be enjoyable, as the hardsuit is a blast to use and HRV is a brilliant concept. I'd choose this over any Call of Duty title without hesitation, but its pay-to-win microtransaction system is a dealbreaker for me. If you're fine with this model, then by all means, enjoy it. But if you share my distaste for pay-to-win mechanics, it's best to pass—this one just isn't for us.

This past week has been incredibly exhausting. From Tuesday to Thursday, I was busy completing the transition to my new classroom. The new space is larger and closer to my grade-level team, which are important factors as I invest countless hours setting it up for the year ahead. Had I known about the move before the school year ended, I would have parted with many unused books and supplies. Unfortunately, I ended up bringing everything along. So, this week was filled with moving, unpacking, and a thorough decluttering process. I'm very grateful to my teammate Jill for her assistance. She continued teaching in my old room this month, and with the help of her wonderful students, she managed to move almost everything for me!

The widespread adoption of broadband internet is revolutionizing the way people express themselves online, with a significant proportion of users in the UK leveraging fast web access to share their thoughts, ideas, and creativity with the world. According to a comprehensive report by the UK-based think-tank Demos, nearly a quarter of broadband subscribers in Britain regularly upload content and maintain personal websites, underscoring the profound impact of high-speed internet on online behavior. The always-on, fast connection afforded by broadband is transforming the internet experience, enabling users to engage with online content in new and innovative ways. With over five million households in the UK already connected to broadband and this number growing rapidly, the report's findings offer a fascinating glimpse into the evolving online habits of the British public. Notably, the study reveals that more than half of broadband users begin their day by logging onto the web, while a significant minority even confess to rising in the middle of the night to browse the internet. Perhaps most importantly, the report argues that broadband is empowering individuals to take a more active and participatory role in the online community, fostering a culture of creativity, self-expression, and digital engagement.

In the real and complex global Thom spectra $\mathbfmo$ and $\mathbfmu$, the splittings imply that certain long exact homotopy groups separate into short exact sequences. Specifically, for any $n \in \mathbb{N$, there exist exact homotopy groups $E_n$ and F_n$ such that $E_n \to F_n \to \mathbfmo$ and $F_n \to \mathbfmu$ are surjective homotopy groups.

Moreover, there exist long exact sequences

$$\xymatrix{E_n \ar[r] & \ar[d] & F_n \ar[r] & \ar[d] & \mathbfmo \ar[r] & \mathbfmu}$$

$$\xymatrix{E_n \ar[r] & \ar[d] & F_n \ar[r] & \ar[d] & \mathbfmo \ar[r] & \mathbfmu}$$

where the vertical arrows are surjective homotopy groups, and the horizontal arrows are injective homotopy groups.

Note that the splittings are not necessarily split, i.e., there may be non-trivial extensions of $E_n$ and $F

Fallout 3 had a better storyline. Settlements often get in the way, making you feel like you're babysitting instead of progressing through the story. Some keys are hard-coded and cannot be rebound. The game is plagued by bugs. At one point, my Pip-Boy disappeared permanently, and I had to research console commands to retrieve it. Power armor is easily accessible from the start, and power cores are readily available in trader shops, greatly diminishing its value compared to previous Fallout games. The story is mediocre, and everything about this game is average, except for its stability - it doesn't crash every 10 minutes like Fallout 3. However, it's not better than Fallout 3 or New Vegas. The hype surrounding this title is unwarranted, given its overall quality.

The tails of diboson production at the LHC are sensitive to the interference between standard model and higher dimension operators, parameterizing the effects of heavy new physics. However, helicity selection rules for diboson scattering amplitudes impose an obstruction to naïve interference contributions of dimension six operators, resulting in the total diboson rate correction's leading contribution cancelling. In this case, measuring the azimuthal decay angles "resurrects" the interference, recouping sensitivity to the "non-interfering" operators. We explore these signatures in detail, and find that the EFT uncertainties associated with higher-dimensional operators are uniquely well-suppressed by the construction of an asymmetry variable generated only by these non-interfering operators, relegating the effects of higher-dimensional, interfering operators to the same status as statistical errors in this observable.

Ever have one of those days when you just don't know what the... What the... Anything? No? Well, then you should skip the rest of this because it won't make any sense to you. To those of you choosing to continue on, I offer the following observation: "Sometimes I wish I'd been given the manual." Manuals, if done correctly at least, let you know what things are for, what they can do, how they do the things they do, what happens if you push button X or Y, what to do if things aren't doing what they're supposed to, and provide the phone number and email address of the omniscient guru who can solve every problem—very useful information. Unfortunately, I didn't get a manual with me.

A new generation of two-dimensional materials, including res$_2$ and rese$_2$ monolayers, black phosphorus monolayers, and group-iv monochalcogenide monolayers, as well as borophene, is redefining the landscape of low-dimensional physics. Unlike graphene, hexagonal boron nitride, and transition metal dichalcogenides, which exhibit the characteristic honeycomb lattice structure, these emerging materials possess intricate atomistic arrangements that deviate from high symmetry. This reduced symmetry gives rise to a rich tapestry of inhomogeneous responses, encompassing electron, optical, valley, and spin phenomena, as well as novel properties such as ferroelasticity, ferroelectricity, magnetism, and superconductivity. Furthermore, the unique structural characteristics of these materials enable the emergence of nonlinear optical properties, spin-wave phenomena, photogalvanic effects, and large nonlinear elastic responses. The interplay between low symmetry and anisotropy also facilitates the manifestation of novel electronic topological properties, nonlinear elastic behavior, and structural phase transformations. In recognition of the burgeoning interest in these materials, the special topic "Beyond Graphene: Low-Symmetry and Anisotropic 2D Materials" has been curated to showcase the latest experimental and theoretical advances in this rapidly evolving field.

Cardinal Cormac Murphy-O'Connor has criticized the Iraq War, stating that the billions of pounds spent on the conflict in Iraq and the Middle East should have been used to reduce poverty. The head of the Catholic Church in England and Wales made the comments on BBC Radio 4 and will reiterate his stance in his Christmas Midnight Mass. In his Christmas message, the cardinal denounced the war in Iraq as a "terrible" waste of money. He and the Archbishop of Canterbury have both spoken out against the war. Speaking on BBC Radio 4's Thought for the Day slot, the cardinal criticized the fact that "billions" have been spent on war, instead of being used to bring people "out of dire poverty, malnutrition, and disease." The cardinal hopes that 2005 will be a year of campaigning to "make history and end poverty.

To do two things at once is to do neither." It's a great critique of multitasking, isn't it? Often attributed to the Roman writer Publilius Syrus, though you know how these attributions can be—he probably never actually said it. What I'm interested in, though, is whether it's true. I mean, it's obviously true for emailing at the dinner table, texting while driving, or perhaps live-tweeting at a TED Talk. But I'd argue that for certain important activities, doing two things at once—or three, or even four—is precisely what we should aim for. Consider Albert Einstein: in 1905, he published four remarkable scientific papers.

Allan Scott, despite experiencing some disappointment, is feeling optimistic about his chances of winning a medal at the upcoming European Indoor Championships following a promising start on the international stage. The 22-year-old athlete from Scotland achieved a commendable fourth place in the 60m hurdles at the Jose M Cagigal Memorial meeting in Madrid. "It was certainly a learning experience, and I am still very much in the running for a medal next week," said the East Kilbride native. The race saw Felipe Vivancos taking first place while matching the Spanish record, followed by Sweden's Robert Kronberg in second and Haiti's Dudley Dorival in third. Although Scott was somewhat dissatisfied with his performance in the final, having clocked a winning time of 7.64 seconds in his heat, he ran slightly slower by 0.04 seconds in the final of his first IAAF Indoor Grand Prix circuit.

There are currently hundreds of thousands of people on transplant lists, waiting for critical organs like kidneys, hearts, and livers that could save their lives. Unfortunately, there aren’t nearly enough donor organs available to fill that demand. What if, instead of waiting, we could create brand-new, customized organs from scratch? That’s the idea behind bioprinting, a branch of regenerative medicine currently under development. We’re not yet able to print complex organs, but simpler tissues, including blood vessels and tubes responsible for nutrient and waste exchange, are already within our grasp. Bioprinting is a biological cousin of 3D printing, a technique that deposits layers of material on top of each other to construct a three-dimensional object one slice at a time.

Fall 1974: I was attempting, for what felt like the 13th time, to fight my way through the second chapter of my high school algebra book. I was not then, nor am I now, a math person. I hate math. I am math phobic. You get the picture. As I struggled to make sense of algebra, the doorbell rang. I put down my book and went to open the door. No one was there. Great. I returned to my studies, and just as I was finding a little glimmer of understanding buried in my subconscious, the doorbell rang again. Bam, just like that, my breakthrough disappeared. I went to the door, and once more, no one was there. Just what a struggling math student needs—an uninvited practical joker.

Currently, most refugees live in cities rather than in refugee camps. They represent over 60 percent of the global refugee population. With the majority residing in urban areas, there is a strong need for a paradigm shift and fresh thinking. Instead of spending money on building walls, it would be more effective to invest in programs that enable refugees to help themselves. (applause) We always have to leave behind all our possessions, but not our skills and knowledge. If allowed to live productive lives, refugees can support themselves and contribute to the development of their host country. I was born in a city called Bukavu, in South Kivu, in the Democratic Republic of Congo. I am the fifth-born in a family of 12 children.

The starving orphan seeking a second helping of gruel. The spinster wasting away in her tattered wedding dress. The stone-hearted miser haunted by the ghost of Christmas past. More than a century after his death, these remain recognizable figures from the work of Charles Dickens. So striking is his body of work that it gave rise to its own adjective. But what are the features of Dickens's writing that make it so special? Dickens’s fiction brims with anticipation through brooding settings, plot twists, and mysteries. These elements of his work kept his audience eager for more. When first published, his stories were serialized, appearing a few chapters at a time in affordable literary journals before being reprinted as books.

Suddenly, over the past two days, I've been overwhelmed by severe bouts of exhaustion—utterly bone-tiring. Last evening, I went home and fell asleep, only waking when my mother called me around eight-thirty. When I woke up, it was completely dark, and I hadn't turned on the lights, so for a moment, I couldn't tell what time or day it was. It was just my mother's voice breaking through the darkness while I tried to grasp reality. I wonder why I'm feeling so drained... The weather is nice this morning. It was drizzling when I arrived. Though I've heard it's been raining in Calcutta for the last three days.

Menstruation: A natural biological process that affects over 800 million women worldwide, yet it remains a taboo subject in many cultures. Despite its prevalence, it's common to hear whispers or hushed tones when discussing menstruation. As I speak to you now, millions of women are experiencing their periods, yet it's often treated as an embarrassing or shameful topic. For many, it's a source of discomfort, from the physical symptoms to the social stigma surrounding it. From my perspective and that of the women I've spoken to, it's frustrating to have to navigate through daily life with the added burden of hiding and discretion. It's exhausting to have to carefully conceal sanitary products in a brown paper bag and rush to the bathroom, trying to avoid drawing attention to oneself. The stigma surrounding menstruation can make it difficult to focus on anything else, even during important meetings or classes, as the discomfort and embarrassment can be all-consuming.

We present a novel mixed-variable symplectic (mvs) integrator for planetary systems, which fully resolves close encounters. The method is based on a time regularization technique that preserves the stability properties of symplectic integrators while reducing the effective step size during close encounters between two planets. We employ a high-order mvs scheme, enabling the integration of large time steps far from close encounters. We demonstrate that this algorithm can resolve nearly exact collisions (i.e., with a mutual separation of a fraction of the physical radius) using the same time-step as in weakly perturbed problems, such as the solar system. We illustrate the long-term behavior of systems of six super-Earths experiencing strong scattering over 50 kyr. We compare our algorithm to hybrid methods, such as Mercury, and show that for an equivalent cost, we achieve much better energy conservation.

Jamieson Issues Warning to Bigots Scotland's justice minister has warned bigoted soccer fans that she intends to hit them "where it hurts most" by banning them from matches. Cathy Jamieson said exclusion orders are among a series of measures being considered in the Scottish Executive's campaign against sectarianism. She praised Celtic and Rangers for their efforts in addressing the problem. However, the minister stated that stopping sectarian abuse associated with Old Firm matches is a key objective. Ms. Jamieson was speaking ahead of the third-round Scottish Cup clash between the Glasgow clubs at Parkhead on Sunday. The sectarianism long associated with certain sections of the fanbase from both clubs has become a significant target for the Executive. Last week, Ms. Jamieson and First Minister Jack McConnell met with supporters' representatives from both clubs to discuss the issue.

Where do I start on this absolutely beautiful game? Its story is absolutely charming. It has so much detail put into it, and it's a bundle of happiness, bad puns, annoying dogs, and secrets. It is serious when it needs to be, but isn't a stranger to funny moments. The character design is on point, with various characters having various personalities and secrets to them. It stands out from other RPGs because you don't have to kill anyone, which is an unused concept in RPGs. With lots of inspiration from Earthbound, one of the best RPGs I've seen, you can tell that it's going to be good. It easily has the best soundtrack I've heard in forever.

Good afternoon, I'm proud to be here at TEDxKrakow. I'll try to speak a little bit today about a phenomenon that can, and actually is, changing the world, and its name is people power. I'll start with an anecdote, or for those of you who are Monty Python lovers, a Monty Python-type sketch. Here it is: it's December 15, 2010. Somebody gives you a bet: you will look at a crystal ball and see the future; the future will be accurate, but you need to share it with the world. Okay, curiosity killed the cat, you take the bet, you look at the crystal ball. One hour later, you're sitting in a building of the national TV, in a top show, and you tell the story.

*To the Moon* transcends the boundaries of a conventional video game—it is a profound emotional journey that stands among the most memorable experiences of my life. While Telltale's *The Walking Dead* may be renowned for its heart-wrenching narrative, it pales in comparison to the devastating yet deeply fulfilling impact of *To the Moon*. Rarely has a work of fiction left me so emotionally shattered yet utterly satisfied.  

This masterpiece exemplifies storytelling at its finest, elevated by a breathtakingly beautiful soundtrack that complements every poignant moment. Admittedly, the controls can feel cumbersome, and the gameplay mechanics are not its strongest asset—yet the narrative is so extraordinary that such flaws become inconsequential. The game’s humor, cleverly woven throughout, never undermines its emotional weight but instead enhances its charm. And for fans of *Doctor Who*, the abundant references are an absolute delight.

When a new user signs up on a website, we typically have no information about him or her, i.e., no interaction with items, no user profile, and no social links with other users. Under such circumstances, it is crucial for our recommender systems to effectively attract the users at the first interaction, so that the users decide to stay on the website and become active users. This problem falls under the category of new user cold-start, and it is crucial to the development and survival of a company. Existing works on user cold-start recommendation either require additional user efforts, such as setting up an interview process, or making use of side information, such as user demographics, locations, social relations, etc. However, users may not be willing to provide the interview and side information on cold-start users is usually not available, which can hinder the effectiveness of these methods.

Fallout 3 had a superior story. The settlement system disrupts the narrative so much that managing them feels like tedious babysitting rather than enjoying the main plot. Certain keys are hardcoded and can't be remapped. The game is riddled with bugs—at one point, my Pip-Boy vanished permanently, forcing me to dig through console commands to restore it. Power armor lacks the prestige of earlier games; it's handed to you almost immediately, and fusion cores are overly abundant in shops. The story is mediocre, and while the game is more stable than Fallout 3 (which crashed constantly), it still falls short. It doesn't surpass Fallout 3 or New Vegas. Despite the hype, this installment disappoints in nearly every aspect.

I'd like to take you on the epic quest of the Rosetta spacecraft. Escorting and landing the probe on a comet has been my passion for the past two years. To do that, I need to explain something about the origin of the solar system. When we go back four and a half billion years, there was a cloud of gas and dust. In the center of this cloud, our sun formed and ignited. Along with that, what we now know as planets, comets, and asteroids formed. What then happened, according to theory, is that when the Earth had cooled down a bit after its formation, comets massively impacted the Earth and delivered water to it. They probably also delivered complex organic material to Earth, and that may have bootstrapped the emergence of life.

Summer TV used to be a dead zone full of repeats or something. But now there are two great new shows on TV, and I’ve been watching both religiously for the last week. *Blow Out*—the gayest concept ever that seemingly has zero gay people in sight. Hairstylist Jonathan Antin wants to build a second location for his incredibly popular West Hollywood salon, this time in Beverly Hills. Normally, no one would care—certainly not me—but as I was flipping through the channels, I caught a glimpse of Jonathan, equally narcissistic and dull, looking at an empty space on Rodeo Dr., declaring (in a public voice), 'I am going to build here the greatest salon in the history of the world—ever!' We developed single-shot, shaped pulses for ultra-high fidelity population transfer on a three-level quantum system in a lambda configuration. To ensure high fidelity, we employ the Lewis-Riesenfeld (L-R) method to derive a family of solutions leading to an exact transfer, where the solutions follow a single dynamical mode of the L-R invariant. Among this family, we identify a tracking solution with a single parameter to control simultaneously the fidelity of the transfer, the population of the excited state, and robustness. We define a measure of the robustness of an ultra-high-fidelity transfer as the minimum percentile deviation on the pulse areas at which the infidelity rises above 10^-4. The robustness of our shaped pulses is found superior to that of Gaussian and adiabatically-optimized pulses for moderate pulse areas.

From  Blair, Lynn

Subject  NSP Powerpac Issues - Lynn

Following our discussion yesterday afternoon, Reyna and I came back and reviewed the NSP Powerpac agreement. We were specifically looking for language in this agreement which may require us to be true to the customer, regardless of whether or not we were allocating storage on a given gas day. Ranking algorithms are fundamental to online platforms, from search engines to recommendation systems. In this study, we uncover an unexpected outcome of popularity-based rankings: items reporting a rare signal tend to capture a disproportionately large share of overall traffic. This "few-get-richer" phenomenon becomes apparent in environments with limited distinct categories of items (such as left-leaning versus right-leaning news sources), where rankings are determined by popularity. We provide a theoretical demonstration that this effect arises when users click predominantly on top-ranked items and exhibit diverse preferences toward different item categories. Through simulations, we examine how varying assumptions about the environment and user behavior impact the intensity of this effect. We further verify our predictions in an online experiment with real participants. Our results hold significant implications for understanding how misinformation spreads.

Density-based clustering operates on the principle of associating groups with specific characteristics of the underlying data's probability distribution. By referencing the true (though unknown) population structure, this approach positions the clustering problem within a standard inferential framework, where the ideal population clustering is defined as the partition generated by the true density function. The nonparametric implementation of this method, termed modal clustering, establishes a connection between groups and the domains of attraction of the density's modes. Practically, this requires a nonparametric density estimate, and careful selection of the smoothing parameter—which influences both the density's shape and its modal structure—is essential for determining the final partition. In this study, we examine the challenge of density estimation for modal clustering from an asymptotic standpoint.

Baryonic feedback effects lead to a suppression of the weak lensing angular power spectrum on small scales. The poorly constrained shape and amplitude of this suppression are an important source of uncertainties for upcoming cosmological weak lensing surveys, such as Euclid or LSST. In this, our first paper in a series of two, we use simulations to build a Euclid-like tomographic mock data-set for the cosmic shear power spectrum and the corresponding covariance matrix, which are both corrected for baryonic effects following the baryonification method of Schneider et al. (2019). Additionally, we develop an emulator to obtain fast predictions of the baryonic power suppression, allowing us to perform a likelihood inference analysis for a standard LCDM cosmology with both cosmological and astrophysical parameters.

I recently downloaded the game and spent a brief period playing it, but unfortunately, it failed to impress me. The login process was more time-consuming than the actual gameplay, as I encountered a recurring issue where the "update to terms of service" prompt would repeatedly appear, requiring me to restart the client to progress. This is somewhat inconvenient, especially since I was asked to link my Steam and GFace accounts, citing the need for downloadable content (DLC). However, this linking is not reversible, which means that a company I'm not familiar with now has access to my Steam information. In terms of the game itself, I found it to be a noticeable clone of another game, with the added disappointment of subpar gunplay. It's astonishing that the developers managed to make it worse, as it's clear that they had the potential to do better.

The Paris triumph has sparked hope among Welsh fans. Is there a more thrilling Six Nations match than Saturday's unforgettable clash in Paris? And can the Welsh resurgence continue to claim their first Grand Slam since 1978? These are the pressing questions not just for Welsh supporters, but for rugby fans everywhere, following a spectacular performance in Paris. Welsh legend Mervyn Davies, a member of two of the three Grand Slam-winning teams of the 1970s, acclaimed it as "one of the greatest performances of the past thirty years." Martyn Williams, who scored two tries for Wales that day, described it as "one of the most surreal games I've ever played." A dejected French coach, Bernard Laporte, simply noted: "There was a French half and there was a Welsh half.

Davies favors Gloucester future, Wales hooker Mefin Davies is likely to stay with the English side Gloucester despite reported interest from the Neath-Swansea Ospreys. BBC Wales understands the Ospreys are interested in the 32-year-old, but he would prefer to remain with Gloucester. Davies, one of the stars of Saturday's RBS Six Nations win over England, is only on a one-year contract at Kingsholm. However, he has proven his worth to the Zurich Premiership side and is likely to receive a new deal next season. The summer demise of the Celtic Warriors region left Davies in the cold and forced him to take a semi-professional contract with Neath RFC. Although he got game time with the Ospreys at the request of Wales management, he admitted before his move to Gloucester that he was angry with the way he was treated.

While not perfect, this game has been incredibly enjoyable. The save system and risk of sudden death can be frustrating at times. During my first playthrough, I lost my main character around the 10-hour mark of a 20-hour campaign, and I would have appreciated the option to reload from a previous save. To mitigate such losses, I've resorted to using a third-party mod and save tool. Additionally, the aiming reticule is somewhat small, and the zoom feature could be more powerful. Despite these issues, the game has been a blast. As someone who isn't typically a fan of zombie games, I've been drawn in by the combination of strategy, missions, and first-person shooter and beat-em-up elements. I find myself playing as often as possible, always tempted to complete just one more mission or supply run before shutting down the game.

I realize this might sound unusual, but I believe robots have the potential to inspire us to become better humans. I spent my childhood in Bethlehem, Pennsylvania, a town closely tied to Bethlehem Steel. My father was an engineer, and during my formative years, he would teach me the intricacies of how things function. Together, we embarked on projects like building model rockets and slot cars. This is the go-kart we constructed as a team. That's me at the wheel, alongside my sister and my best friend from that time. So, I had graduated seven years ago from Berkeley with a dual degree in molecular and cell biology and linguistics, and I attended a career fair on campus, where I landed an interview with a startup called Theranos. At the time, there wasn't much information available about the company, but what was there was truly impressive. Essentially, Theranos was creating a medical device that would allow you to run your entire blood panel on a finger-stick of blood. This meant you wouldn't have to get a large needle stuck in your arm to get your blood test done. This was intriguing not only because it was less painful, but also, it could potentially open the door to predictive diagnostics.

I'm thrilled to embark on the epic quest of the Rosetta spacecraft, a mission that has captivated my attention for the past two years. As we prepare to escort and land the probe on a comet, I'd like to take a moment to share with you the fascinating story of the solar system's origin. Let's journey back in time, approximately 4.5 billion years ago, to a era when a vast cloud of gas and dust enveloped our cosmic neighborhood. At the heart of this primordial cloud, our sun burst forth, igniting the spark of life. As the planets, comets, and asteroids took shape, a pivotal event occurred  the delivery of comets to Earth. According to theory, these celestial bodies, now known as comets, struck our planet with immense force, depositing water and potentially even complex organic matter, which may have jumpstarted the emergence of life on Earth.

According to recent research, high-speed broadband is significantly empowering individuals to express themselves online. A study by the UK think-tank Demos reveals that one in four broadband users in Britain regularly uploads content and maintains personal websites. The report highlights that the availability of constant, fast internet connections is transforming how people engage with the web. Currently, over five million households in the UK are equipped with broadband, and this figure continues to rise rapidly. The Demos report investigates the influence of broadband on users' online behaviors, discovering that more than half of broadband users access the internet before having breakfast. Moreover, one in five users confessed to waking up in the middle of the night specifically to browse the web. The report further emphasizes that broadband is motivating individuals to take a more engaged and participative approach to the online world.

Deadline nears for Fiat-GM deal. Fiat and General Motors (GM) have until midnight on 1 February to resolve a dispute over a potential takeover. The deadline marks the point at which Fiat will gain the right to sell its car division to GM, as part of an alliance agreed in 2000. GM, whose own European operations are losing money, no longer wants to own the unprofitable Fiat unit. Reports of stalled talks sent Fiat shares down 1.2% on Tuesday, following Monday's 4% gain on hopes of a payout. The US firm is believed to be offering around $2bn (£1.06bn) to exit the arrangement. It has argued the deal was invalidated by Fiat's decision to sell off its finance arm and halve GM's stake through a capital-raising effort. The 2000 deal stemmed from a race between GM and DaimlerChrysler to partner with Fiat.

Electric network frequency (ENF) fluctuations hold immense potential in multimedia forensics, serving as a potent tool for estimating frequency components. Our novel approach leverages temporal windowing, based on the filter-bank capon spectral estimator, to efficiently estimate ENF. Unlike traditional methods, we utilize a non-rectangular temporal window, allowing for more flexible and accurate frequency estimation. Additionally, we employ a Gohberg-Semencul factorization of the model covariance matrix, resulting in a more efficient and accurate spectral estimation process. Our approach outperforms existing state-of-the-art methods in both power recordings and speech recordings, with a time window of just one second. Fast implementation of matrix inversions is achieved through the use of Krylov matrices, further enhancing the efficiency of our method.

While it may seem an obvious statement, I began pondering this issue about 12 years ago, initially in the context of developing countries. However, considering the global scope, I believe it's essential to recognize that every country on the planet has areas where good teachers are sorely needed. If you envision a world map, I think you'll agree that for each country, you could draw circles around regions where the need for quality education is most pressing. Moreover, these are often the very areas from which the greatest challenges arise. We have an intriguing paradox - good educators are often reluctant to venture to places where they are needed most urgently. In 1999, I embarked on an experiment to address this conundrum, starting with a simple initiative in New Delhi.

Melzer stuns Agassi as second seed Andre Agassi suffered a crushing defeat at the hands of Jurgen Melzer in the SAP Open quarter-finals. Agassi was frequently outmaneuvered by the Austrian's clever drop shots in San Jose, ultimately losing 6-3, 6-1. Defending champion and top seed Andy Roddick staged a comeback to defeat Sweden's Thomas Enqvist 3-6, 7-6 (8-6), 7-5. However, unseeded Cyril Saulnier upset fourth seed Vincent Spadea 6-2, 6-4, and Tommy Haas overcame eighth seed Max Mirnyi 6-7 (2-7), 7-6 (7-3), 6-2. Melzer has now beaten Agassi in two of their three encounters. "I had a solid game plan and executed it flawlessly," he said. "It's always challenging to face Andre. I didn't want him to dictate the game. He forces you to cover the entire court.

I find investigative reporting to be problematic. It seems wrong to me, or perhaps I should rephrase that, the article I'm working on and its consequences are not exactly what I'm comfortable with. Perhaps it's because I don't fully believe in it, and therefore I can't fully embrace it. The angst behind it. The full-out passion and commitment required to question everything. I've grown up in a country where questioning is not tolerated. Really, you just don't do it. They shoot you for that shit. They put you in jail with all these internal security measures. I'm not used to this freedom of speech...not used to questioning out loud. Sure, I'm not the easiest person to get along with.

When I was 14, my family was in the process of adopting my little brothers from Ethiopia. One day, my mom asked, "What day should we put for their birthday?" I thought it was a ridiculous question and replied, "The day they were born, obviously?" Then my mom explained, "Well, Kristen, neither of your little brothers has a birth certificate, so how do you suggest we find out when that was?" I was taken aback. Now, 20 years later, I'm still working on this issue, but instead of trying to solve the mystery of my brothers' missing birth certificates, I'm trying to solve this problem globally. So, what do birth certificates have to do with international development?

I liked the idea of that as an offense - maybe that could have been a way out of being burned as a witch. As a non-American blogger, I've been a little reluctant to get involved in some of these discussions, particularly the bits about flags, but since I started the subject, I suppose I'd better say something. Firstly, I think the idea of having a law against flag-burning is completely nuts. The moment you start according a symbol - any symbol - that kind of automatic reverence and protection, you've taken a small but important step towards totalitarianism. Symbols are inherently fickle - they don't inherently represent anything in themselves; it's what they are perceived as meaning or made to represent that's important.

Mutation-based fuzzing typically uses an initial set of non-crashing seed inputs, known as a corpus, to generate new inputs through mutation. A corpus of potential seeds often contains thousands of similar inputs, which can lead to inefficient fuzzing efforts through exhaustive mutation from all available seeds. To tackle this issue, fuzzers come equipped with distillation tools (e.g., afl-cmin) that select the smallest subset of seeds, which trigger the same range of instrumentation data points as the full corpus. It is commonly suggested that minimizing the number and cumulative size of the seeds results in more efficient fuzzing, a hypothesis we systematically explore. We present the results of 34+ CPU-years of fuzzing using five distillation approaches to understand their impact on finding bugs in real-world software.

Sustainability represents the what, the where, and the how of what is caught. The who and the why are what matter most to me. I want to know the people behind my dinner choices. I want to know how they impact me. I want to know how they rely on the ocean's bounty for their livelihood. Understanding all of this allows us to shift our perception of seafood from a commodity to an opportunity to restore our ecosystem. It enables us to celebrate the seafood we're fortunate enough to eat. So, what do we call this? I think we call it restorative seafood. Sustainability is the capacity to endure and maintain, while restorative is the ability to replenish and progress.

Please be advised that the CFTC has approved new Nymex Rule 6.40C ("Average Price Calculations by Futures Commission Merchants"), which is attached and is now in effect. This new rule, consistent with a recent CFTC advisory, permits FCMS, under certain conditions specified in the rule, to use their own internal, proprietary systems to generate average price ("AP") calculations when multiple prices are received on an order or series of orders.

UK debut for Kevin Spacey movie: Hollywood stars Kevin Spacey and Kate Bosworth attended the British premiere of the new film, Beyond the Sea, in London's Leicester Square on Thursday. Spacey, 45, wrote, directed, and starred in the film, inspired by the life of 1950s crooner Bobby Darin. "This is my tribute to someone I think was a remarkable talent," said Spacey, who, as Darin, sings all 18 songs on the film's soundtrack. Bosworth, 21, plays Darin's wife—real-life Hollywood actress Sandra Dee. "I knew absolutely nothing about Bobby Darin before this film, but now I'm a huge fan," said Bosworth, who attended the premiere with her British boyfriend Orlando Bloom.

We introduce a novel Skyrme-like conserved current in the effective theory of pions and vector mesons based on the idea of hidden local symmetry. The associated charge is equivalent to the Skyrmion charge for any smooth configuration. In addition, there exist singular configurations that can be identified as \( n_f=1 \) baryons charged under the new symmetry. Under this identification, the vector mesons play the role of the Chern-Simons vector fields living on the quantum Hall droplet that forms the \( n_f=1 \) baryon. We propose that this current is the correct effective expression for the baryon current at low energies. This proposal gives a unified picture for the two types of baryons and allows them to continuously transform one to the other in a natural way. In addition, Chern-Simons dualities on the droplet can be interpreted as a result of Seiberg-like duality between gluons and vector mesons.

Debra Hill, the screenwriter and producer best known for her role in creating the 1970s horror classic "Halloween," has passed away in Los Angeles at the age of 54 after battling cancer. Hill co-wrote the 1978 film, which featured Jamie Lee Curtis as a babysitter haunted by a psychopath. Under the direction of John Carpenter, the film grossed over $60 million (£31.3 million) globally, setting a record for independent films at the time. Hill also collaborated with Carpenter on "Escape from New York" and "The Fog." Born in New Jersey, she started her career as a production assistant, advancing through the industry to become an assistant director and second-unit director before partnering with Carpenter. Hill is celebrated as a trailblazer for women in film, taking on roles during the 1970s that were typically filled by men.

China's Beijing-based Three Gorges Corporation, the builder of the massive Three Gorges Dam, is defying a government order to halt construction of one of its massive projects, the Xiluodu Dam, according to the Chinese State Press. The company is instead choosing to pay a fine for non-compliance, despite being ordered to suspend work on the dam, which is one of 30 large-scale construction projects halted due to a lack of proper environmental assessments. Additionally, the company has ignored orders to cease construction at two other projects, the Three Gorges Underground Power Plant and the Three Gorges Project Electrical Power Supply Plant. As of now, only 22 of the 30 projects targeted by China's State Environmental Protection Agency (SEPA) for failing to conduct mandatory environmental impact assessments have complied with the shutdown order, while the remaining eight projects, including the Xiluodu Dam, continue to operate without proper environmental checks.

It seems that the Wizards on the coast have forgotten this crucial aspect. If you opt to play with friends, you can earn gold, and gold is crucial. If you choose to play for free, it will take you roughly four hours a day for upwards of a month, solo, to unlock the cards.

Man City 0-2 Man Utd: Manchester United reduced Chelsea's Premiership lead to nine points after a scrappy victory over Manchester City. Wayne Rooney met Gary Neville's cross to the near post with a low shot, which went in via a deflection off Richard Dunne, to put United ahead. Seven minutes later, the unfortunate Dunne hooked a volley over David James' head and into his own net. Steve McManaman wasted City's best chance when he shot wide from three yards in the first half. In the opening 45 minutes, United had looked unlikely to earn the win they needed to maintain any chance of catching Chelsea in the title race. Their approach play was more labored than patient, and they managed to fashion just one chance—a Paul Scholes header over the bar.

Entanglement serves as a fundamental resource in quantum information processing. A commonly employed method for detecting entanglement is the entanglement witness, where the measurement of the witness operator yields non-negative results for all separable states but may produce negative values for certain entangled states. In practice, constructing an efficient entanglement witness for general multipartite entangled states is highly challenging due to the exponential growth of the Hilbert-space dimension with increasing system size. For $n$-partite Greenberger-Horne-Zeilinger (GHZ)-like states, the most robust witness scheme necessitates $n+1$ local measurement settings and can withstand up to $1/2$ white noise. In contrast, the most efficient witness for GHZ-like states requires only two local measurement settings and remains effective for white noise levels up to $1/3$.

Iraq is set to invite bids for two telephone licenses, aiming to significantly enhance nationwide coverage over the next decade. The Ministry of Communications announced that bids are welcome from local, Arab, and international companies. The successful bidder will collaborate with the Iraqi Telecommunications and Post Company (ITPC) to install and operate a fixed phone network, offering voice, fax, and internet services. The ministry expressed its ambition to raise Iraq's "very low telephone service penetration rate from about 4.5% today to approximately 25% within 10 years." Additionally, it seeks to develop a "highly visible and transformative telecommunications sector." Details regarding the bidding and tender process will be available on the ministry's website on February 9. The ministry is also planning an investor roadshow in Amman, Jordan.

You can't help but feel it, you can't escape it  a piece of you will always remain with me. Even if I never bore you a child, even if I was never married to you, even if I was never officially bound to you... I loved you immensely, and to this day, the feeling still resonates within my heart. Forever, I will always be reminded of the few precious nights we spent together, our hearts and limbs intertwined in a soulful embrace, bathed in the salty darkness of an evening by the beach... or a kiss under the rain. Whenever I feel the sensation of a dark glass piercing my heart, you will be there. That piece of you... the eternal love that once resided within me... and still lingers. For her.

This paper introduces a millimeter-scale CMOS 64$\times$64 single charged particle radiation detector system designed for external beam cancer radiotherapy. A 1$\times$1 $\mu m^2$ diode is employed to measure the energy deposited by a single charged particle within the depletion region. The array offers a substantial detection area of 512$\times$512 $\mu m^2$. Instead of detecting the voltage drop due to radiation, the system captures the pulse width, which is the duration for the voltage to return to its baseline. This approach eliminates the need for power-intensive and bulky analog-to-digital converters. A prototype ASIC has been fabricated using the TSMC 65 nm LP CMOS process, with an average static power consumption of 0.535 mW under a 1.2 V analog and digital power supply. The entire system's functionality is successfully validated in a clinical setting using a 67.5 MeV proton beam.

Menstruation. A natural biological process, long shrouded in secrecy and stigma, has been transformed into a hushed conversation. Despite the fact that over 800 million women worldwide are menstruating at any given time, the topic remains a source of discomfort and embarrassment. As I speak, women are silently enduring the physical and emotional toll of their periods, often hiding their discomfort with a sense of shame. From my own experience and that of those around me, I can attest to the exhaustion that comes with navigating this natural yet taboo aspect of womanhood. The constant need to be discreet, the struggle to maintain a sense of normalcy despite the pain and discomfort, and the isolation that can result from feeling like an outcast are all too familiar. It is time for this stigma to be broken, for menstruation to be recognized as a natural and necessary part of life, and for women to be afforded the respect and dignity they deserve.

Understanding how strongly correlated two-dimensional (2D) systems can lead to unconventional superconductivity with high critical temperatures remains one of the most significant unresolved challenges in condensed matter physics. Ultracold 2D Fermi gases have become ideal model systems—clean and highly controllable—for investigating the interplay between strong correlations and reduced dimensionality. However, direct evidence of superfluidity in such systems has remained elusive. In this study, we observe superfluidity in an ultracold 2D Fermi gas by moving a periodic potential through the system and detecting the absence of dissipation below a critical velocity \(v_{\rm c}\). We measure \(v_{\rm c}\) across varying interaction strengths and identify a peak in the crossover regime between bosonic and fermionic superfluidity. These results firmly establish ultracold Fermi gases as a versatile platform for exploring the effects of reduced dimensionality on strongly correlated superfluids.

As it appears, whatever creative liberties are taken to craft the vibrant colors within freeze pops, there must be a clear warning  a hint of the unusual may make one's life a bit unconventional. While laughter is an excellent coping mechanism, one cannot help but wonder if we would have found ourselves stumbling upon that inferno, sipping beer in the midst of a fierce wind, had we not been indulging in those sweet treats earlier. Finding ourselves at the doorstep of a bar, unfortunately closed for business, we took a moment to peruse an office-supplies catalogue that had arrived in the mail. Fate smiled upon us. Who could have predicted that within these pages lay the extraordinary offers of a lifetime? Not I, for certain. I list but a few  purchase 36 permanent marker pens, receive a stunt kite. Purchase 30 lever arch files, receive a barbecue.

According to a two-year study, more women and older individuals are taking proactive measures to secure their home computers against online threats. The number of women purchasing software to shield PCs from viruses, spam, and spyware has increased by 11.2% annually between 2002 and 2004. Conducted for the net security firm Preventon, the study indicates that security awareness is expanding among a diverse range of internet users. Notably, 40% of those who purchase home internet security programs are retirees, with this number rising by an average of 13.2% over the past three years. Interestingly, more retired women (53%) are investing in security software compared to their male counterparts. This research highlights the evolving demographics and shattered stereotypes of web users, alongside an increasing recognition of the risks associated with high-speed broadband connections.

More women are turning to internet security. Older people and women are increasingly taking charge of protecting home computers against malicious online attacks, according to a two-year study. The number of women purchasing programs to protect PCs from viruses, spam, and spyware attacks rose by 11.2% each year between 2002 and 2004. The study, conducted for the net security firm Preventon, shows that security messages are reaching a diverse audience of internet users. It is estimated that 40% of those buying home net security programs are retired individuals. Over the last three years, this number has increased by an average of 13.2%. Moreover, more retired women (53%) are buying security software compared to retired men. The research reflects the changing stereotypes and demographics of web users, as well as an increased awareness of the greater risks that high-speed broadband internet connections can pose to users.

The Chancellor, Gordon Brown, has stated that the focus of the upcoming election campaign will centre on the economy's future, with Labour's poll strategy. This comes after a meeting at Number 10 Downing Street, where cabinet members gathered to discuss the election. Brown emphasized that voters will recognize that Labour has brought stability and growth, and will continue to do so in the future. Meanwhile, the Conservatives have outlined their plans to tackle the 'yob culture' issue, and the Liberal Democrats have provided more details about their proposals to replace council tax. The Archbishop of Canterbury has written an open letter to all three parties, urging them not to turn the election into a competition about who can most effectively frighten voters about terrorism, asylum, and crime. He called on them not to exploit people's fears in this election.

Low-symmetry 2D materials, such as res$_2$ and rese$_2$ monolayers, black phosphorus monolayers, group-IV monochalcogenide monolayers, borophene, and others, exhibit more intricate atomistic structures than the honeycomb lattices of graphene, hexagonal boron nitride, and transition metal dichalcogenides. The reduced symmetries of these emerging materials give rise to inhomogeneous electron, optical, valley, and spin responses, as well as entirely new properties, including ferroelasticity, ferroelectricity, magnetism, spin-wave phenomena, large nonlinear optical properties, photogalvanic effects, and superconductivity. Novel electronic topological properties, nonlinear elastic properties, and structural phase transformations can also occur due to low symmetry. The "Beyond Graphene  Low-Symmetry and Anisotropic 2D Materials" special topic was assembled to highlight recent experimental and theoretical research on these emerging materials.

DTE Energy, www.dtecs.com/sitemap.html, has retained us to assist them in placing two Directors of Sales for the Eastern and Western regions. The Eastern position will be located in Ann Arbor, Michigan, and the Western position in Denver, Colorado. Both Directors of Sales will report to the Vice President of Sales. Someone once quipped that politics is, of course, 'showbiz for ugly people.' So, on that basis, I feel like I've really arrived. The other thing to consider is the immense honor it is, as a politician, to give a TED Talk, particularly in the UK, where the reputation of politics has taken a hit due to the expenses scandal. In fact, there was even a recent story that scientists had thought about replacing rats in their experiments with politicians. When asked why, they replied, 'Well, there's no shortage of politicians, and no one really minds what happens to them. After all, there are some things that rats just won't do.' Menstruation  a natural biological process shrouded in centuries of stigma, has undergone a profound transformation, rendering it a topic that most of us dare not discuss openly. As I converse with you now, over 800 million women worldwide are currently experiencing their period. Without it, none of us would exist, yet it remains an "embarrassing" subject to broach. Drawing from my personal experience and that of the women around me, I can attest that it's a grueling ordeal. It's exhausting to carefully extract a discreet pad from a brown paper bag, hastily stash it in one's pocket during a class, and rush to the washroom with an air of furtiveness. It's draining to sit through lessons and meetings, pretending to be utterly composed, while internally struggling to cope with the intense physical discomfort of period cramps.

I downloaded the game and played it for a short time, but it didn’t stand out. I spent more time attempting to log in than actually playing. There's a bug that claims the terms of service have been updated, but when you click 'accept,' the message just reappears. You have to restart the client to resolve this issue. That alone isn't too bad, but why do I need to link my Steam and Gface accounts? They claim it's necessary for DLC, yet this seems unnecessary. Moreover, it's irreversible, so now a company I don't care about has access to my Steam information. The game itself is just average. It clearly imitates Call of Duty, but they've somehow managed to make the gunplay even worse. How is that even possible?

I experienced no performance issues even at release, and after all the patches, it plays exceptionally well. In my opinion, this is truly a great and entertaining game, although the format can be somewhat annoying (as the free-roam city area repopulates with new enemies every time you progress in the story). I particularly enjoyed the 'dual play' segments (where you can play as Batman along with one other character), and I greatly appreciated the ability to use the controller for most parts of the game and switch to the mouse and keyboard for the tank combat segments. Driving your Batmobile around the free-roam city—especially when chasing the APC commanders—becomes quite enjoyable once you master using the brakes to skid around corners. Once you get the hang of it, driving around the city is truly enjoyable!

Sid Meier's Civilization: Beyond Earth is a turn-based game that challenges players to build their civilization to a predetermined goal or 'victory.' Unlike previous games in the series, this installment takes place in the future and introduces new mechanics such as the affinity system and orbital layer. While I appreciate 2K's effort to innovate and add new features, I found the game to be overwhelming at times due to these new mechanics. One area that could be improved is the addition of new artwork and visuals, particularly in the communication and interaction with other leaders. By incorporating more detailed and visually striking environments, the game could become more immersive and engaging.

On a lighter note, the look people get when they're struggling can be quite entertaining, I have to admit.

Warframe is an enjoyable game for the initial few hours of play, offering an engaging experience. However, as time progresses, the game's limitations become apparent, with repetition in combat, enemies, and gameplay becoming the primary focus. A notable exception is the developers' dedication to regular updates, which often include substantial content additions, such as significant system overhauls, new warframes, and various tweaks and features. This makes Warframe suitable for casual play. Nevertheless, its ability to sustain long-term interest is questionable, unless you have a strong affinity for grinding. On the positive side, the game is free, making it worth trying out, especially if you're looking for a brief gaming fix.

I had the opportunity to experience EU at a later stage, having purchased it on sale, which also included EW. As a result, I was fortunate enough to avoid the initial issues that some players encountered. Despite its challenging early stages, I thoroughly enjoyed the game, finding it to be a truly rewarding experience when executed correctly. In contrast, my experience with XCOM2 has been overwhelmingly positive. The game runs smoothly on medium settings, even on my relatively outdated GTX560 with 2GB of VRAM. Initially, the game defaulted to high settings, resulting in some minor performance issues, but reducing the settings to medium resolved the problem entirely. It's possible that I've simply been fortunate in avoiding the issues that have plagued some players, but it's difficult not to notice that many of the negative reviews seem to stem from an unrealistic sense of entitlement.

February 7, 2014 - Unfortunately, I cannot thoroughly recommend or enjoy this game due to the short time I've played it. I know I would be addicted if it didn't have the following two game-breaking issues. The lack of Australian servers puts you at a severe disadvantage, creating unbearable lag. Moreover, I played through the offline training mode and it was stuttering and lagging as well (it's astonishing that this happened). Additionally, the poor ping rate only adds to the frustration. Everything about the game is chaotic, from the menus to the backgrounds, training, and in-game assets. I've never experienced this issue with this card and initially thought it might not be compatible with my graphics card. Setting my GPU back to stock settings and, sure enough, it didn't make a difference.

Bastion is a brilliant game with an engaging storyline. Set in the aftermath of a calamity, you embark on rebuilding the bastion. The game offers plenty of choices, including deciding what to construct for strategic advantages, selecting weapons and power-ups, and choosing drinks for bonus effects. The art style and music are fantastic, with vibrant visuals that prove the apocalypse doesn't have to be dull. Best of all, the game features narration that responds to your actions. For instance, if you pick up a weapon and then swap it for another, the narration will comment on your choice of the new weapon.

Baryonic feedback effects lead to a suppression of the weak lensing angular power spectrum on small scales. The poorly constrained shape and amplitude of this suppression are an important source of uncertainties for upcoming cosmological weak lensing surveys such as Euclid or LSST. In this first paper in a series of two, we use simulations to build a Euclid-like tomographic mock data-set for the cosmic shear power spectrum and the corresponding covariance matrix, which are both corrected for baryonic effects following the baryonification method of Schneider et al. (2019). In addition, we develop an emulator to obtain fast predictions of the baryonic power suppression, allowing us to perform a likelihood inference analysis for a standard $\Lambda$CDM cosmology with both cosmological and astrophysical parameters.

I'm a cultural omnivore, whose daily commute is fueled by an iPod filled with a diverse range of artists, from classical composers like Wagner and Mozart, to pop diva Christina Aguilera, country singer Josh Turner, and gangsta rap artist Kirk Franklin. My music library also features concerti, symphonies, and much more. As a voracious reader, my tastes span from Ian McEwan to Stephanie Meyer, and I've even devoured the entire Twilight tetralogy. At home, I indulge in my home theater, where I watch a wide array of DVDs, video on demand, and television shows. My favorite programs include "Law & Order: SVU," Tina Fey's "30 Rock," and "Judge Judy," where the cases are real, the people are real, and the rulings are final.

The aftermath of the first week back from practicum was a struggle, but it wasn't until the second week that the smoke began to clear. Post-practicum trauma was a hidden epidemic that is only now coming to light. I was struck, but the eventual meeting with the course coordinator allowed me to release my burden, and in doing so, I prevented another student from being stuck with a pedagogically deficient practicum experience. Yesterday, on Monday, I ran into another agitated classmate who was determined to warn the student who was about to embark on the next practicum, and I calmed her down, suggesting that we should first approach the administrator.

Observations of thermally driven transverse vibrations in a photonic crystal waveguide (PCW) are presented. The PCW comprises two parallel nanobeams separated by a 240 nm vacuum gap. Developed models are validated for transducing beam motion into phase and amplitude modulation of a weak optical probe propagating in a guided mode of the PCW, for probe frequencies both far from and near the dielectric band edge. Given the PCW's design for near-field atom trapping, this research establishes a foundation for assessing potential detrimental effects of thermal motion on optical atomic traps near PCW surfaces. The long-term objective is to establish strong atom-mediated links between individual phonons of vibration and single photons propagating in the guided modes of the PCW, thereby enabling quantum-level optomechanics involving atoms, photons, and phonons.

A groundbreaking study by University College London reveals that lowering the drink-drive limit by a third could have a significantly greater impact on road safety than previously anticipated. According to the research, reducing the blood alcohol concentration limit from 80mg per 100ml to 50mg could potentially save 65 lives annually, a substantial increase from the previously estimated 50 lives per year. The findings, published by the Parliamentary Advisory Council for Transport Safety, coincide with the upcoming vote on the government's Road Safety Bill, which proposes changes to speeding fines but does not address the drink-drive limit. The research, led by renowned transport expert Professor Richard Allsop, also suggests that this reduction would lead to 230 fewer serious injuries on UK roads each year, resulting in an estimated annual saving of £119m for the country.

I have always prided myself on making anyone who frequents Casa Pescatore as comfortable as possible. I want them to feel at home, just as they would in their own residence. Yesterday, I hope I succeeded. The masses all congregated on Perry Ave with what many consider 'extreme prejudice'. At 4:30pm, my best friend Eric and his son Nunu came over with a huge pan of baked ziti. The tables were set, and I had all the food I cooked ready; the bells were ringing crazily. Before I could blink, my best friend from Florida walked through the door, and that made me smile. I missed that guy so much. The bonus was him bringing his wife along. She is expecting, and this is the closest I could get to being an uncle. I spoiled that girl with tray after tray of food and tiramisu.

Mutation-based fuzzing relies on an initial corpus of non-crashing seed inputs that serve as the foundation for generating new test cases through modification. However, these seed collections frequently contain numerous redundant inputs with minimal variation, resulting in inefficient fuzzing as the system exhaustively processes near-identical samples. To optimize this process, modern fuzzers incorporate distillation techniques (such as afl-cmin) that identify and retain only the most essential seeds—those capable of exercising the same breadth of program instrumentation as the complete corpus. Industry experience indicates that reducing both the quantity and total size of seed inputs enhances fuzzing efficiency, a hypothesis we rigorously examine through extensive experimentation. Our study presents findings from over 34 CPU-years of comprehensive testing, evaluating five distinct distillation methodologies and their effectiveness in uncovering vulnerabilities within production-grade software systems.

1. Everyone has that one movie or series they're obsessed with—what's yours? For me, it's the *Evil Dead* series (in case it wasn't obvious). I even have one of the largest collections of memorabilia from those films.

Even nature's most unpleasant creatures hold secrets, yet who would welcome a swarm of cockroaches approaching? However, one of the key differences between natural and human technologies lies in resilience. Robust systems remain stable in unfamiliar and challenging environments. Remarkably, cockroaches can self-right while running over uneven terrain. When we added a jet pack or subjected them to an earthquake, we discovered that their exquisitely tuned limbs allowed them to self-right without expending any mental energy. They can effortlessly navigate complex terrain, such as grass, without becoming unstable. In fact, we observed a new behavior where, due to their shape, they automatically roll to the side to navigate this artificial test section of grass. Robust systems can perform multiple tasks with the same basic structure. Here's a new behavior we've discovered.

This game offers stunning 4k resolution at 60fps with a GTX 970, includes dual audio options, and comes with Neptune gear—what's not to like? Check out the video here: https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=nygsb617tmo. It’s an excellent port and a perfect starting point for newcomers to the series.
