We need to establish a comprehensive weather service for water, but until we collectively demand accountability, the incentives to fund it will not be in place. The first time I spoke at a conference was here at TED, eight years ago. Fresh out of graduate school, I had no idea that those few minutes on stage would frame the questions I would be asked for the next decade. Like many 20-somethings, I naively expected to solve the world's problems – specifically, the world's water problems – with my technology. I had a lot to learn. It was a seductive idea to believe that our biggest water quality problems persist because they're so difficult to identify. I presumed that all we needed were simpler, faster, and more affordable sensors. I was mistaken.

Summer television used to be a barren landscape of reruns, but now there are two exceptional new shows that have captivated my attention. I've been watching both obsessively for the past week. One show stands out for its audacious concept, despite having no apparent LGBTQ+ representation. Hairstylist Jonathan Antin aims to expand his successful West Hollywood salon to Beverly Hills, but it's his bombastic declaration that caught my attention. While flipping through channels, I stumbled upon Jonathan, looking equally self-absorbed and uninspired, standing in an empty space on Rodeo Drive, proclaiming in a public voice, 'I'm going to build here the greatest salon in the history of the world!' I was hooked. Not since Rocco have I found a character so infuriatingly fascinating to watch.

A woman in the United States has filed a lawsuit against Hewlett-Packard (HP), claiming that its printer ink cartridges are designed to expire on a set date. The plaintiff, a resident of Georgia, alleges that a chip within the cartridge signals the printer to request a refill even when it is not necessary. The legal action aims to include all U.S. consumers who have purchased an HP inkjet printer since February 2001. HP, the leading printer manufacturer globally, has declined to comment on the case. The company’s ink cartridges employ chip technology to detect low ink levels and inform users when a change is needed. However, the lawsuit asserts that these chips also deactivate the cartridges at a predetermined time, irrespective of their ink status.

Podcasts mark the rise of DIY radio with digital music players, which can hold up to 10,000 songs, offering a vast space to fill. With more and more iPod owners filling that space with audio content created by an eclectic assortment of producers, podcasting has emerged as a popular trend. Its strongest proponent is former MTV host and VJ (video jockey) Adam Curry. Podcasting takes its name from the Apple iPod, although you don't need an iPod to create one or listen to a podcast. Essentially, a podcast is an internet-based radio show, which podcasters create in the comfort of their own homes. They require only a microphone, a PC, and some editing software. They then upload their shows to the internet, allowing others to download and listen to them, all for free.

I can't think of anyone who has only one interest in life, and that's all they want to do for the rest of their life. Around 15% of American workers don't have traditional full-time jobs; they're half-time, part-time, contract workers or temps. The term "side hustle" seems to fit with this ethos, where people are piecing together a few different things to make a living. The word "side hustle" has its roots in popular African American newspapers. By the 1950s, these papers used the term "hustle" to refer to some kind of scam, but by the 1950s, they were also using "side hustle" to refer to legitimate work. A side hustle is distinct from a second job, which is often about necessity.

Prince was crowned the "top music earner" of 2004, surpassing renowned artists like Madonna and Elton John in *Rolling Stone* magazine's annual ranking. The iconic singer amassed $56.5 million (£30.4 million) from concert revenues, album sales, and publishing royalties, driven by his successful *Musicology* tour and album release. Madonna secured second place with earnings of $54.9 million (£29.5 million) during her *Re-Invention* world tour. Meanwhile, veteran duo Simon & Garfunkel claimed 10th place, generating $24.9 million (£13.4 million) from their much-anticipated comeback tour.  

*Rolling Stone* noted, "Prince reclaimed the spotlight after a decade on the commercial sidelines." His 2004 tour alone grossed $90.3 million (£48.5 million) in ticket sales, and his latest album, *Musicology*, sold an impressive 1.9 million copies.

Upon initial registration, a new user typically lacks a digital footprint on a website, characterized by an absence of interactions with items, a non-existent user profile, and no established social connections with other users. Despite this void of information, it is crucial for recommender systems to captivate these users from the outset, encouraging them to remain on the platform and evolve into active participants. This challenge falls under the category of new user cold-start, a critical issue that can significantly impact a company's growth and sustainability. Current approaches to addressing user cold-start recommendations often necessitate additional user engagement, such as participating in an interview process, or rely on supplementary information, including demographic data, geographical location, and social relationships. However, users may be reluctant to invest time in such processes, and supplementary information is frequently unavailable for cold-start users, rendering these approaches ineffective.

A U.S. woman is suing Hewlett Packard (HP), claiming its printer ink cartridges are secretly programmed to expire on a certain date. The unnamed woman from Georgia alleges that a chip inside the cartridge signals the printer to refill even when unnecessary. The lawsuit aims to represent anyone in the U.S. who has purchased an HP inkjet printer since February 2001. HP, the world's largest printer company, declined to comment on the lawsuit. HP ink cartridges use chip technology to detect low ink levels and prompt users to replace them. However, the suit argues the chips also deactivate the cartridges on a preset date, regardless of whether they are empty.

Tottenham Sporting Director Frank Arnesen has firmly dismissed rumors of a personal conflict with former coach Jacques Santini being the reason for his abrupt resignation. Recent media reports had suggested Santini felt his authority was being undermined by Arnesen's influence at White Hart Lane.  

Speaking to BBC Radio Five Live, Arnesen stated, "These claims are completely unfounded. Jacques resigned solely due to private matters—it had nothing to do with any professional disagreements. He personally assured me that his decision was entirely his own."  

When pressed for details on Santini's sudden departure after only 13 matches in charge, Arnesen admitted he had no further insight. "Jacques never disclosed the specifics, and I respect his privacy. We must accept his decision with understanding and dignity," he added.

Spontaneous conversations in natural settings, such as those captured in child-centered recordings, are among the most challenging types of audio to process. However, developing speech processing models capable of handling such diverse conditions would be highly valuable for language acquisition research, where understanding both the quantity and quality of speech children hear and produce is crucial. Such models also hold promise for early diagnosis and assessing remediation outcomes. In this paper, we outline our approach to creating an open-source neural network designed to classify audio segments into four categories: vocalizations by the child wearing the recording device, vocalizations by other children, adult male speech, and adult female speech. To achieve this, we compiled diverse child-centered corpora totaling 260 hours of recordings across 10 languages.

Embark on an extraordinary journey with me to the Rosetta spacecraft, a mission that has consumed my passion for the past two years. To successfully escort and land the probe on a comet, I must first take you on a journey through the origin of our solar system. Let us travel back in time to approximately 4.5 billion years ago, when a vast cloud of gas and dust existed. At the heart of this cloud, our sun took shape and ignited, accompanied by the formation of what we now know as planets, comets, and asteroids. As the Earth cooled down after its formation, comets collided with our planet in a massive impact, delivering water and potentially even complex organic material to our world. This may have played a crucial role in the emergence of life on Earth, a theory that has far-reaching implications for our understanding of the origins of life on our planet.

The estimation of the intrinsic dimension of a dataset is a fundamental step in most dimensionality reduction techniques. This article introduces *intrinsic*, an R package that implements novel, state-of-the-art likelihood-based estimators of the intrinsic dimension of a dataset. Specifically, the methods included in this package are the *two-nn*, *gride*, and *hidalgo* models. To make these new estimators easily accessible, the package provides a few high-level, intuitive functions built upon a broader set of efficient, low-level routines. *Intrinsic* includes models that fall into two categories: homogeneous and heterogeneous intrinsic dimension estimators. The first category comprises the *two-nn* and *gride* models. The functions dedicated to these methods perform inference under both frequentist and Bayesian frameworks. The second category includes *hidalgo*, a Bayesian mixture model for which an efficient Gibbs sampler is implemented.

My journey to become a polar specialist, photographing and specializing in the polar regions, began when I was four years old, when my family moved from southern Canada to northern Baffin Island, near Greenland. There we lived with the Inuit in a tiny community of 200 people, where we were one of three non-Inuit families. In this community, we didn’t have a television, computers, or even a radio. We didn’t even have a telephone. All of my time was spent outside with the Inuit, playing. The snow and ice were my sandbox, and the Inuit were my teachers. That’s where I became truly obsessed with this polar realm.

Low-symmetry 2D materials—such as ReS$_2$ and ReSe$_2$ monolayers, black phosphorus monolayers, group-IV monochalcogenide monolayers, borophene, among others—have more complex atomistic structures than the honeycomb lattices of graphene, hexagonal boron nitride, and transition metal dichalcogenides. The reduced symmetries of these emerging materials give rise to inhomogeneous electron, optical, valley, and spin responses, as well as entirely new properties such as ferroelasticity, ferroelectricity, magnetism, spin-wave phenomena, large nonlinear optical properties, photogalvanic effects, and superconductivity. Novel electronic topological properties, nonlinear elastic properties, and structural phase transformations can also occur due to low symmetry. The 'Beyond Graphene: Low-Symmetry and Anisotropic 2D Materials' special topic was assembled to highlight recent experimental and theoretical research on these emerging materials.

Optomechanics and electromechanics have enabled the creation of macroscopic mechanical oscillators in their quantum ground states, in quadrature squeezed states, and in entangled states of motion. This breakthrough has paved the way for the development of novel ideas for harnessing mechanical oscillators in the processing and communication of quantum information, as well as precision force sensors operating beyond the standard quantum limit. However, the linear interaction between motion and light or electricity restricts access to the broader class of quantum states of motion, including cat states or energy-squeezed states. Recognizing this limitation, researchers have proposed the possibility of circumventing it by creating strong quadratic coupling between motion and light. Experimental demonstrations of quadratically coupled optomechanical systems have indeed been achieved, but these have not yet accessed non-classical states of motion. Nonetheless, this research has opened up new avenues for the exploration of quantum mechanics and its potential applications.

Here, we report the observation of thermally driven transverse vibrations in a photonic crystal waveguide, comprising two parallel nanobeams separated by a 240 nm vacuum gap. Theoretical models are developed and validated to describe the transduction of beam motion into phase and amplitude modulation of a weak optical probe propagating in a guided mode of the waveguide for probe frequencies both near and far from the dielectric band edge. This research provides a foundation for evaluating the potential detrimental effects of thermal motion on optical atomic traps near the surfaces of photonic crystal waveguides, which have been designed for near-field atom trapping. Ultimately, our long-term goal is to establish strong atom-mediated links between individual phonons of vibration and single photons propagating in the guided modes of the waveguide, thereby enabling optomechanics at the quantum level, harnessing the synergy between atoms, photons, and phonons.

We're seen as the organization that serves as the last resort for failed social policies. I can't define who comes to us or how long they stay. Podcasting has revolutionized the way people consume digital music, allowing individuals to store up to 10,000 songs on their Apple iPod or other digital music players. However, podcasting has also led to an explosion of creative content, with an unpredictable array of producers creating audio content. The term "podcasting" originated from the Apple iPod, although it is not necessary to own one to create or listen to a podcast. Essentially, a podcast is an internet-based radio show, where podcasters can produce their own shows from the comfort of their own homes. All they require is a microphone, a PC, and some editing software. They then upload their shows to the internet, making them accessible for free download and listening by others.

The assembly history of the Milky Way is currently being unveiled, thanks to the data provided by the Gaia mission. It is likely that the globular cluster system of our galaxy has followed a similarly intricate formation path. To constrain this formation path, we explore the link between the globular clusters and the known merging events that the Milky Way has experienced. To this end, we combined the kinematic information provided by Gaia for almost all galactic clusters with the largest available sample of cluster ages, carefully correcting for systematic errors. To identify clusters with a common origin, we analyzed their dynamical properties, particularly in the space of integrals of motion. We find that about 40% of the clusters likely formed in situ.

Subject: Urgent: Impact of ISO Rule Changes on PX Day Ahead Auction

To: All PX Market Participants  
From: John Yurkanin, Chief Operating Officer  
Date: Monday, December 11, 2000

I am writing to address a critical issue affecting the PX Day Ahead Auction caused by recent rule changes implemented by the California ISO. The emergency tariff filing submitted last Friday has disrupted the continuity of our market operations. The introduction of both soft and hard caps has created a significant disconnect, posing unacceptable risks for all participants. Contrary to the ISO's intentions of decreasing real-time energy sales and procurement, this approach is likely to drive suppliers towards the more lucrative pricing opportunities offered by the ISO. In response, the PX is actively petitioning Terry Winter of the ISO to permit immediate submission of adjustment bids at any relevant price for already submitted schedules. We believe this is necessary to restore stability and ensure competitive market dynamics.

Yukos heading back to US courts, Russian oil and gas company Yukos is due in a US court on Thursday as it continues to fight for its survival. The firm is in the process of being broken up by Russian authorities to pay a $27 billion ($14 billion) tax bill. Yukos filed for bankruptcy in the US, hoping to use international business law to halt the forced sale of its key oil production unit, Yurganskneftegas. The unit was, however, sold for $9.4 billion to state oil firm Rosneft but only after the state auction had been disrupted. Yukos lawyers now say the auction violated US bankruptcy law. The company and its main shareholders have vowed to go after any company that buys its assets, using all and every legal means.

Causal inference from observational data is a key objective in many data analyses within the health and social sciences. Yet, academic statistics has often been critical of data analyses aimed at establishing causality. The emergence of the term "data science" offers a historic chance to redefine data analysis in a way that naturally incorporates causal inference from observational data. As others have done before, we categorize the scientific contributions of data science into three types of tasks: description, prediction, and counterfactual prediction (which includes causal inference). Clearly classifying these data science tasks is essential for discussing the data, assumptions, and analytical methods required to effectively achieve each task. We contend that the failure to clearly outline the role of subject-matter expertise in data analysis leads to widespread misunderstandings about data science.

A group of MPs has intensified pressure on Tony Blair regarding House of Lords reform by publishing a detailed blueprint for change. The cross-party coalition unveiled a draft bill proposing a smaller second chamber, with 70% of its members elected. Since 1999, when 600 hereditary peers lost their seats, MPs and peers have struggled to reach consensus on reform. The group claims it can secure support to remove the remaining 92 hereditary peers. The government previously delayed plans to eliminate hereditary peers, citing insufficient support due to opposition in the Lords. Tony Blair has emphasized the need for consensus on reforms. There are indications that Labour's manifesto may include proposals to alter at least the powers of the Lords.

Where does the carbon dioxide we release go? Carbon plays a crucial role in a natural cycle, present in all living organisms  in the soil, in the oceans, in the atmosphere, and in rocks deep beneath the Earth's surface. When plants and other living organisms die, a significant portion of their stored carbon is released back to the atmosphere, where it is absorbed by new plants and microorganisms, perpetuating the cycle. However, over millions of years, some of the carbon stored in ancient trees, sea life, and other organisms becomes trapped in rock formations, eventually transforming into fossil fuels  coal, oil, and natural gas. Natural geological events, such as volcanic eruptions, release some of this trapped carbon, while human activities, like the burning of fossil fuels, also release a significant amount of prehistoric carbon dioxide. In fact, human activities today release approximately 60 times more carbon dioxide than all the volcanic eruptions on the planet each year, highlighting the profound impact of human activities on the global carbon cycle.

We establish natural splittings for the values of global Mackey functors at orthogonal, unitary, and symplectic groups. In particular, the restriction homomorphisms between the orthogonal, unitary, and symplectic groups of adjacent dimensions are naturally split epimorphisms. The interest in these splittings stems from equivariant stable homotopy theory, as the equivariant stable homotopy groups of every global spectrum form a global Mackey functor. Consequently, the splittings imply that certain long exact homotopy group sequences decompose into short exact sequences. For the real and complex global Thom spectra $\mathbf{mo}$ and $\mathbf{mu}$, the splittings imply the regularity of various Euler classes related to the tautological representations of $O(n)$ and $U(n)$.

Baryonic feedback effects result in a suppression of the weak lensing angular power spectrum on small scales. The insufficiently constrained shape and amplitude of this suppression represent a significant source of uncertainty for forthcoming cosmological weak lensing surveys like Euclid or LSST. In this first paper of a two-part series, we employ simulations to construct a Euclid-like tomographic mock dataset for the cosmic shear power spectrum and its corresponding covariance matrix, both adjusted for baryonic effects using the baryonification approach of Schneider et al. (2019). Furthermore, we develop an emulator to rapidly predict the baryonic power suppression, enabling us to conduct a likelihood inference analysis for a standard ΛCDM cosmology that incorporates both cosmological and astrophysical parameters.

Optomechanics and electromechanics have enabled the preparation of macroscopic mechanical oscillators in their quantum ground states, quadrature-squeezed states, and entangled states of motion. Beyond pushing increasingly larger and more tangible objects into the quantum regime, this capability has spurred ideas for using mechanical oscillators in quantum information processing and communication, as well as for precision force sensors operating beyond the standard quantum limit. However, the effectively linear interaction between motion and light or electricity restricts access to broader classes of quantum states of motion, such as cat states or energy-squeezed states. Early optomechanical proposals highlighted the possibility of overcoming this limitation by establishing strong quadratic coupling between motion and light. While experimental demonstrations of quadratically coupled optomechanical systems exist, they have not yet achieved nonclassical states of motion.

Do you ever have one of those moments when you begin to reflect on life? Like you're sitting in math class bored out of your skull, and you wonder what exactly goes on in your brain that makes you understand math? And you realize that you exist and you are in math class and you wonder why. Then you realize how amazing existing is. And suddenly it hits you—the fact that you are going off on this random train of thought is incredibly pathetic. Ever had that happen? I hate that. You know what else I hate? Block schedules. I want to tell you about the future of money. Let's start with a story about a culture that lived in Micronesia in the early 1900s, called the Yap. Now, I want to tell you about the Yap because their form of money is really interesting. They used these limestone discs called rai stones. Now, the Yap didn't actually move these rai stones around or exchange them the way we do with our coins, because rai stones could get to be pretty massive. Warframe is an enjoyable game for the initial few hours of play. However, after those hours, you'll likely find that the game's offerings become repetitive, with the combat, enemies, and gameplay becoming the primary focus. The one notable highlight is that the developers are dedicated to regularly releasing updates, which often include significant changes, new Warframes, and other improvements. While it's a decent game to play casually, it may not hold your interest beyond a few months unless you're a dedicated grinder. On the plus side, the game is free, so I recommend giving it a try. If you're looking for a quick fix, it's definitely worth a shot.

Last night in class, we discussed that week’s assigned reading, "Getting Back Into Place: Toward a Renewed Study of the Place-World" by Edward S. Casey. The first chapter addresses “implacement” and the second, “displacement.” In the first chapter, Casey argues that our contemporary world and culture revolve around time as the situating force: we are running out of time, we have saved time, we are just in time. He suggests that centering culture around place creates a more grounded community. (This is a very rough summation, just a disclaimer in case my professor or any classmates read this post.) So far, this is the first book that has truly grabbed my attention.

Spirit Awards hail 'Sideways' as the comedy has dominated this year's Independent Spirit Awards, winning all six of the awards for which it was nominated. It was named Best Film, while Alexander Payne won Best Director and Best Screenplay, along with writing partner Jim Taylor. It also won acting awards for stars Paul Giamatti, Thomas Haden Church, and Virginia Madsen. 'Sideways' is tipped to do well at Sunday's Oscars, with five nominations. The awards, now in their 20th year, are given to films made outside the traditional studio system and are traditionally held the day before the Oscars. Other winners included Catalina Sandino Moreno, who took Best Actress for her role as a drug smuggler in the Colombian drama 'Maria Full of Grace.' Moreno is also nominated for Best Actress at the Oscars. The Best First Screenplay award went to Joshua Marston for 'Maria Full of Grace.'

The direpack package aims to provide a set of modern statistical dimension reduction techniques as a single, consistent package in Python. The included methods fall into three categories: projection pursuit-based dimension reduction, sufficient dimension reduction, and robust M estimators for dimension reduction. In addition, regularized regression estimators based on these reduced dimension spaces are provided, ranging from classical principal component regression to sparse partial robust M regression. The package also includes a variety of pre-processing utilities, such as generalized spatial signs, as well as dedicated plotting functionality and cross-validation utilities. Furthermore, direpack has been designed to be consistent with the scikit-learn API, making it easy to incorporate the included estimators into statistical and/or machine learning pipelines.

The reboot of the Devil May Cry franchise has brought about numerous changes, particularly for the series' protagonist, Dante. While some fans of the original series may be dismayed by these alterations, it's undeniable that they've improved the character. This new game by Ninja Theory demonstrates just that. DMC serves as the perfect entry point for newcomers to the series, offering a detailed backstory for Dante's transformation from an arrogant, cynical character to a selfless hero. The game skillfully weaves a compelling narrative arc for the new Dante, making him an engaging character to follow.

As the story progresses, we see Dante's introduction into a corrupt world he willfully chose to ignore, and we witness his journey from cynicism to selflessness. Throughout, the game provides a clear and captivating narrative, making it easy for newcomers to become invested in the character's growth and development.

Prince crowned 'top music earner'. Prince earned more than any other pop star in 2004, beating artists such as Madonna and Elton John in US magazine Rolling Stone's annual list. The singer banked $56.5m (£30.4m) from concerts, album, and publishing sales with his Musicology tour and album. He kept Madonna in second place, as she earned $54.9m (£29.5m) while embarking on her global Re-Invention tour. Veterans Simon and Garfunkel were in 10th place, their comeback tour helping them earn $24.9m (£13.4m) last year. "Prince returned to centre stage after a decade in the commercial wilderness," the magazine reported. The singer's 2004 tour took $90.3m (£48.5m) in ticket sales, and he sold 1.9 million copies of his latest album, Musicology.

Hearts secured a convincing 2-1 victory over Livingston in their Scottish Cup quarter-final tie, thanks to a blistering start that saw them score two goals within the opening 10 minutes. The match got off to a dream start for the home side when Lee Miller rose above the Livingston defence to head home a Jamie McAllister free kick, expertly floated into the box, in the first minute. The set piece was awarded after Gus Bahoken clumsily fouled Miller 35 yards from goal, and Miller made the most of the opportunity, looping his header over the outstretched Colin Meldrum from 10 yards out. Hearts doubled their advantage just nine minutes later, as Saul Mikoliunas' pace down the right flank allowed him to deliver a pinpoint cross to the back post, where McAllister timed his run to perfection, meeting the ball with a clinical header that left Meldrum with no chance. Although Craig Easton pulled a goal back for the visitors with a contentious header after an hour, Hearts held firm to book their place in the next round.

The concept of bioprinting, also known as bioprinting technology or biomanufacturing, is a relatively new field that involves the use of 3D printing techniques to create living tissue structures. The goal of bioprinting is to create functional, three-dimensional tissue structures that can be used to replace or repair damaged organs in the human body.
The idea behind bioprinting is to use 3D printing techniques to create a scaffold or matrix of biological material, such as collagen or gelatin, that can be used to support the growth of cells. The cells are then introduced to the scaffold and allowed to grow and differentiate, eventually forming a fully functional organ.
Bioprinting has the potential to revolutionize the field of organ transplantation by providing a virtually unlimited supply of organs for transplantation. Currently, there is a severe shortage of donor organs available for transplantation, resulting in long waiting lists and many people dying while waiting for a transplant. Bioprinting could potentially overcome this problem by allowing for the creation of custom-made organs that can be grown to order.
Bioprinting is still in its infancy, and there are many technical and ethical challenges that must be overcome before it

Charles Dickens' indelible mark on literature remains unrivaled, even a century after his passing. His iconic characters – the impoverished orphan yearning for a meager second helping, the forlorn spinster shrouded in the tattered remnants of her wedding finery, and the miser haunted by the specter of Christmases past – continue to captivate audiences with their haunting familiarity. The profound impact of his literary legacy has given rise to a distinct adjective, a testament to the enduring power of his craft. So, what are the defining characteristics that set Dickens' writing apart? His masterful storytelling is distinguished by an atmosphere of foreboding, clever plot twists, and intriguing enigmas, all of which combine to create an irresistible sense of anticipation. This narrative alchemy proved irresistible to his original audience, who eagerly devoured each installment of his serialized tales, initially published in affordable literary journals before being compiled into book form.

According to a UK think-tank report, a quarter of broadband users in Britain regularly upload content and maintain personal websites, with more than five million households in the UK having broadband connections and that number growing rapidly. The report highlights how having an always-on, fast internet connection is transforming the way people use the internet. More than half of those with broadband log on to the web before breakfast, while one in five admit to getting up in the middle of the night to browse the web. Significantly, the report suggests that broadband is empowering people to take a more active role online.

An electron is typically considered to possess only one type of kinetic energy, but could it have more, due to its spin and charge, or by exciting other electrons? In one dimension, the physics of interacting electrons is well-captured at low energies by the Tomonaga-Luttinger-Liquid (TLL) model, yet little has been observed experimentally beyond this linear regime. Here, we report on measurements of many-body modes in 1D gated-wires using a tunnelling spectroscopy technique. We observe two separate Fermi seas at high energies, associated with spin and charge excitations, together with the emergence of three additional 1D'replica' modes that strengthen with decreasing wire length. The effective interaction strength in the wires is varied by changing the amount of 1D inter-subband screening by over 45%.

Hearts secured their spot in the Scottish Cup quarter-final by overcoming Livingston with a 2-1 victory, thanks to their remarkable start in the opening 10 minutes. Lee Miller drew first blood within the first minute, expertly heading Jamie McAllister's free kick over Colin Meldrum into the net. McAllister quickly followed up, personally adding to the score by meeting Saul Mikoliunas' cross at the far post and sealing the game early on for the hosts. Although Craig Easton netted a controversial goal for Livingston after an hour, Hearts maintained their lead until the final whistle. The first goal was set up following a mistake by Gus Bahoken, who fouled Miller 35 yards from goal. McAllister's deft free kick found its way to an unguarded Miller, who leapt above the static Livingston defense to score from 10 yards out. Just ten minutes into the match, Hearts extended their advantage as Mikoliunas dashed down the right flank and delivered a precise cross, allowing McAllister to head past Meldrum.

When working with survey data, it is important to account for the random effects that can occur in the sampling process. Traditional methods of estimation, such as maximum likelihood estimation, can be biased due to the use of individual survey weights that are constructed to be inversely proportional to the unit survey inclusion probabilities. This can lead to unbiased inference only in the large sample limit. In contrast, Bayesian methods can be used to achieve unbiased inference by using weight-exponentiated forms of the random effects distribution. In this paper, we propose a Bayesian approach that constructs weight-exponentiated forms of the random effects distribution to achieve unbiased inference for generating hyperparameters of the random effects. We compare our approach with frequentist methods that rely on numerical integration and show that only the Bayesian method achieves both unbiased estimation with respect to the sampling design distribution and consistency with respect to the population generating distribution. Our simulations and real data example demonstrate the utility of our approach across different modeling formulations and sampling designs. This work serves as a capstone for recent developments that combine traditional survey estimation approaches with the Bayesian modeling paradigm and provides a bridge between the two rich but disparate sub-fields.

Cardinal Cormac Murphy-O'Connor has criticised the billions of pounds spent on the Iraq war and conflicts in the Middle East, stating that the funds could have been better utilised to alleviate poverty. The head of the Catholic Church in England and Wales expressed his views on BBC Radio 4 and is expected to reiterate his stance during his Christmas midnight mass. In a Christmas message, the cardinal denounced the Iraq war as a "terrible" waste of resources, echoing sentiments previously shared by the Archbishop of Canterbury. During his appearance on BBC Radio 4's Thought for the Day, he lamented the fact that vast sums have been spent on war, rather than being invested in initiatives to lift people out of dire poverty, malnourishment, and disease. The cardinal urged that 2005 should be a year of concerted effort to "make history poverty".

Allan Scott is feeling optimistic about securing a medal at next week's European Indoor Championships following a promising start on the international scene. The 22-year-old from East Kilbride finished fourth in the 60m hurdles at the Jose M. Cagigal Memorial Meeting in Madrid. "It was a valuable learning experience, and I'm definitely not ruling out the possibility of competing for a medal next week," Scott stated. The race was won by Felipe Vivancos, who matched the Spanish record, while Sweden's Robert Kronberg came in second, and Haiti's Dudley Dorival secured third place. Although Scott was slightly disappointed with his performance in the final, he had previously won his heat with a time of 7.64 seconds but ran 0.04 seconds slower in his first IAAF Indoor Grand Prix Circuit final.

Imagine being able to connect your mind to a device that promises endless pleasure for the rest of your life. If presented with this opportunity, would you take it? This is the intriguing question posed by philosopher Robert Nozick in his thought experiment known as the Experience Machine. This scenario invites us to envision a world where scientists have created a machine that replicates real life while ensuring perpetual pleasure without any suffering. The trade-off? You must abandon reality permanently, although you would scarcely notice the difference. Your experiences would feel indistinguishable from real life. The natural highs and lows of life would be replaced with a never-ending series of highs. Tempting, isn't it?

Tories outlining policing plans: local communities would be asked to go to the polls to elect their own area police commissioner, under plans unveiled by the Conservatives. Party leader Michael Howard said the new role would replace 'inconspicuous' police authorities. He said the new office would not supersede the job of a chief constable. The Lib Dems said the plan could let extreme groups run policing, while Labour criticised 'extravagant' Tory promises on policing. Responding to the plans, the chairman of the Police Federation of England and Wales, which represents rank-and-file officers, said it was essential operational independence was retained. Jan Berry said: 'It is a service, not a political football to be kicked around every time an election approaches. These plans could result in those with extreme political views dictating what actually happens on the ground,' she warned.

Beyond Earth, the latest installment in the Sid Meier's Civilization series, is a turn-based game where you guide your civilization's evolution to achieve a specific objective or 'victory'. Unlike previous games in the series, which spanned the Earth's timeline through various eras such as the Stone Age, Medieval Age, and Modern Era, this game takes a bold leap into the future by setting you on a mission to colonize a new planet. I appreciate the innovative approach taken by the developers, which introduced fresh mechanics such as the Affinity System and Orbital Layer. However, I found that these new mechanics sometimes led to an overwhelming experience. To further enhance the game, I believe that incorporating updated artwork, particularly the environmental elements encountered during interactions with other leaders, could greatly improve the overall gameplay experience.

Spintronic nanodevices exhibit ultrafast nonlinear dynamic and recurrence behaviors on a nanosecond scale, which promises to enable spintronic reservoir computing (RC) systems. Here, two physical RC systems based on a single magnetic skyrmion memristor (MSM) and 24 spin-torque nano-oscillators (STNOs) were proposed and modeled to process image classification tasks and nonlinear dynamic system predictions, respectively. Based on our micromagnetic simulation results of the nonlinear responses of MSM and STNO to current pulse stimulation, the handwritten digit recognition task demonstrates that an RC system using a single MSM achieves outstanding performance in image classification. Additionally, complex unknown nonlinear dynamic problems can also be effectively solved by a physical RC system composed of 24 STNOs, as confirmed in second-order nonlinear dynamic system and NARMA10 tasks.

Roxy Music to Headline Wight Music Festival
 .
Roxy Music has been confirmed as the headliner for the Wight Music Festival, which will take place from June 10-12. The original band members - Bryan Ferry, Phil Manzanera, Andy Mackay, and Paul Thompson - will take to the stage on the Saturday night of the festival. The band is also working on new material, their first since the 1982 album Avalon.
Roxy Music was formed in 1971 by Ferry, Mackay, and Brian Eno, with Manzanera joining the following year. Their first hit was 1972's "Virginia Plain," with other hits including "Street Life," "Love Is the Drug," and the 1981 John Lennon cover, "Jealous Guy.

What is a parent? What is a parent? It's not an easy question. Today we have adoption, stepfamilies, surrogate mothers. Many parents face tough questions and tough decisions. Shall we tell our child about the sperm donation? If so, when? What words to use? Sperm donors are often referred to as "biological fathers," but should we really be using the word "father"? As a philosopher and social scientist, I have been studying these questions about the concept of parenthood. But today, I will talk to you about what I learned from talking to parents and children. I will show you that they know what matters most in a family, even though their family looks a little different.

These are undeniably grim economic times, my fellow TEDsters—grim indeed. Yet I’d like to lift your spirits by sharing one of the most remarkable, if largely overlooked, commercial success stories of the past two decades. In its own peculiar way, it rivals the achievements of giants like Microsoft or Google. Moreover, this is an industry that has weathered the current recession with remarkable composure. I’m speaking, of course, about organized crime.  

Now, you might argue that organized crime is nothing new—and you’d be absolutely right. But over the last 20 years, it has undergone an unprecedented expansion, now accounting for roughly 15 percent of global GDP. I like to refer to it as the global shadow economy—or, more succinctly, McMafia.  

So, what fueled this extraordinary surge in cross-border crime?

The orientational relaxation of molecules is a process in which the molecules in a liquid or solid change their orientation with respect to their surroundings. This can occur due to various factors, such as thermal fluctuations, pressure, or the movement of the molecules themselves. In a temperature range of about fifty degrees, the relaxation of the nematic-isotropic transition can be presented by a sum of several exponential processes, two of which play a decisive role. These main processes replace each other in a temperature range of about fifty degrees. Dielectric spectroscopy also made it possible to distinguish two processes of orientational relaxation: the first is due to rotation of the side mesogenic groups, and the second is associated with motion of the main chain segments.

This paper introduces a millimeter-scale CMOS 64$\times$64 single charged particle radiation detector system designed for external beam cancer radiotherapy. A 1$\times$1 $\mu \text{m}^2$ diode is used to measure energy deposition by a single charged particle in the depletion region, and the array design achieves a substantial detection area of 512$\times$512 $\mu \text{m}^2$. Instead of detecting the voltage drop induced by radiation, the proposed system measures the pulse width—essentially, the duration it takes for the voltage to return to its baseline. This approach eliminates the need for power-intensive and sizable analog-to-digital converters. A prototype ASIC is fabricated using the TSMC 65 nm LP CMOS process, consuming an average static power of 0.535 mW under a 1.2 V analog and digital power supply. The system's functionality has been successfully verified in a clinical 67.5 MeV proton beam setting.

My path to becoming a polar specialist, focusing on photographing and studying the polar regions, began when I was just four years old. This journey started when my family relocated from southern Canada to the northern end of Baffin Island, near Greenland. We settled in a small Inuit community consisting of only 200 people, where ours was one of just three non-Inuit families. In this remote village, we lived without the conveniences of television, computers, or even a radio. We didn't have a telephone either. I spent all my time outdoors with the Inuit, learning and playing. The snow and ice became my playground, and the Inuit were my mentors. This unique experience sparked my lifelong fascination with the polar world.

In this paper, we examine the landscape of the negative log-likelihood function of Gaussian mixture models with a general number of components. Due to non-convexity, multiple local minima exist that are not globally optimal, even when the mixture is well-separated. We demonstrate that all local minima share the same form of structure, which partially identifies the component centers of the true mixture. Each local minimum involves a non-overlapping combination of fitting multiple Gaussian distributions to a single true component and fitting a single Gaussian to multiple true components. Our results apply to the setting where the true mixture components satisfy a certain separation condition, and are valid even when the number of components is over- or under-specified. For Gaussian mixtures with three components, we obtain sharper results in terms of the scaling between the components.

Wales 'must learn health lessons'. The new health minister for Wales says there are lessons to learn from England in tackling waiting lists. Dr Brian Gibbons, on his first full day in the job after Jane Hutt was sacked, admitted "big challenges" but insisted the "essentials" were in place. But both Plaid Cymru and the Liberal Democrats said Dr Gibbons needed to change policy. Meanwhile, Ms Hutt defended her record, saying waiting times and lists were "only 10% of the health agenda". Dr Gibbons, who was a GP in Blaengwynfi, in the Upper Afan Valley, before becoming AM for Aberavon, said NHS staff wanted a period of consolidation after "tremendous change and reform". One of the biggest problems that had faced Ms Hutt during her five-and-a-half years as the assembly's first health minister was the length of waiting lists in Wales.

Even the most unappealing creatures in nature hold valuable secrets. Take cockroaches, for instance—despite their off-putting reputation, they possess fascinating capabilities that highlight a profound contrast between natural and human-made technologies: robustness. Robust systems excel in maintaining stability across complex and unfamiliar environments. Astonishingly, cockroaches demonstrate the ability to self-stabilize while traversing uneven terrain. When we equipped them with a jet pack or introduced a disturbance akin to an earthquake, we found that their exquisitely designed legs enable them to maintain balance without engaging any cognitive processes. They effortlessly navigate challenging landscapes, such as grass, without becoming destabilized. Additionally, we observed a novel behavior facilitated by their unique shape, which allows them to naturally roll to one side to maneuver through artificial grass obstacles. Robust systems are capable of performing a variety of tasks using the same structural framework, and this new behavior exemplifies that principle.

A significant shift is underway in the realm of home computer security, with a growing number of women and older adults taking proactive steps to safeguard their systems against malicious online threats. According to a comprehensive two-year study conducted by Preventon, a leading net security firm, there has been a notable increase in the number of women purchasing software to protect their PCs from viruses, spam, and spyware. Between 2002 and 2004, this demographic saw an average annual growth rate of 11.2% in security software purchases. The study's findings also reveal that a substantial proportion of home net security program buyers are retirees, with this segment experiencing an average annual growth rate of 13.2% over the past three years. Notably, retired women are leading the charge, with 53% of this demographic purchasing security software, outpacing their male counterparts. These trends reflect a broader evolution in the demographics and online behaviors of web users, as well as a growing awareness of the heightened risks associated with high-speed broadband internet connections.

The UK's Conservative Party has announced a comprehensive health screening initiative for immigrants, set to be implemented if they secure a victory in the upcoming general election. This strategic plan targets individuals seeking to reside and work in Britain for an extended period, specifically those hailing from non-EU countries with a high prevalence of tuberculosis. 

Key aspects of the proposal include mandatory chest X-rays and supplementary tests for individuals intending to stay six months or longer in the UK, provided they originate from countries with elevated TB rates. Furthermore, all non-EU nationals planning to stay in the country for a year or more will be required to undergo a thorough medical examination, encompassing screenings for tuberculosis, hepatitis B, and HIV.

In the event of a positive TB test result, visa applications will be automatically rejected. Conversely, cases involving other medical conditions will be assessed and addressed on an individual basis, allowing for a more nuanced and flexible approach to immigration health assessments.

Online reviews provide product evaluations for customers to make decisions. Unfortunately, these evaluations can be manipulated using fake reviews ("spams") by professional spammers, who have developed increasingly insidious and powerful spamming strategies by adapting to deployed detectors. Spamming strategies are hard to capture, as they can vary quickly over time, differ across spammers and target products, and more critically, remain unknown in most cases. Furthermore, most existing detectors focus on detection accuracy, which is not well-aligned with the goal of maintaining the trustworthiness of product evaluations. To address these challenges, we formulate a minimax game where spammers and spam detectors compete with each other on their practical goals that are not solely based on detection accuracy. Nash equilibria of the game lead to stable detectors that are agnostic to any mixed detection strategies.

"Warning Issued Over Tsunami Aid Scam Website  

Internet users are being warned to steer clear of a fraudulent website falsely claiming to collect donations for tsunami victims. The site appears credible at first glance, as it mimics an outdated version of the official Disasters Emergency Committee (DEC) webpage. However, the DEC has confirmed it has no affiliation with the fake site and has reported the matter to the police.  

This scam is just one of many attempting to exploit the public’s generosity following the tsunami disaster. The fraudulent website’s link is being spread via a spam email currently in circulation. The email’s subject line reads *"Urgent Tsunami Earthquake Appeal,"* and its content—riddled with poor grammar and spelling errors—is typical of phishing scams.

A documentary offering an unfiltered glimpse into the life of chart-topping singer George Michael will be featured at this year's Berlin Film Festival. Titled "A Different Story," the film will be screened in the Panorama section of the festival, which takes place from February 10 to 20. The documentary includes candid discussions by Michael covering both his illustrious career and personal journey, from his early years with Wham! to more recent events. George Michael will be present at the festival to introduce the screening on February 16. Accompanying him will be the film's director, Southan Morris, and executive producer, Andy Stephens. The 93-minute film delves into Michael's beginnings with Wham!, his subsequent solo career, his legal disputes with Sony, and his outspoken views on the Iraq War and American politics.

Virgin Radio offers 3G broadcast. UK broadcaster Virgin Radio says it will become the first station in the world to offer radio via 3G mobiles. The radio station, in partnership with technology firm Sydus, will broadcast on selected 2G and high-speed 3G networks. Later this year, listeners will be able to download software from the Virgin website which enables the service. James Cridland, head of new media at Virgin Radio, said: 'It places radio at the heart of the 3G revolution.' Virgin Radio will be the first station made available, followed by two digital stations, Virgin Radio Classic Rock and Virgin Radio Groove. Mr Cridland said: 'This application will enable anyone, anywhere to listen to Virgin Radio simply with the phone in their pocket.'

Voters flock to blog awards site as voting gets underway for the annual Bloggies, which recognize the best web blogs - online spaces where individuals publish their thoughts - of the year. Nominations were announced on Sunday, but the official site experienced a temporary closure due to an overwhelming influx of visitors. Weblogs have been nominated in 30 categories, ranging from the top regional blog to the best-kept-secret blog. Blogs had a remarkable year, with a top US dictionary naming "blog" the word of 2004. Technorati, a blog search engine, tracks approximately six million blogs and reports that over 12,000 are added daily. According to US research think-tank Pew Internet and American Life, a new blog is created every 5.8 seconds, although less than 40% of the total are updated at least once every two months.

Spontaneous conversations in real-world settings, such as those found in child-centered recordings, have been shown to be among the most challenging audio files to process. Nevertheless, building speech processing models that can handle such a wide variety of conditions would be particularly useful for language acquisition studies, in which researchers are interested in the quantity and quality of the speech that children hear and produce, as well as for early diagnosis and measuring the effects of remediation. In this paper, we present our approach to designing an open-source neural network to classify audio segments into vocalizations produced by the child wearing the recording device, vocalizations produced by other children, adult male speech, and adult female speech. To this end, we gathered diverse child-centered corpora, totaling 260 hours of recordings and covering 10 languages.

Two twin domes, two radically opposed design cultures. One is composed of thousands of steel parts, the other of a single silk thread. One is synthetic, the other organic. One imposes itself on the environment, the other creates it. One is designed for nature, the other is designed by nature. Michelangelo said that when he looked at raw marble, he saw a figure struggling to be free. The chisel was Michelangelo's only tool. However, living things are not chiseled; they grow. And in our smallest units of life, our cells, we carry all the information required for every other cell to function and replicate. Tools also have consequences. At least since the Industrial Revolution, the world of design has been dominated by the rigors of manufacturing and mass production.

Yukos seeks court action on the sale. Yukos will return to a US court on Wednesday to seek sanctions against Baikal Finance Group, the little-known firm that purchased its main asset. Yukos has stated it will sue Baikal and others involved in the sale of Yuganskneftegas for $20 billion in damages. Yukos' US lawyers will try to have Baikal's assets frozen after the Russian government ignored a US court order last week that blocked the sale. Baikal's background and motives for purchasing the unit remain unclear. Russian newspapers have claimed that Baikal—which bought the Yuganskneftegas production unit for $9.4 billion (261 billion roubles, £4.8 billion) on Sunday at a state-provoked auction—has strong links with Surgutneftegas, Russia's fourth-largest oil producer.

Arnesen denies rift with Santini. Tottenham sporting director Frank Arnesen has denied that coach Jacques Santini resigned because of a clash of personalities at White Hart Lane. There had been newspaper speculation that Santini felt undermined by Arnesen's role at the club.  

"It is absolutely not true," Arnesen told BBC Radio Five Live. "There is only one thing that made him resign, and that is his own personal problems. He has talked to me recently and said this matter is absolutely for himself."  

Arnesen said he was unable to shed any light on the problems that caused Santini to quit after just 13 games in charge. He added: "Jacques has never gone into exactly what it was. But I trust him in that; you have to accept it. I think we should respect it.

For two decades, I dedicated my career to the world of finance, mastering its intricacies with absolute certainty. The global economy wasn't just my profession—it was my life. While I could discuss ecological concerns or apocalyptic theories with effortless familiarity, I dismissed them as improbable. Then came 2007, a year marked by an unprecedented upheaval: the subprime crisis and the catastrophic fall of major American banks. This seismic event struck me like lightning. It sparked heated debates with my wife about my vocation and the fundamental flaws in our economic framework. Beyond the immediate turmoil, I came to a sobering realization—my convictions had been profoundly misguided, and we were steering toward catastrophe.

Anti-spam laws bite spammer hard. The net's self-declared spam king is seeking bankruptcy protection. Scott Richter, the man behind optinrealbig.com and billions of junk mail messages, said lawsuits had forced the company into Chapter 11. Optinrealbig was fighting several legal battles, most notably against Microsoft, which is pushing for millions of dollars in damages. The company said filing for Chapter 11 would help it try to resolve its legal problems but still keep trading. Listed as the third biggest spammer in the world by junk mail watchdog Spamhaus, Optinrealbig was sued in December 2003 for sending mail messages that violated anti-spam laws. The lawsuit was brought by Microsoft and New York Attorney General Eliot Spitzer, who alleged that Mr. Richter and his accomplices sent billions of spam messages through 514 compromised net addresses in 35 countries.

While not perfect, this game has been a thoroughly enjoyable experience. However, the save system and the possibility of sudden death can lead to some frustration, particularly during the early stages of the game. I experienced this firsthand when I lost my main character around the 10th hour of my 20-hour playthrough. A save feature to reload at that point would have been greatly appreciated. Fortunately, I've since discovered a third-party mod/save tool to mitigate the risk of catastrophic failures. Additionally, the aiming reticle could be slightly larger and the zoom function could be more powerful. Despite these minor drawbacks, I've thoroughly enjoyed my time with the game. As someone who doesn't typically play zombie-themed games, I've found the combination of strategy, missions, and first-person shooter/beat-em-up elements to be particularly engaging. I've found myself playing it as often as I can get away, often getting sucked into just one more mission or supply run before reluctantly shutting the game down.

A new drink limit 'would cut toll' - more lives than previously thought could be saved by cutting drink-drive limits by a third, a report says. University College London research claims that cutting the limit from 80mg of alcohol per 100ml of blood to 50mg would save 65 lives a year. The findings are being published by the Parliamentary Advisory Council for Transport Safety as MPs are due to vote on the government's Road Safety Bill. The bill includes changes to speeding fines, but not to the drink-drive limit. The research, carried out by transport expert Professor Richard Allsop, says that cutting the limit would also lead to 230 fewer serious injuries on the UK's roads compared with 2003. He estimates that this would save the country £119m a year. A previous estimate, contained in a 1998 Department of Transport document, suggested that such a move could save 50 lives a year.

MPs to lords reform plan a group of MPs has tried to raise the pressure on Tony Blair over reform to the House of Lords by publishing a detailed blueprint for change. The cross-party group has unveiled a draft bill proposing a smaller second chamber in which 70% of members would be elected. MPs and peers have failed to agree on reform since 1999 when 600 hereditaries lost their seats. The group says it can win support for removing the last 92 hereditaries. The government postponed plans to remove the remaining hereditary peers because they said they were unlikely to succeed after opposition in the Lords. Tony Blair has argued that there needs to be consensus on reforms. There have been suggestions that there will be proposals for changing at least the powers of the Lords in Labour's manifesto.

Downloads enter the US singles chart as digital music downloads are incorporated into the main US singles chart for the first time. Billboard's revamped Hot 100 chart now combines data from sales of music downloads, previously tracked on a separate download chart, with physical singles sales and airplay information. Green Day's 'Boulevard of Broken Dreams' currently holds the number two spot on Billboard's pop chart and tops the digital chart. The UK singles chart is also set to include download sales later this year. In the US, digital sales are already used to compile Billboard's Hot Digital Sales chart, and will now be merged with physical singles sales and airplay data to form the new Hot 100 chart. Additionally, Billboard's new Pop 100 chart combines airplay, digital, and physical sales, but limits its airplay information to US radio stations that play chart music.

We introduce a novel mixed-variable symplectic (MVS) integrator designed for planetary systems, capable of fully resolving close encounters. This method employs a time regularization technique that preserves the stability properties of symplectic integrators while dynamically reducing the effective step size during planetary close approaches. By utilizing a high-order MVS scheme, the algorithm maintains efficiency with large time steps in regions devoid of close encounters. We demonstrate that this approach can accurately resolve near-collisions (with separations as small as a fraction of a planetary radius) while employing the same time-step used for weakly perturbed systems, such as the Solar System. The long-term stability of the method is validated through simulations of six super-Earths undergoing strong gravitational scattering over 50 kyr. Comparisons with hybrid methods like Mercury reveal superior energy conservation at comparable computational cost.

The global demand for life-saving organ transplants far exceeds the available supply, leaving hundreds of thousands of patients in desperate need of kidneys, hearts, and livers. This critical shortage has spurred groundbreaking innovations in regenerative medicine, particularly in the emerging field of bioprinting. Imagine a future where we could engineer fully functional, personalized organs rather than relying solely on donor transplants. While we haven't yet perfected the creation of complex organs, scientists have already achieved remarkable success in bioprinting fundamental biological structures like blood vessels and tubular networks essential for nutrient delivery and waste removal. This revolutionary technology builds upon the principles of 3D printing, meticulously assembling living tissues layer by layer to construct viable biological structures with unprecedented precision.

$context$. The assembly history experienced by the Milky Way is currently being unveiled thanks to the data provided by the $Gaia$ mission. It is likely that the globular cluster system of our galaxy has followed a similarly intricate formation path. $aims$. To constrain this formation path, we explore the link between the globular clusters and the known merging events that the Milky Way has experienced. $methods$. To this end, we combined the kinematic information provided by $Gaia$ for almost all Galactic clusters, with the largest sample of cluster ages available after carefully correcting for systematic errors. To identify clusters with a common origin, we analyzed their dynamical properties, particularly in the space of integrals of motion. $results$. We find that about 40% of the clusters likely formed in situ.

I have been hesitant to get involved in some of these discussions, particularly the bits about flags, as a non-American blogger. However, since I started it, I suppose I'd better say something. Firstly, I think the idea of having a law against flag-burning is completely misguided. The moment you accord automatic reverence and protection to any symbol, you've taken a small but significant step towards totalitarianism. Symbols are fickle - they don't inherently represent anything in themselves; it's what they are perceived as meaning or made to represent that's important.

When random effects are correlated with sample design variables, the traditional approach of using individual survey weights, constructed to be inversely proportional to the unit survey inclusion probabilities, to form a pseudo-likelihood no longer yields asymptotically unbiased inference. We develop a weight-exponentiated formulation for the random effects distribution that ensures unbiased inference for generating hyperparameters of the random effects. We contrast our approach with frequentist methods that rely on numerical integration, revealing that only the Bayesian method achieves both unbiased estimation with respect to the sampling design distribution and consistency with respect to the population generating distribution. Our simulations and real-data examples for a survey of business establishments demonstrate the utility of our approach across different modeling formulations and sampling designs. This work serves as a culmination of recent developmental efforts that combine traditional survey estimation approaches with the Bayesian modeling paradigm, providing a bridge across the two rich but disparate subfields.

A US woman sues over cartridges. A US woman is suing Hewlett Packard (HP), saying its printer ink cartridges are secretly programmed to expire on a certain date. The unnamed woman from Georgia says that a chip inside the cartridge tells the printer that it needs refilling, even when it does not. The lawsuit seeks to represent anyone in the US who has purchased an HP inkjet printer since February 2001. HP, the world's biggest printer firm, declined to comment on the lawsuit. HP ink cartridges use chip technology to sense when they are low on ink and advise the user to make a change. But the suit claims the chips also shut down the cartridges at a predetermined date, regardless of whether they are empty.

This was a good weekend. I did a lot of God's green leaf, after a long time, I listened to a lot of Nevermore (something I should have started doing much earlier) and read a lot. I finished three books over the weekend - Bruce Sterling's Heavy Weather, Larry Niven's World of Ptavvs, and Octavia Butler's Parable of the Sower. Heavy Weather was a good cyberpunk romp through a near-future USA, devastated by global warming and general anarchy. While it was clever, well-paced, and altogether very convincing, I must confess that I failed to come away with any really significant sensations about the story or the characters. Not that both aren't well-constructed and vividly etched - it's a good book, but not a great one, I guess.

And there's nothing wrong with PCs – if I were a full-bore gamer with time to do such a thing, I'd certainly have a PC system for the wealth of available games. However, except for a few unique products, the huge wall of games at CompUSA starts getting repetitive after a while – at first I'm envious, then I start noticing that it's really just a lot of different flavors of the same soda...first-person shooters, mystery games, strategy games, simulations. There are so many variations of Age of Empires that it becomes comical. AOE – with orcs! AOE – with spaceships! AOE – with fuzzy wombats! It's like the endless movie trailers that begin with the line  'In a world...'.

**Redefining Parenthood in the Modern Age**  

The essence of parenthood is far from simple. In today’s world, families take many forms—adoptive homes, blended families, surrogacy arrangements—each presenting unique challenges and profound questions. How should parents navigate conversations about sperm donation? When is the right time to broach the subject, and what language best honors the child’s understanding? Terms like "biological father" spark debate: does "father" truly capture the nuance of such relationships?  

As a philosopher and social scientist, I’ve dedicated years to examining these complexities. But beyond theory, the most illuminating insights come from the voices of parents and children themselves. Their experiences reveal a universal truth: what truly binds a family isn’t its structure, but the love and commitment at its core. Today, I’ll share their wisdom—proof that even unconventional families understand what matters most.

Spintronic nanodevices exhibit remarkable nonlinear dynamics and recurrence behaviors at nanosecond timescales, making them ideal candidates for spintronic reservoir computing (RC) systems. This study presents two innovative physical RC implementations: one utilizing a single magnetic skyrmion memristor (MSM) and another employing an array of 24 spin-torque nano-oscillators (STNOs), designed for image classification and nonlinear dynamic system prediction tasks respectively.  

Our micromagnetic simulations demonstrate that when stimulated by current pulses, the MSM-based RC system achieves exceptional performance in handwritten digit recognition, showcasing its superior capability for image processing. Furthermore, the STNO-based RC system, comprising 24 oscillators, proves highly effective in addressing complex nonlinear dynamic problems, as evidenced by its successful application to second-order nonlinear systems and NARMA10 tasks. These findings highlight the significant potential of spintronic devices in advancing next-generation neuromorphic computing platforms.

We consider the connection between two constructions of the mirror partner for the Calabi-Yau orbifold. This orbifold is defined as a quotient by a suitable subgroup $g$ of the phase symmetries of the hypersurface $x_m$ in the weighted projective space, cut out by a quasi-homogeneous polynomial $w_m$. The first, Berglund-H\"ubsch-Krawitz (BHK) construction, uses another weighted projective space and the quotient of a new hypersurface $x_{m^t}$ inside it by some dual group $g^t$. In the second, Batyrev construction, the mirror partner is constructed as a hypersurface in the toric variety defined by the reflexive polytope dual to the polytope associated with the original Calabi-Yau orbifold. We provide a simple evidence of the equivalence of these two constructions.

(pauses to breathe in, then out) So, I didn't always make my living from music. For about five years after graduating from an outstanding liberal arts university, this was my day job. (laughs) I was a self-employed living statue called the eight-foot bride, and I love telling people I did this for a job, because everybody always wants to know, 'Who are these freaks in real life?' (laughs) Hello. I painted myself white one day, stood on a box, put a hat or a can at my feet, and when someone came by and dropped in money, I handed them a flower – and some intense eye contact. And if they didn't take the flower, I threw in a gesture of sadness and longing – as they walked away.

Acclaimed actor Kevin Spacey's latest cinematic endeavour, "Beyond the Sea", made its UK debut in London's iconic Leicester Square, drawing in a star-studded crowd. The film, a poignant tribute to the life of 1950s music legend Bobby Darin, marks a triple triumph for Spacey, who not only stars in the title role but also wrote and directed the movie. In a remarkable display of his vocal prowess, Spacey performs all 18 songs featured on the film's soundtrack, a testament to his admiration for Darin's remarkable talent. Joining Spacey on the red carpet was his co-star, Kate Bosworth, who brings to life the role of Sandra Dee, Darin's wife and a renowned Hollywood actress in her own right. Bosworth, who attended the premiere with her British partner Orlando Bloom, confessed to having been unfamiliar with Darin's work prior to the film, but has since become an ardent fan.

Weight pruning has been widely recognized as a straightforward and effective method for eliminating redundancy in deep neural networks (DNNs), thereby achieving acceleration on various platforms. However, most pruning techniques are essentially trade-offs between model accuracy and regularity, leading to impaired inference accuracy and limited on-device acceleration performance. To address this issue, we introduce a novel sparsity dimension, namely pattern-based sparsity, which combines pattern and connectivity sparsity, and proves to be highly accurate and hardware-friendly. With carefully designed patterns, our approach consistently achieves accuracy enhancement and better feature extraction ability on different DNN structures and datasets, and our pattern-aware pruning framework also enables pattern library extraction, pattern selection, pattern, and connectivity pruning and weight training simultaneously. Our approach naturally fits seamlessly into compiler optimization for highly efficient DNN execution on mobile platforms.

The article proposes a new protocol for sequential measurements of a quantum system, which is based on the principles of stochastic dynamics and zeno physics. The protocol aims to confine and protect quantum dynamics from decoherence in accordance with the Zeno physics, by means of repeated measurements and dynamical decoupling control pulses. The protocol consists of three main tasks:
 (i) analytically predict the probability that a quantum system is transferred into a target quantum state by means of stochastic sequential measurements. (ii) analytically predict the probability that a quantum system is transferred into a target quantum state by means of stochastic sequential measurements. (iii) optimally reconstruct the spectral density of environmental noise sources by orthogonalizing in the frequency domain the filter functions driving the designed quantum-sensor.

The proposed protocol has the potential to enhance our capability to observe and manipulate open quantum systems, thus bringing advances to quantum science and technologies. The protocol can be used to study the dynamics of open quantum systems, which are quantum systems interacting with their environments, and to develop new quantum technologies such as quantum sensors and quantum communication.

The article is written in a clear and concise manner, with a good balance between theoretical background and experimental perspectives. The authors provide a detailed explanation of the Zeno physics and its relevance to the protocol

Where does all the carbon we release go? Carbon works in a natural cycle, present in all living things: in the soil, in the oceans, in the atmosphere, and in rocks deep underground. When plants and other living things die, much of their stored carbon makes its way back to the atmosphere until new plants grow and reabsorb the carbon. But over millions of years, some of the carbon stored in ancient trees and sea life becomes rock, and some becomes fossil fuels: coal, oil, and natural gas. Natural events like volcanic eruptions release some of the carbon trapped in rock. And human activities, like burning fossil fuels, also release some of that prehistoric carbon. Today, humans release around 60 times more carbon than all the volcanoes on the planet each year.

Hands down, my favorite first-person shooter of all time. Run-and-gun Rambos need not apply. Recoil and ballistics are realistic. Single hits to the chest, neck, or head from rifle-caliber weapons almost always result in death unless it's just a graze. Rounds drop over distance, and short, controlled bursts are required to stay on target. There's no magical ammunition pool—if you reload halfway through your magazine constantly, you'll end up with a pouch full of half-empty mags. You can't run forever; sprint for too long, and your aim will suffer as you gasp for breath until you recover. Rest your weapon on your elbows while prone or on a windowsill to steady your aim. Accuracy suffers when standing.

Roxy Music on the Isle of Wight will appear at the Isle of Wight Music Festival, alongside Morrissey, Supergrass, and Idlewild. The festival takes place from June 10-12. Original band members Bryan Ferry, Phil Manzarek, Andy McKay, and Paul Thompson will take to the stage on the Saturday night of the festival. The band is also working on new material, their first since the 1982 album Avalon. Roxy Music was formed in 1971 by Ferry, McKay, and Brian Eno, with Manzarek joining the following year. Their first hit was 1972's 'Virginia Plain', with other hits including 'Street Life', 'Love is the Drug', and the 1981 John Lennon cover, 'Jealous Guy'.

You can tell by the hours I’ve put into this game that I truly enjoy it. This is everything I hoped a tactical shooter from Call of Duty would be. However, don’t buy this game expecting it to play like COD, Battlefield, or any other mainstream shooter. It stands in a league of its own and should be appreciated as such. I can’t count how many times I’ve heard new players complain about dying too quickly. Seriously—god forbid you get shot in the face and actually die. It’s not like that never happens in real life, right?  

Edit: Okay, so I’ve been playing this game for a while now and have invested quite a bit into it. It’s one of the best multiplayer experiences out there—perfect for when you’re not in the mood for CS:GO.

Cardinal Cormac Murphy-O'Connor has criticised the Iraq War, stating that the billions of pounds spent on the conflict in Iraq and the Middle East should have been used to reduce poverty. The head of the Catholic Church in England and Wales made these remarks on BBC Radio 4 and will reiterate his stance during his Christmas Midnight Mass. In his Christmas message, the cardinal denounced the war in Iraq as a "terrible" waste of money. Both he and the Archbishop of Canterbury have spoken out against the war. During his appearance on BBC Radio 4's *Thought for the Day* segment, he criticized the allocation of "billions" to war efforts instead of using those funds to lift people "out of dire poverty, malnourishment, and disease." The cardinal declared that 2005 should be the year to campaign to "make poverty history.

We've been keeping an eye on his swelling, and there's definitely quite a bit of it.
