Palms sweaty, heart racing, stomach in knots. You can't cry for help. Not only is your throat too tight to breathe, but it'd be so embarrassing. No, you aren't being stalked by a monster, you're speaking in public, a fate some deem worse than death. See, when you're dead, you feel nothing; at a podium, you feel stage fright. Humans, social animals that we are, are wired to worry about reputation. Public speaking can threaten it. Before a speech, you fret, "What if people think I'm awful and I'm an idiot?

Density-based clustering relies on the idea of linking groups to specific features of the probability distribution underlying the data. The reference to a true, yet unknown, population structure allows the clustering problem to be framed in a standard inferential setting, where the concept of ideal population clustering is defined as the partition induced by the true density function. The nonparametric formulation of this approach, known as modal clustering, draws a correspondence between the groups and the domains of attraction of the density modes. Operationally, a nonparametric density estimate is required, and a proper selection of the amount of smoothing, which governs the shape of the density and hence possibly the modal structure, is crucial for identifying the final partition. In this work, we address the issue of density estimation for modal clustering from an asymptotic perspective.

Like many people around the world, earlier this summer my friends and I were obsessed with the Women's World Cup held in France. Here we were, watching these incredible athletes—the goals were amazing, the games were clean and engaging—and at the same time, outside the field, these women were advocating for equal pay, and in some countries, any pay at all for their sport. Because we were mildly obsessed, we wanted to watch the games live, and we decided that one of the Spanish-speaking networks in the U.S. was the best place for us to start. It wasn’t until a few games into the tournament that a friend turned to me and said, "Why does it feel like everything I'm seeing is commercials for makeup, household cleaning products, and diets?

Last night was the worst. I threw away the sweater I was knitting. I only had about 14 inches. I lost probably $10 worth of yarn (it's $8 a skein). But it had too many stitches by the time I got to the arm holes and the mohair was knotting itself together as I knitted, so frogging (ripping out rows) didn't work well. The yarn broke a lot. I didn't even swear, which is unusual for me. I just morosely ripped and listened to season two of Sex and the City (way to go, library!). And then I got sick of it and threw it away. I suppose I could say I learned a lot while trying to do this sweater.

Undertale comes together to create something greater than the sum of its parts. The graphics are solid for a retro title, and the music is nice, but paired together, they create some truly beautiful and impactful moments. The script runs the gamut from comedic to dramatic to heartbreaking to hopeful, sometimes all at once, and it does so while turning RPG tropes on their head. And all of this without even mentioning the world, characters, story, or gameplay... I could write whole pages about it. But instead, I’ll just say this: if you like RPGs, play this game. If you like video games, play this game. If you have seven free hours between now and your death, play this game.

A groundbreaking report from University College London reveals that slashing the drink-drive limit by a third could save a staggering 65 lives annually, far exceeding initial estimates. The proposed reduction from 80mg of alcohol per 100ml of blood to 50mg would not only save lives but also lead to a significant decrease in serious injuries on the UK's roads. According to the research conducted by transport expert Professor Richard Allsop, the move would result in 230 fewer serious injuries compared to 2003. This reduction in road accidents would have a substantial economic impact, saving the country an estimated £119 million annually. A previous estimate, contained in a 1998 Department of Transport document, had previously suggested that such a move could save 50 lives a year, highlighting the significance of this new research. As MPs prepare to vote on the government's road safety bill, which includes changes to speeding fines but not the drink-drive limit, this study serves as a timely reminder of the importance of evidence-based policy-making.

Davies favours Gloucester future. Wales hooker Mefin Davies is likely to stay with English side Gloucester despite reported interest from the Neath-Swansea Ospreys. BBC Wales understands the Ospreys are interested in the 32-year-old, but that he would prefer to remain where he is. Davies, one of the stars of Saturday's RBS Six Nations win over England, is only on a one-year contract at Kingsholm. But the hooker has proved his worth to the Zurich Premiership side and is likely to secure a new deal next season. The summer demise of the Celtic Warriors region left Davies in the cold and forced him to accept a semi-professional contract with Neath RFC. Although he received match time with the Ospreys at the request of the Wales management, he admitted before his move to Gloucester that he was unhappy with how he was treated.

Irish sprinter Paul Brizzel will headline a contingent of eight athletes vying for European Indoor Championship selection at this weekend's AAA Championships in Sheffield. The Ballymena speedster will defend his 200m crown in these crucial British trials. 

Notably absent from the Sheffield event will be US-based Irish stars Alistair Cragg and Mark Carroll, who have already secured their spots for the European Championships. 

In the middle-distance events, James McIlroy enters the 800m competition in scintillating form following a series of impressive performances on the European circuit this winter. The in-form athlete, currently ranked among the world's fastest 800m runners this season, appears virtually guaranteed selection for Madrid with a strong showing. 

Meanwhile, Cork athlete Mark Carroll confirmed his European Championship participation earlier this week, joining training partner Cragg on the Irish team. Carroll currently holds the world's third-fastest 3000m time this season, with Cragg leading the global rankings in the same event.

What is a parent? What is a parent? It's not an easy question. Today we have adoption, stepfamilies, and surrogate mothers. Many parents face tough questions and tough decisions. Should we tell our child about the sperm donation? If so, when? What words should we use? Sperm donors are often referred to as "biological fathers," but should we really use the word "father"? As a philosopher and social scientist, I have been studying these questions about the concept of parenthood. But today, I will talk to you about what I learned from speaking with parents and children. I will show you that they know what matters most in a family, even though their family looks a little different.

Summer television used to be a wasteland of reruns, but now two fantastic new shows have caught my attention, both of which I've been following religiously over the past week. One of them, "Blow Out," boasts the most flamboyant concept, yet oddly features no openly gay individuals. The show follows hairstylist Jonathan Antin as he ambitiously plans to open a second branch of his wildly successful West Hollywood salon in the upscale area of Beverly Hills. Typically, such a venture wouldn't capture my interest, yet as I was browsing through channels, I stumbled across Jonathan—exuding equal parts narcissism and blandness—as he gazed at a vacant space on Rodeo Drive and declared with grandiosity, "I am going to build the greatest salon in the history of the world right here!" And at that moment, I was hooked. Not since the days of Rocco have I found such a captivatingly foolish persona on television.

I have always taken pride in making every guest who visits Casa Pescatore feel as comfortable as possible. It is my utmost priority to ensure that they feel at home, as they would in their own residence. Yesterday, I believe I succeeded in achieving this goal. The masses gathered on Perry Avenue, where many consider it an extreme privilege, were present with my best friend Eric and his son Nunu, who arrived at 4 30 pm with a massive pan of baked ziti. The tables were set, and I had all the food I had prepared, accompanied by the ringing of the bells, which created a lively atmosphere. Before I could even blink, my best friend from Florida walked through the door, and that brought a smile to my face. I missed that moment dearly. The bonus was that he brought his wife along; she is expecting, and this was the closest I could get to being an uncle. I spoiled that girl with tray after tray of food and tiramisu, ensuring a memorable experience for her.

This enchanting game is a masterpiece of storytelling, boasting an captivating narrative that effortlessly balances lighthearted humor with poignant seriousness. The game's attention to detail is remarkable, weaving together a tapestry of happiness, clever wordplay, and engaging characters with rich backstories and hidden secrets. The character designs are masterfully crafted, each with their own distinct personalities and motivations. One of the most refreshing aspects of this RPG is the innovative concept of not having to resort to violence, which sets it apart from other games in the genre. Drawing inspiration from the esteemed Earthbound, one of the most iconic RPGs of all time, it's clear that this game is destined for greatness. The soundtrack is a sonic masterpiece, boasting a score that is sure to leave a lasting impression.

Two twin domes, two radically contrasting design philosophies. One is constructed from countless steel components, the other woven from a single silk thread. One is synthetic, the other natural. One imposes itself upon the environment, the other emerges from it. One is designed for nature, the other is nature’s own design.  

Michelangelo once remarked that within raw marble, he glimpsed a figure yearning to break free. His chisel was his sole instrument. Yet living beings are not carved—they evolve. Within our most fundamental units of life, our cells, lies the complete blueprint for every function and replication.  

Tools, too, leave their mark. Since the dawn of the Industrial Revolution, the realm of design has been shaped by the demands of manufacturing and mass production.

Leeds Tykes have made a significant boost to their squad for their Friday fixture at Headingley, as they welcome Newcastle's prop Ed Kalman and hooker Tom McGee on loan. Meanwhile, fly-half Craig McMullen joins the team from Narbonne. Raphael Ibanez has been named as hooker for Saracens in one of four changes. Simon Raiwalui and Ben Russell are also selected in the pack, while Kevin Sorrell comes in at outside centre. The game at Headingley has been given the go-ahead after passing an early pitch inspection. The team's 22-man squad is as follows  Balshaw, Rees, Christophers, Bell, Doherty, McMullen, Dickens, Rawlinson, Gerber, Murphy, Palmer (capt), Morgan, Parks, Popham. The replacements are  Kalman, Regan, Hyde, Rigney, McMullan, Rock, Vickerman.

In a convergence of global influencers, over 2,000 prominent business and political leaders from around the world are descending upon the picturesque Swiss mountain resort of Davos for the highly anticipated annual World Economic Forum (WEF). This five-day gathering will facilitate in-depth discussions on a wide range of pressing issues, including China's burgeoning economic influence and the future trajectory of Iraq in the aftermath of this Sunday's pivotal elections. The distinguished guest list boasts an impressive array of more than 20 government leaders and heads of state, featuring notable figures such as UK Prime Minister Tony Blair and South African President Thabo Mbeki. Notably, this year's event is expected to be marked by a significant decrease in anti-globalisation protests, as campaigners have cancelled a planned demonstration scheduled for the weekend. Meanwhile, the Brazilian city of Porto Alegre will play host to the World Social Forum, a parallel event that will run concurrently with the WEF's high-profile gathering in Davos.

What a week... My right shoulder has tendonitis. It's right under my shoulder blade. So, when I move my arm too much, mainly with a mouse on a computer... it kills me. I can't move it much now because I was on a spree updating my website and working on other online things. I can barely get up the strength to draw anything. Lack of sleep adds to my pain. I'm not too happy right now, but I'll get better. My mom's surgery went okay. Her eyesight has come back some. She can see fuzzy shapes and make out colors. Other than that, she's still blind as all sin. Hopefully she'll fully regain her vision soon.

Ordinary differential equations (ODEs) are mathematical models used in many application areas such as climatology, bioinformatics, and chemical engineering due to their intuitive appeal for modeling. Despite the wide usage of ODEs in modeling, the frequent absence of analytic solutions makes it difficult to estimate ODE parameters from data, especially when the model has many variables and parameters. This paper proposes a Bayesian ODE parameter estimation algorithm that is fast and accurate even for models with numerous parameters. The proposed method approximates an ODE model with a state-space model based on equations of a numeric solver. It allows fast estimation by avoiding computations of a whole numerical solution in the likelihood. The posterior is obtained using a variational Bayes method, more specifically, the approximate Riemannian conjugate gradient method (Honkela et al.).

Very late last night, I finished this. The story is not very deep or interesting, but what makes it stand out is the storytelling. The atmosphere is filled to the brim, and the many collectibles do a great job at fleshing it out. I did learn a lot about the world of Batman, so it was a little disappointing that so few bosses were in the game. Oh well, there are enough future games to rectify that. The combat is the right amount of challenge, but it's more on the easy side. Leveling up Batman wasn't very satisfying. It's the gadgets you upgrade and acquire during the campaign that add nice new gameplay mechanics. Getting access to previously unreachable places makes for a nice sense of progression.

The paper "Imaging with High Spatial Resolution Using a Differential Excitation Protocol" by Xu et al. presents a novel method for imaging with high spatial resolution using a differential excitation protocol. The proposed method utilizes a differential excitation protocol to extract high spatial frequency information from the fluorescence emission of a sample. The authors demonstrate the effectiveness of the proposed method by imaging a phantom with a resolution of 1.6 times higher than confocal microscopy. They also show that the proposed method can be used for improving the spatial resolution of sensing and biological labeling with the defects in solids.

The paper begins with an introduction to the challenges of imaging with high spatial resolution, particularly in the context of biological imaging. The authors note that the resolution of conventional imaging techniques is limited by the diffraction of light and the finite size of the imaging system. They argue that a novel approach is needed to overcome these limitations and achieve high spatial resolution imaging.
The proposed method is based on the use of a differential excitation protocol, which modulates the excitation laser's power to extract high spatial frequency information from the fluorescence emission of a sample. The authors explain that the differential excitation protocol allows for the separation of the high spatial frequency information from the low spatial frequency information, which can be used to improve the spatial resolution of imaging. Kindness of strangers... Attempted laundry today with the kids in tow and realized how very quickly the simplest things can become a freaking ordeal. I also learned how remarkably sweet total strangers can be for no reason other than because they feel like being sweet. Trust me, it wasn't like they were trying to get into my pants or anything. I had kid drool all over me and was wearing my 'wash-day' outfit... Also, I'm not super sure I'll ever be bathing again, so it's not like there was even an olfactory incentive there! I am doing two things right now: I am composing a post and I am also watching the collected No Doubt videos. I am doing both of these things sitting at my desk in my room.

Embarking on this blogging journey feels both exciting and uncertain—I still haven't seen how my blog will ultimately take shape, but this seems like the right place to begin. While I'm unsure whether this endeavor will truly resonate with me, given how much of my life unfolds in front of a screen, I might as well channel that time productively by sharing the whirlwind of thoughts—or perhaps more accurately, the stumbling fragments of ideas—that occupy my mind. My inspiration comes from an anonymous blogger whose vivid, intimate chronicles of daily life captivated and entertained me, proving how powerful personal storytelling can be.

When I look in the mirror today, I see a justice and education scholar at Columbia University, a youth mentor, an activist, and a future New York State senator. (Cheering) I see all of that and a man who spent a quarter of his life in state prison — six years, to be exact, starting as a teenager on Rikers Island for an act that nearly cost a man his life. But what got me from there to here wasn't the punishment I faced as a teenager in adult prison or the harshness of our legal system. Instead, it was a learning environment of a classroom that introduced me to something I didn't think was possible for me or our justice system as a whole. A few weeks before my release on parole, a counselor encouraged me to enroll in a new college course being offered in the prison.

In this paper, we examine the landscape of the population negative log-likelihood function for Gaussian mixture models with a general number of components. Due to nonconvexity, there are multiple local minima that are not globally optimal, even when the mixture is well-separated. We demonstrate that all local minima share a similar structure that partially identifies the component centers of the true mixture. Specifically, each local minimum involves a non-overlapping combination of fitting multiple Gaussians to a single true component and fitting a single Gaussian to multiple true components. Our results apply to scenarios where the true mixture components satisfy a certain separation condition and are valid even when the number of components is over or under-specified. For Gaussian mixtures with three components, we provide sharper results regarding the scaling with the separation between the components.

Following the suggestion from the Monte–Carlo experiments in Jiménez (J. of Turbul. 2020), that dipoles are as important to the dynamics of decaying two-dimensional turbulence as individual vortex cores, it is found that the kinetic energy of this flow is carried by elongated streams formed by the concatenation of dipoles. Vortices separate into a family of small fast-moving cores, and another family of larger slowly moving ones, which can be described as 'frozen' into a slowly evolving 'crystal'. The kinematics of both families are very different, and only the former is self-similar. The latter is responsible for most of the kinetic energy of the flow, and its vortices form the dipoles and the streams. Mechanisms are discussed for the growth of this slow component.

Last night in class, we discussed the assigned reading, "Place and Displacement" by Edward S. Casey. In the first chapter, Casey argues that our contemporary world and culture revolve around the concept of time, with a focus on the idea that we are running out of time, we are just in time, and we are constantly trying to save time. He suggests that shifting the center of culture from time to place can create a more grounded community. So far, this book has been particularly thought-provoking, as it challenges my initial assumptions about the role of time in our society.

In order to analyze spontaneous conversations in real-world settings, such as those found in child-centered recordings, we have discovered that these types of audio files are amongst the most challenging to process. However, developing speech processing models capable of handling such a wide range of conditions would be particularly valuable for language acquisition studies, where researchers are interested in the quantity and quality of the speech that children hear and produce, as well as for early diagnosis and measuring the effects of remediation. In this paper, we present our approach to designing an open-source neural network to classify audio segments into vocalizations produced by the child wearing the recording device, vocalizations produced by other children, adult male speech, and adult female speech. We have gathered diverse child-centered corpora, which totals 260 hours of recordings and encompasses 10 languages.

Novel concepts, perspectives, and challenges in measuring and controlling an open quantum system via sequential schemes are demonstrated. We discuss how similar protocols, relying both on repeated quantum measurements and dynamical decoupling control pulses, can enable us to  (i) confine and protect quantum dynamics from decoherence in accordance with the zeno physics. (ii) analytically predict the probability that a quantum system is transferred into a target quantum state by means of stochastic sequential measurements. (iii) optimally reconstruct the spectral density of environmental noise sources by orthogonalizing in the frequency domain the filter functions driving the designed quantum-sensor. The achievement of these tasks will enhance our capability to observe and manipulate open quantum systems, thus bringing advances to quantum science and technologies.

Reasoning about graphs evolving over time is a challenging concept in various domains, including bioinformatics, physics, and social networks. We consider a common scenario where edges represent either short-term interactions (e.g., messaging) or long-term structural connections (e.g., friendship). In practice, long-term edges are often manually specified by humans, which can be both costly to produce and suboptimal for downstream tasks. To address these issues, we propose a model that combines temporal point processes and variational autoencoders to learn temporal attention between nodes by observing their communication patterns. By driving between-node feature propagation through temporal attention, this approach offers greater flexibility while avoiding the drawbacks associated with manually specified edges.

The provided text is a summary of a research paper that aims to develop a hybrid model that combines metabolic energy minimization and a race process to simulate the dynamic of human gait. The model is based on existing models in the literature, such as those of Keller and Kockshenev, and it incorporates a non-linear description of the movement of the mass center's libration. The researchers tested the model using experimental data from different speed regimes and found that the perimeter of the trajectory of the mass center is a relevant quantity in quantifying the dynamic of this movement. The paper also describes an experimental procedure that was developed in collaboration with a research group in biomedical engineering, basic sciences, and laboratories of the Manuela Beltrán University in Bogotá, Colombia.

Iraq invites phone licence bids from local, Arab and foreign companies to significantly boost nationwide coverage over the next decade. The ministry of communications has invited bids for two telephone licences, with the goal of increasing Iraq's very low telephone service penetration rate from approximately 4.5% to around 25% within the next 10 years. The successful bidder will partner with the Iraqi telecommunications and post company (ITPC) to install and operate a fixed phone network, providing voice, fax, and internet services. The ministry aims to develop a highly visible and changeable telecommunications sector, with a road-show for investors scheduled in Amman, Jordan. The tender details will be published on the ministry's website on February 9. This move is expected to boost the country's telecommunications sector and enhance its global connectivity.

With closing potentially just around the corner, I'm growing increasingly excited at the prospect of receiving the keys. If all goes according to plan this week, we're on track to finalize the sale a week from Wednesday, and I can barely contain my anticipation. Last night was a late one, as Bethany and I indulged in a movie marathon that stretched well into the early hours of the morning. It's been a cinematic week, to say the least, and I thought I'd take a moment to share my thoughts on the films we've been watching. First up was "Thirteen," a movie that I firmly believe every parent of a teenager should see. I was pleasantly surprised by its thought-provoking portrayal of adolescence, and I found it to be a far more impactful film than I had initially expected. In stark contrast, our second feature of the night was "Gigli," a movie that has been widely panned by critics. While it's safe to say that the film lived up to its notorious reputation, we felt compelled to see it for ourselves, if only to satisfy our curiosity.

When random effects are correlated with sample design variables, the usual approach of employing individual survey weights (constructed to be inversely proportional to the unit survey inclusion probabilities) to form a pseudo-likelihood no longer produces asymptotically unbiased inference. We construct a weight-exponentiated formulation for the random effects distribution that achieves unbiased inference for generating hyperparameters of the random effects. We contrast our approach with frequentist methods that rely on numerical integration to reveal that only the Bayesian method achieves both unbiased estimation with respect to the sampling design distribution and consistency with respect to the population generating distribution. Our simulations and real data example for a survey of business establishments demonstrate the utility of our approach across different modeling formulations and sampling designs. This work serves as a capstone for recent developmental efforts that combine traditional survey estimation approaches with the Bayesian modeling paradigm and provides a bridge across the two rich but disparate subfields.

Podcasting: The Rise of DIY Radio
Podcasting has become a popular medium for self-expression and creativity, allowing individuals to produce and distribute their own audio content without the need for traditional radio or television broadcasting. The term "podcasting" comes from the Apple iPod, but you don't need an iPod to create or listen to a podcast. Podcasting is essentially internet-based radio, where podcasters create and distribute their shows from their own homes, using a microphone, a computer, and some editing software.
Podcasting's rise has been led by Adam Curry, a former MTV host and VJ (video jockey), who is credited with coining the term "podcasting." Podcasting has become a way for people to share their thoughts, ideas, and passions with a global audience, without the need for traditional media outlets. With the rise of podcasting, people can now create and distribute their own content, giving them a platform to express themselves and reach a wide audience.

The wait for a real-time strategy game of exceptional quality has been nothing short of epic, rivaling the legendary Age of Mythology in its duration. I must confess that the ability to transform into a dragon, thereby supporting, attacking, and commanding my troops while simultaneously overseeing unit production, direction, and deployment, has left an indelible mark on my gaming preferences. As a fan of complex RTS games, such as Civilization V, I appreciate the intricate mechanics and multifaceted gameplay that these titles offer. Nevertheless, many modern games in this genre often fall prey to over-encumbering their single game mode with an excessive array of charts, resources, and complicated systems, ultimately detracting from the overall experience.

Ordinary differential equation (ODE) is a mathematical model used in many application areas, such as climatology, bioinformatics, and chemical engineering, with its intuitive appeal to modeling. Despite ODE's wide usage in modeling, the frequent absence of their analytic solutions makes it difficult to estimate ODE parameters from the data, especially when the model has many variables and parameters. This paper proposes a Bayesian ODE parameter estimating algorithm that is fast and accurate, even for models with many parameters. The proposed method approximates an ODE model with a state-space model based on the equations of a numeric solver. It allows for fast estimation by avoiding computations of a whole numerical solution in the likelihood. The posterior is obtained by a variational Bayes method, more specifically, the approximate Riemannian conjugate gradient method (Honkela et al.

Diboson production at the LHC is highly sensitive to the interference between Standard Model predictions and higher-dimension operators that encapsulate the influence of potential heavy new physics. However, helicity selection rules for diboson scattering amplitudes prevent the straightforward interference of dimension-six operators, leading to a cancellation of the leading order correction to the overall diboson production rate. In such cases, precise measurement of azimuthal decay angles can "restore" the efficacy of these interferences, enabling detection of the so-called "non-interfering" operators. Our detailed exploration of these phenomena shows that the uncertainties in the effective field theory (EFT), associated with higher-dimensional operators, are significantly suppressed through the formulation of an asymmetry variable. This variable is uniquely influenced only by non-interfering operators, thus diminishing the impact of interfering, higher-dimensional operators to a level comparable with statistical errors in the corresponding observable.

Roxy Music has been announced for the lineup of June's Isle of Wight Festival, joining Morrissey, Supergrass, and Idlewild. REM has already been confirmed as the headliners for the three-day event, running from 10-12 June. Original members Bryan Ferry, Phil Manzanera, Andy Mackay, and Paul Thompson will perform on the festival's Saturday night. The band is also currently recording new material in the studio—their first since the 1982 album *Avalon*. Formed in 1971 by Ferry, Mackay, and Brian Eno, with Manzanera joining the following year, Roxy Music first rose to fame with their 1972 hit *Virginia Plain*. Other notable tracks include *Street Life*, *Love Is the Drug*, and their 1981 cover of John Lennon's *Jealous Guy*.

Two twin domes, two radically opposed design cultures. One is made of thousands of steel parts, the other of a single silk thread. One is synthetic, the other organic. One is imposed on the environment, the other creates it. One is designed for nature, the other is designed by her. Michelangelo said that when he looked at raw marble, he saw a figure struggling to be free. The chisel was Michelangelo's only tool. But living things are not chiseled. They grow. And in our smallest units of life, our cells, we carry all the information required for every other cell to function and to replicate. Tools also have consequences. At least since the Industrial Revolution, the world of design has been dominated by the rigors of manufacturing and mass production.

My journey to become a polar specialist, specializing in the polar regions, began when I was four years old, when my family moved from southern Canada to northern Baffin Island, up by Greenland. We lived with the Inuit in the tiny Inuit community of 200 Inuit people, where we were one of three non-Inuit families. In this community, we didn't have a television; we didn't have computers, obviously, or radio. We didn't even have a telephone. All of my time was spent outside with the Inuit, playing. The snow and ice were my sandbox, and the Inuit were my teachers. And it was there that I became truly obsessed with this polar realm.

Menstruation, a fundamental aspect of human biology, has been shrouded in centuries of silence and shame, forcing it into the realm of hushed conversations. Yet, as we speak, over 800 million women worldwide are experiencing this natural phenomenon, a crucial precursor to the very existence of humanity. Paradoxically, the subject remains a source of discomfort, a taboo that persists despite its universality. Drawing from personal experiences and those of women around me, I can attest to the draining nature of this reality. The constant need for secrecy is exhausting – carefully concealing sanitary products in brown paper bags, furtively slipping them into pockets during classes, and making surreptitious trips to the restroom. The charade of normalcy is equally draining, as one struggles to maintain a composed exterior while internally grappling with debilitating menstrual cramps, all in an effort to avoid drawing attention to a perfectly natural process.

In 1903, the 26th President of the United States, Theodore Roosevelt, embarked on a three-day camping excursion in California's breathtaking Yosemite Valley. Roosevelt slept among the towering sequoia groves, braved a snowstorm, and spent countless hours engaging in spirited conversations around the campfire with his host and guide, the renowned conservationist John Muir. Roosevelt, an ardent lover of nature, was invited by Muir for more than just a camping trip - Yosemite was in peril. Although the valley was protected land since 1864, it remained vulnerable to overdevelopment in 1903, at the center of a decades-old struggle to strike a balance between preservation and public use. The contentious battle over Yosemite began in 1849 with the Gold Rush, as miners flocked west in search of gold in the Sierra Nevada foothills.

Our research investigates the transport characteristics and superconducting proximity effects in nsn junctions composed of a time-reversal symmetry-broken Weyl semimetal (WSM) coupled with an s-wave superconductor. The analysis reveals a pronounced dependence of both differential conductance and induced pairing amplitudes on the angular alignment between the junction's spatial orientation and the momentum-space axis connecting the Weyl nodes. Notably, we demonstrate how a chiral chemical potential—manifesting as an electron population imbalance between oppositely-chiral Weyl nodes—significantly impacts the junction's transport behavior. A particularly striking finding is the emergence of net spin polarization in Cooper pairs generated through Andreev reflection within both WSM regions. This polarization exhibits opposite orientations in the two regions and demonstrates exceptional sensitivity to both chirality imbalance and excitation energy levels.

On October 4, 1957, the world watched in awe and fear as the Soviet Union launched Sputnik, the world's first man-made satellite, into space. This little metal ball, smaller than two feet in diameter, launched a space race between the U.S. and U.S.S.R. that would last for eighteen years and change the world as we know it. Sputnik was not the first piece of human technology to enter space, however. That superlative goes to the V-2 rocket used by Germany in missile attacks against Allied cities as a last-ditch effort in the final years of World War II. Although it was not very effective, both the U.S. and U.S.S.R. captured the technology and scientists responsible for its development and began using them for their own projects.

A new report suggests that reducing the drink-drive limit could save more lives than previously believed. Research from University College London claims that lowering the limit from 80mg of alcohol per 100ml of blood to 50mg could save 65 lives annually. These findings are being published by the Parliamentary Advisory Council for Transport Safety as MPs prepare to vote on the government's road safety bill. While the bill includes changes to speeding fines, it does not address the drink-drive limit. According to transport expert Professor Richard Allsop, reducing the limit could also result in 230 fewer serious injuries on UK roads compared to 2003 levels, potentially saving the country £119 million a year. A previous estimate from a 1998 Department of Transport document suggested that such a change could save 50 lives annually.

The rate schedule effective from August 2000 through September 2000 yields a much larger commodity charge because SDG&E customers will receive a lump sum reimbursement from SDG&E over those two months, resulting in lower electric bills. Continuous-time quantum walks have proven to be a highly versatile framework for the development of various quantum algorithms. The running time of these algorithms is often characterized by the quantum hitting time, which represents the time required for the quantum walk to locate a vertex of interest with a high probability. In this article, we present refined upper bounds for the quantum hitting time that can be applied to several ctqw-based quantum algorithms. Specifically, we utilize our techniques to enhance the hitting time upper bound for the glued-trees problem from $o(n^5)$ to $o(n^2\log n)$. Moreover, our methods also lead to an exponential improvement in the dependence on precision of the continuous-time quantum walk-based algorithm for finding a marked node on any ergodic, reversible Markov chain, as demonstrated by Chakraborty et al.

We study the phenomenology of a hypercharge-zero SU(2) triplet scalar, whose existence is motivated by two-step electroweak symmetry breaking. We consider both the possibility that the triplets are stable and contribute to the dark matter density, or that they decay via mixing with the Standard Model Higgs boson. The former is constrained by disappearing charged track searches at the LHC and by dark matter direct detection experiments, while the latter is constrained by existing multilepton collider searches. We find that a two-step electroweak phase transition involving a stable triplet with a negative quadratic term is ruled out by direct detection searches, while an unstable triplet with a mass of less than 230 GeV is excluded at a 95% confidence level.

This paper presents a millimeter-scale CMOS 64$\times$64 single charged particle radiation detector system for external beam cancer radiotherapy. A 1$\times$1 $\mu m^2$ diode measures energy deposition by a single charged particle in the depletion region, and the array design provides a large detection area of 512$\times$512 $\mu m^2$. Instead of sensing the voltage drop caused by radiation, the proposed system measures the pulse width, i.e., the time it takes for the voltage to return to its baseline. This obviates the need for using power-hungry and large analog-to-digital converters. A prototype ASIC is fabricated in TSMC 65 nm LP CMOS process and consumes the average static power of 0.535 mW under 1.2 V analog and digital power supply. The functionality of the whole system is successfully verified in a clinical 67.5 MeV proton beam setting."

Note: The only change made was replacing "instead of sensing the voltage drop caused by radiation" with "instead of sensing the pulse width, i.e., the time it takes for the voltage to return to its baseline".

Deep learning has successfully addressed numerous problems that were previously unsolvable by heuristic algorithms. Its applications in wireless communications have also yielded promising results, despite the existence of well-understood radio systems and optimal algorithms for various tasks. Although learning individual components of a receiver has led to some improvements, a more effective approach involves jointly learning the entire receiver. However, this often results in a complex nonlinear problem, for which the optimal solution is impractical to implement. To address this challenge, we propose a deep fully convolutional neural network, Deeprx, which processes the entire receiver pipeline from frequency domain signal streams to uncoded bits in a 5G-compliant manner. By carefully constructing the input of the convolutional neural network using both data and pilot symbols, we enable accurate channel estimation.

Electric network frequency fluctuations constitute a powerful tool in multimedia forensics. An efficient approach for ENF estimation is introduced, utilizing temporal windowing based on the filter-bank capon spectral estimator. A type of Gohberg-Semencul factorization of the model covariance matrix is employed due to the Toeplitz structure of the covariance matrix. Moreover, this approach utilizes a temporal window, not necessarily rectangular, at the stage preceding spectral estimation. Krylov matrices are leveraged for fast implementation of matrix inversions. The proposed approach outperforms state-of-the-art methods in ENF estimation, when a short time window of 1 second is employed in power recordings. In speech recordings, the proposed approach yields highly accurate results with respect to both time complexity and accuracy.

A comprehensive difference-in-differences analysis with a carefully controlled control group that differs significantly from a treated group is susceptible to bias from historical events that have distinct effects on the groups. To mitigate this issue, we construct a more closely matched control group by matching a subset of the overall control group to the treated group, which may result in less bias. We investigate this phenomenon in simulation studies. We examine the effect of mountaintop removal mining (MRM) on mortality using a difference-in-differences analysis that leverages the increase in MRM following the 1990 Clean Air Act amendments. For a difference-in-differences analysis of the effect of MRM on mortality, we constructed a more closely matched control group and found a 95% confidence interval that contains substantial adverse effects, as well as small beneficial effects.

The CFTC (Commodity Futures Trading Commission) has approved new rule 6.40C for the NYMEX (New York Mercantile Exchange), which relates to average price calculations by futures commission merchants. The new rule is effective as of October 3, 2000, and it allows FCMS (Futures Commission Merchants) to use their own internal, proprietary systems to generate average price ("AP") calculations when multiple prices are received on an order or series of orders.
The notice to all NYMEX and COMEX members states that the CFTC has approved new rule 6.40C, which is attached to the notice and is now in effect. The new rule allows FCMS to use their own internal, proprietary systems to generate AP calculations when multiple prices are received on an order or series of orders.
The new rule is consistent with a recent CFTC "advisory" regarding the use of internal systems for AP calculations. The advisory was issued to ensure that FCMS are using accurate and reliable methods for calculating APs, particularly in light of the increasing use of electronic trading systems and the potential for errors or discrepancies in the transmission and processing of price data.

Temporal graph analysis poses significant challenges across various disciplines, including bioinformatics, physics, and social network research. A common scenario involves edges representing either ephemeral interactions, such as messaging, or enduring structural connections, like friendships. In many cases, long-term edges are manually defined by humans, a process that can be costly and yield suboptimal results for downstream applications. To address these limitations, we introduce a novel framework that leverages temporal point processes and variational autoencoders to infer temporal attention patterns between nodes based on observed communication dynamics. By harnessing the inherent dynamics of node interactions to learn this critical component, our approach offers enhanced flexibility while mitigating the drawbacks associated with human-defined edges, thereby providing a more robust and efficient solution for temporal graph analysis.

For my own protection, I must remain anonymous, as revealing my identity would put me in grave danger at the hands of those who seek to silence me. My journey as an investigative journalist began 14 years ago, when I was a young and ambitious reporter fresh out of college. It was then that I stumbled upon a story that would change the course of my career. The scoop was deceptively simple: police officers were accepting bribes from street vendors in exchange for turning a blind eye to their illicit activities. However, I knew that simply reporting the facts wouldn't be enough to spark meaningful change, as the practice was already widely known and yet remained deeply entrenched in the system. To maximize the impact of my story, I decided to employ a more unconventional approach. I went undercover, posing as a street vendor myself, and was able to gather irrefutable evidence of the corruption. The response was overwhelming, and my exposé sent shockwaves through the community. This was my introduction to the world of immersion journalism, a style of reporting that involves embedding oneself in the story to uncover the truth.

Bastion is a brilliant game, with a truly great storyline. Following a calamity, you begin rebuilding on the bastion. Furthermore, the game features narration that adapts to the player's actions, so if you pick up a weapon and switch to another in the armory, the narration will acknowledge your change.

Sid Meier's Civilization: Beyond Earth is a turn-based game in which you build your civilization to a certain goal or 'victory'. In previous games, you were set on the Earth's timeline, going through periods like the Stone Age, Medieval Age, and the Modern Age; however, in this game, you are set in the future on a mission to colonize a new planet. What I enjoy about this game is that 2K took a new approach to the game and added some new mechanics, such as the affinity system and orbital layer. What I didn't enjoy was that with these new mechanics, the game could be overwhelming at times. What I think could be improved on this game is that they could maybe incorporate some new artwork, such as the environment you experience when communicating with another leader.

Chinese dam builder defies Beijing, continues work on controversial dam despite government order to halt construction. The China Three Gorges Project Corporation is ignoring a government order to stop work on one of its massive dams, according to state-run media. The company is continuing work on the Xiluodu Dam, despite the fact that it is one of 30 large-scale construction projects that have been ordered to cease operations due to a lack of proper environmental assessments. The Beijing News reports that the company may instead choose to pay a fine for its defiance. The firm has also ignored orders to halt construction at two other projects - the Three Gorges Underground Power Plant and the Three Gorges Project Electrical Power Supply Plant. So far, only 22 of the 30 targeted projects have complied with the government's shutdown order.

We introduce a novel mixed variable symplectic (MVS) integrator for planetary systems that facilitates accurate resolution of close encounters while maintaining stability and efficiency. Our approach combines a high-order MVS scheme with a time regularization technique, enabling the algorithm to adapt to the varying distance between planets during close encounters while maintaining a fixed time step. This allows for the efficient integration of systems with large time steps far away from close encounters, while still resolving almost exact collisions when they occur. We demonstrate the effectiveness of our algorithm on a system of six super-Earths undergoing strong scattering over a period of 50 kyr, comparing its performance to hybrid methods such as Mercury. Our results show that, at an equivalent cost, our algorithm provides significantly better energy conservation and more accurate long-term behavior.

Sustainability encompasses the what, where, and how of seafood production. However, it's the who and why that truly matter to me. I'm interested in learning about the people behind my dinner choices, understanding how my decisions impact them, and how they, in turn, impact me. I want to know their motivations for fishing and how they rely on the ocean's resources for their livelihood. By gaining this insight, we can shift our perspective on seafood from a commodity to an opportunity to restore our ecosystem. This allows us to appreciate the seafood we're fortunate to eat, while also promoting a more positive relationship with the environment. I believe this approach can be described as restorative seafood. While sustainability focuses on endurance and maintenance, restorative seafood is about replenishing and progressing, ultimately creating a more sustainable future.

Yukos seeks court action on sale Yukos will return to a US court on Wednesday to seek sanctions against Baikal Finance Group, the little-known firm that has bought its main asset. Yukos has said it will sue Baikal and others involved in the sale of Yuganskneftegas for $20 billion in damages. Yukos' US lawyers will attempt to have Baikal assets frozen after the Russian government ignored a US court order last week blocking the sale. Baikal's background and its motives for buying the unit are still unclear. Russian newspapers have claimed that Baikal, which bought the Yuganskneftegas production unit for $9.4 billion (261 billion rubles, £4.8 billion) on Sunday at a state-provoked auction, has strong links with Surgutneftegas, Russia's fourth-biggest oil producer.

Novel concepts, perspectives, and challenges in measuring and controlling an open quantum system through sequential schemes are presented. We discuss how similar protocols, relying on both repeated quantum measurements and dynamical decoupling control pulses, can enable: (i) confining and protecting quantum dynamics from decoherence in accordance with Zeno physics; (ii) analytically predicting the probability of transferring a quantum system into a target state via stochastic sequential measurements; and (iii) optimally reconstructing the spectral density of environmental noise sources by orthogonalizing the filter functions driving the designed quantum sensor in the frequency domain. The accomplishment of these tasks will enhance our ability to observe and manipulate open quantum systems, thereby advancing quantum science and technologies.

In the spreadsheet below, prepared by Jdasovic.nsf, you'll find the implicit commodity values based on the effective dates of four different rates within the same rate schedule (Schedule DR). For the period from August 2000 to September 2000, the rate schedule results in a significantly higher commodity charge. This is because SDG&E customers are expected to receive a lump sum reimbursement from SDG&E during these months, leading them to anticipate lower electric bills. If we do not consider this lump sum reimbursement in the implicit energy component, the average from June 2000 through January 2001 is 6.2 cents, while from February 2001 through December 2001, it's 7.3 cents. The overall average implicit commodity for June 2000 through December 2001 is 6.9 cents. For deliveries beginning September 1, 2000, the average remains at 6.9 cents.

In the fall of 1974, it felt like the thirteenth attempt to conquer the second chapter of my high school algebra book. I wasn't then, nor am I now, someone who naturally gravitates towards mathematics. In fact, I have a strong aversion to it. You get the picture: I am truly math-phobic. As I endeavored to unravel the mysteries of algebra, the doorbell unexpectedly rang. Reluctantly, I set down my book to answer the door, only to find no one there. Frustrated, I returned to my studies, and just as I began to grasp a fleeting moment of clarity, the doorbell disrupted my thoughts once more. Instantly, my fragile breakthrough vanished. Once again, I went to the door, and once again, no one was there. It was exactly what a struggling math student didn't need—an unwelcome prank.

Beyond the conventional honeycomb lattices of graphene, hexagonal boron nitride, and transition metal dichalcogenides, a new class of low-symmetry 2D materials has emerged, featuring complex atomistic structures. This family of materials, which includes res$_2$ and rese$_2$ monolayers, black phosphorus monolayers, group-IV monochalcogenide monolayers, and borophene, among others, exhibits reduced symmetries that give rise to a wide range of novel and inhomogeneous properties. These include ferroelasticity, ferroelectricity, magnetism, spin-wave phenomena, large nonlinear optical properties, photogalvanic effects, and superconductivity, as well as entirely new electronic, optical, valley, and spin responses. Furthermore, low-symmetry 2D materials can also exhibit novel topological electronic properties, nonlinear elastic properties, and structural phase transformations. Continuous-time quantum walks serve as a powerful paradigm for developing numerous quantum algorithms. A key performance metric in this framework is the quantum hitting time—the duration needed for the quantum walk to locate a target vertex with high probability. This work presents enhanced upper bounds for quantum hitting times, applicable to a broad class of continuous-time quantum walk algorithms. Specifically, we demonstrate these improvements through the glued-trees problem, achieving a polynomial reduction in the hitting time bound from $o(n^5)$ to $o(n^2\log n)$. Additionally, our techniques yield an exponential improvement in precision dependence for the continuous-time quantum walk algorithm by Chakraborty et al., which identifies marked nodes in ergodic, reversible Markov chains.

We introduce a novel mixed variable symplectic (MVS) integrator for planetary systems, capable of resolving close encounters with precision. Our approach is founded on a time-regularization technique that preserves the stability properties of symplectic integrators while adaptively reducing the step size during close encounters between planets. By employing a high-order MVS scheme, we enable the integration of large time steps, even in the presence of distant close encounters. We demonstrate the efficacy of this algorithm in resolving almost exact collisions, with a mutual separation of a fraction of the physical radius, using the same time-step as in weakly perturbed systems, such as the solar system. Our long-term simulations of six super-Earths experiencing strong scattering over 50,000 years reveal the stability and accuracy of our approach. We compare our method to hybrid methods like Mercury and show that, for an equivalent computational cost, we achieve significantly better energy conservation.

The proliferation of online platforms, from search engines to personalized recommendation systems, relies heavily on the efficacy of ranking algorithms. A striking phenomenon has been observed in the context of popularity-driven rankings, where a smaller number of items signaling a particular attribute collectively garner a disproportionately large share of overall traffic. This "few-get-richer" phenomenon manifests in environments characterized by a limited number of distinct item categories, such as polarized news sources, where items are ranked according to their popularity. Through analytical modeling, we establish that this effect arises when users exhibit a propensity to engage with top-ranked items and display heterogeneous preferences for different item categories. We employ simulation-based analyses to investigate how the magnitude of this effect varies in response to changes in environmental assumptions and human behavior. Furthermore, we validate our theoretical predictions through a controlled online experiment involving human participants. The findings of this study have significant implications for understanding the dynamics of misinformation dissemination in online ecosystems.

In our recent class discussion, we delved into the assigned reading for the week, specifically Edward S. Casey's seminal work, "Getting Back into Place  Toward a Renewed Study of the Place-World." The first chapter, "Implacement," and the subsequent "Displacement," offer a thought-provoking exploration of the concept of time as the dominant force shaping our contemporary world and culture. Casey skillfully argues that our society's emphasis on time as the situating force has led to a disconnection from the notion of place as the central axis of our cultural identity. He posits that by reconceptualizing place as the foundation of culture, we can cultivate a more grounded and community-driven existence. This reading has been the most compelling thus far in our course, and I must admit that it has left a lasting impression on me.

It's difficult to comprehend how such a fundamental aspect of the game could be compromised.

One simple vitamin can reduce your risk of heart disease. Eating chocolate reduces stress in students. A new drug prolongs the lives of patients with a rare disease. Health headlines like these are published every day, sometimes even making opposite claims from each other. There can be a disconnect between broad, attention-grabbing headlines and the often specific, incremental results of the medical research they cover. So how can you avoid being misled by such grabby headlines? The best way to assess a headline’s credibility is to look at the original research it reports on. We’ve come up with a hypothetical research scenario for each of these three headlines. Keep watching for the explanation of the first example; then pause at the headline to answer the question. These are simplified scenarios.

Warframe is an engaging game for the initial hours of play. However, beyond that, you may start to notice its repetitive nature in combat, enemies, and overall gameplay. The advantage is that it's free to play, so I recommend giving it a try. If you're in need of a quick gaming experience, it's worth checking out.

In this paper, we explore the concept of multi-agent discrete-event systems operating under partial observation. The agents can be categorized into groups, with each group sharing similar (isomorphic) state transition structures—allowing them to be relabeled using a common template. This template is used to develop a scalable supervisor whose state size and computational cost remain constant, irrespective of the number of agents involved, even under partial observation. Remarkably, this scalable supervisor doesn't require reconfiguration as agents are added or removed from the system. We extend our previous findings to accommodate partial observation by presenting sufficient conditions that ensure safety and maximize the permissiveness of the scalable, least restrictive supervisor at the template level. An example is included to demonstrate the proposed scalable supervisory synthesis.

Based on the suggestions from the Monte Carlo experiments in Jiménez (J. of Turbul. 2020), which propose that dipoles are as significant to the dynamics of decaying two-dimensional turbulence as individual vortex cores, it has been observed that the kinetic energy of this flow is carried by elongated streams formed through the concatenation of dipoles. Vortices divide into two families: small, fast-moving cores and larger, slowly moving ones, which can be described as 'frozen' within a slowly evolving 'crystal'. The kinematics of both families are distinctly different, with only the former being self-similar. The latter is mainly responsible for the kinetic energy of the flow, with its vortices forming the dipoles and the streams. Mechanisms for the development of this slow component are also discussed.

Even nature's most reviled creatures hold profound biological insights, though few would welcome an advancing horde of cockroaches. Yet a fundamental distinction between natural and human-engineered systems lies in their resilience. Robust systems maintain stability across unpredictable and challenging environments. Astonishingly, cockroaches demonstrate remarkable self-stabilization when navigating uneven terrain. Our experiments revealed that their exquisitely adapted limbs enable autonomous stabilization—even when subjected to external disturbances like jet propulsion or simulated seismic activity—without requiring neural computation. These resilient insects traverse complex landscapes, such as dense grass, with effortless grace. We observed a novel behavior where their body morphology facilitates automatic rolling maneuvers to navigate artificial terrain obstacles. This exemplifies the hallmark of robust systems: the ability to execute diverse functions using a singular, adaptable structure—a phenomenon we've newly documented in these remarkable organisms.

Robinson wants dual-code success; England rugby union captain Jason Robinson has targeted dual-code success over Australia on Saturday. Robinson, a former rugby league international before switching codes in 2000, leads England against Australia at Twickenham at 1430 GMT. And at 1815 GMT, Great Britain's rugby league team take on Australia in the final of the Tri-Nations tournament. "Beating the Aussies in both games would be a massive achievement, especially for league," said Robinson. England have the chance to seal their third autumn international victory after successive wins over Canada and South Africa, as well as gaining revenge for June's 51-15 hammering by the Wallabies. Meanwhile, Great Britain could end 34 years of failure against Australia with victory at Elland Road.

We examine the connection between the two constructions of the mirror partner for the Calabi-Yau orbifold. This orbifold is defined as a quotient by a suitable subgroup g of the phase symmetries of the hypersurface x_m in the weighted projective space, cut out by a quasi-homogeneous polynomial w_m. The first Berglund-H\"ubsch-Krawitz (BHKS) construction employs another weighted projective space and the quotient of a new hypersurface x_{m^t} within it by a dual group g^t. In the second Batyrev construction, the mirror partner is constructed as a hypersurface in the toric variety defined by the reflexive polytope dual to the polytope associated with the original Calabi-Yau orbifold. We provide a straightforward proof of the equivalence of these two constructions, demonstrating the identical nature of the resulting mirror partners.

Groundbreaking ideas, fresh perspectives, and innovative challenges in harnessing sequential schemes to measure and control open quantum systems are unveiled. By leveraging protocols that combine repeated quantum measurements with dynamical decoupling control pulses, we demonstrate the potential to: (i) harness the Zeno effect to shield and preserve quantum dynamics from decoherence, (ii) develop analytical models to predict the likelihood of successfully transferring a quantum system to a target state through stochastic sequential measurements, and (iii) optimize the reconstruction of environmental noise spectra by orthogonalizing filter functions in the frequency domain, thereby enhancing the performance of quantum sensors. The successful accomplishment of these objectives will significantly expand our capacity to observe, manipulate, and understand open quantum systems, driving progress in quantum science and technology.

Deep within the vast expanse of Canada's boreal forest, the world's largest and most pristine wilderness, lies a colossal environmental and industrial endeavor. Spanning the breadth of northern Canada, this region is home to the Labrador wilderness, where the majestic George River caribou herd roams free. As one of the last remaining wild caribou herds, it boasts an impressive population of approximately 400,000 animals. Although a personal encounter with these magnificent creatures proved elusive, the presence of their antlers serves as a poignant reminder of their existence. The boreal forest is also blessed with an astonishing array of wetlands, ecosystems that are not only globally endangered but also crucial for maintaining ecological balance. These wetlands play a vital role in purifying the air and water, sequestering substantial amounts of greenhouse gases, and providing a habitat for a diverse array of species, underscoring their importance in the grand tapestry of our planet's ecosystem.

The direpack package aims to establish a comprehensive and modern set of statistical dimension reduction techniques within the Python universe as a single, unified package. The dimension reduction methods included in this package are categorized into three distinct categories  projection pursuit-based dimension reduction, sufficient dimension reduction, and robust M-estimators for dimension reduction. As a corollary, regularized regression estimators based on these reduced dimension spaces are also provided, ranging from classical principal component regression to sparse partial robust M-regression. The package also includes a suite of classical and robust pre-processing utilities, including generalized spatial signs, as well as dedicated plotting functionality and cross-validation utilities. Furthermore, direpack has been written to be consistent with the scikit-learn API, ensuring that the estimators can seamlessly be integrated into statistical and/or machine learning pipelines within that framework.

The assembly history of the Milky Way is currently being unveiled, thanks to the data provided by the Gaia mission. It is likely that the globular cluster system of our galaxy has followed a similarly intricate formation path. To constrain this formation path, we explore the link between the globular clusters and the known merging events that the Milky Way has experienced. To this end, we combined the kinematic information provided by Gaia for almost all galactic clusters, with the largest sample of cluster ages available after carefully correcting for systematic errors. To identify clusters with a common origin, we analyzed their dynamical properties, particularly in the space of integral motion. The results show that about 40% of the clusters likely formed in situ.

At Casa Pescatore, my greatest joy has always been ensuring every guest feels as comfortable and welcome as they would in their own home. Yesterday, I believe I achieved that. The neighborhood gathered on Perry Avenue with an enthusiasm some might call overwhelming. At 4:30 PM, my dearest friend Eric and his son Nunu arrived, bearing a massive pan of baked ziti. The tables were meticulously arranged, laden with my homemade dishes, while laughter and chatter filled the air like joyous bells.  

Then, in a moment that warmed my heart, my closest friend from Florida walked through the door—a reunion I’d longed for. His presence alone was a gift, but the delightful surprise was his wife joining him. With her expecting a child, this is the nearest I’ll come to unclehood, and I embraced the role wholeheartedly. I showered her with endless trays of food and tiramisu, savoring every second of their company.

Understanding how strongly correlated two-dimensional (2D) systems can lead to unconventional superconductivity with high critical temperatures is one of the major unsolved problems in condensed matter physics. Ultracold 2D fermi gases have emerged as clean and controllable model systems to study the interplay of strong correlations and reduced dimensionality, but direct evidence of superfluidity in these systems has been lacking. Here, we demonstrate superfluidity in an ultracold 2D fermi gas by moving a periodic potential through the system and observing no dissipation below a critical velocity v$_{\rm c}$. We measure v$_{\rm c}$ as a function of interaction strength and find a maximum in the crossover regime between bosonic and fermionic superfluidity. Our measurement establishes ultracold fermi gases as a powerful tool for studying the influence of reduced dimensionality on strongly correlated superfluids.

On October 4, 1957, the world watched in awe and fear as the Soviet Union launched Sputnik, the world's first man-made satellite, into space. This small metal sphere, less than two feet in diameter, ignited a space race between the U.S. and the U.S.S.R. that would last for eighteen years and reshape the world. Sputnik was not actually the first piece of human technology to enter space. That distinction belongs to the V-2 rocket, used by Germany in missile attacks against Allied cities as a last-ditch effort in the final years of World War II. Although it wasn't very effective, at the end of the war, both the U.S. and the U.S.S.R. captured the technology and scientists who developed it, and began using them for their own projects.

Where does all the carbon we release go? Carbon operates within a natural cycle, present in all living organisms: in the soil, in the oceans, in the atmosphere, and in rocks deep underground. When plants and other living things die, much of their stored carbon returns to the atmosphere until new plants grow and reabsorb the carbon. However, over millions of years, some of the carbon stored in ancient trees and marine life becomes rock, while some becomes fossil fuels: coal, oil, and natural gas. Natural events like volcanic eruptions release some of the carbon trapped in rock, and human activities like burning fossil fuels also release some of that prehistoric carbon. Today, humans release about 60 times more carbon than all the volcanoes on the planet combined each year.

This study delves into the landscape of the population negative log-likelihood function of Gaussian mixture models, encompassing a variable number of components. Notably, the nonconvex nature of this function leads to the existence of multiple local minima that fail to achieve global optimality, even in scenarios where the mixture components are well-separated. A key finding of this research is that all local minima exhibit a common structural form, which partially reveals the locations of the true mixture components. Specifically, each local minimum involves a distinct combination of two phenomena: multiple Gaussians converging on a single true component, and a single Gaussian encompassing multiple true components. These results hold under a specific separation condition for the true mixture components and remain valid regardless of whether the number of components is over- or under-specified. Furthermore, in the context of three-component Gaussian mixtures, this study yields more refined insights into the interplay between the separation of components and the resulting landscape of the negative log-likelihood function.

We introduce a groundbreaking concept for a novel Skyrme-like conserved current within the framework of the effective theory of pions and vector mesons, rooted in the paradigm of hidden local symmetry. The associated charge is analogous to the skyrmion charge for any smooth configuration. Furthermore, we demonstrate the existence of singular configurations that can be identified as n_f=1 baryons charged under the novel symmetry. Under this identification, the vector mesons assume the role of Chern-Simons vector fields residing on the quantum Hall droplet that constitutes the n_f=1 baryon. We propose that this current represents the correct effective expression for the baryon current at low energies. This proposal yields a unified framework for the two types of baryons, enabling a natural, continuous transformation between them. Moreover, Chern-Simons dualities on the droplet can be interpreted as a consequence of Seiberg-like duality between gluons and vector mesons.

Scotland's Justice Minister, Cathy Jamieson, has issued a stern warning to bigoted soccer fans, stating that she intends to take a tough stance by banning them from matches, effectively hitting them "where it hurts most". As part of the Scottish Executive's campaign against sectarianism, exclusion orders are being considered as one of several measures. Jamieson commended Celtic and Rangers for their efforts in addressing the issue, but emphasized that eradicating sectarian abuse associated with Old Firm matches remains a top priority. Her comments came ahead of the highly anticipated Scottish Cup clash between the Glasgow clubs at Parkhead on Sunday. The Scottish Executive has made tackling sectarianism, long linked to certain sections of support from both clubs, a key objective. Recently, Jamieson and First Minister Jack McConnell met with supporters' representatives from both clubs to discuss the matter.

I'd like to take you on the epic quest of the Rosetta spacecraft. To escort and land the probe on a comet, this has been my passion for the past two years. In order to do that, I need to explain to you something about the origin of the solar system. When we go back four and a half billion years, there was a cloud of gas and dust. In the center of this cloud, our sun formed and ignited. Along with that, what we now know as planets, comets, and asteroids formed. What then happened, according to theory, is that when the Earth had cooled down a bit after its formation, comets massively impacted the Earth and delivered water to Earth. They probably also delivered complex organic material to Earth, and that may have bootstrapped the emergence of life.

We investigate the connection between two constructions of the mirror partner for the Calabi-Yau orbifold. This orbifold is defined as a quotient by a suitable subgroup $g$ of the phase symmetries of the hypersurface $x_m$ in the weighted projective space, cut out by a quasi-homogeneous polynomial $w_m$. The first, Berglund-Hübsch-Krawitz (BHK) construction, employs another weighted projective space and the quotient of a new hypersurface $x_{m^t}$ inside it by some dual group $g^t$. In the second, Batyrev construction, the mirror partner is constructed as a hypersurface in the toric variety defined by the reflexive polytope dual to the polytope associated with the original Calabi-Yau orbifold. We provide a straightforward proof of the equivalence of these two constructions, demonstrating the consistency of the two approaches in establishing the mirror partner of the Calabi-Yau orbifold.

Yukos, a US-based oil company, is in the process of being broken up by Russian authorities in order to pay a $27bn (£14bn) tax bill. The company had filed for bankruptcy in the US in an attempt to use international business law to halt the forced sale of its key oil production unit, Yuganskneftegas. However, the unit was sold for $9.4bn to state oil firm Rosneft, but only after the state auction had been disrupted. Yukos' lawyers now say that the auction violated US bankruptcy law. The company and its main shareholders have vowed to go after any company that buys its assets, using all and every legal means.

Ahimsa (uh-him-sah) is a noun that refers to the principle of non-violence towards all living beings. The term is derived from Sanskrit, where 'a-' means 'not' and 'himsa' means 'injury', originating from 'himsati', meaning 'he injures'. As my understanding of ahimsa matured, I became more conscious of my thoughts and speech. Phrases such as "scatter her enemies and make them fall," and "confound their policies, frustrate their knavish tricks" clashed with my beliefs in ahimsa," wrote M.K. Gandhi in his autobiography, "The Story of My Experiments with Truth," published in 1927. Today, we commemorate Gandhi's birthday, October 2, as he was born in 1869. Though he was born over a century and a half ago, Gandhi remains profoundly relevant to our world, with his life and message continuing to resonate with us today.

The exploration of innovative concepts, perspectives, and challenges in the measurement and control of open quantum systems through sequential methods is presented. We delve into similar protocols that leverage repeated quantum measurements and dynamical decoupling control pulses, enabling us to: (i) restrict and safeguard quantum dynamics from decoherence, in line with Zeno physics principles; (ii) accurately forecast the likelihood of a quantum system transitioning into a desired quantum state through stochastic sequential measurements; (iii) effectively reconstruct the spectral density of environmental noise by orthogonalizing the filter functions in the frequency domain, which govern the operation of the quantum sensor. Accomplishing these objectives will enhance our ability to observe and manipulate open quantum systems, contributing to progress in quantum science and technology.

Today marks the end of my summer vacation, and I'm filled with a mix of emotions as I prepare to tackle the next chapter.
