'To the moon' is no mere game... Nay, it is an unparalleled experience, and it ranks among the best of my life. Think The Walking Dead is sad? Hohoho! Well, it's no match for To the Moon! Never have I been so emotionally invested in something purely fictional. This masterpiece represents the pinnacle of storytelling in my opinion, and the soundtrack is a beautifully crafted and perfect accompaniment. Yes, the controls can be a bit finicky, and the gameplay may not be its strongest point, but the story is so exceptional that I didn't mind. Plus, the game's humor is great, and it seamlessly integrates with the distinguished nature of the narrative... And the Doctor Who references are endless.

It's not difficult for me to understand why others enjoy this game, but it wasn't for me. I have a vivid imagination and I'm more interested in playing immersive, room-sized games with controllers. While the game's scale is initially awe-inspiring, I also felt that same scale hindered my enjoyment. The learning curve and the time required to do anything interesting at the beginning were too steep for me. Perhaps if I were more patient and had more time for gaming, this would be more engaging. However, I don't have the luxury of time, and there are many other VR games I'd rather spend my time on.

The Bloggies, a prestigious annual award, has officially commenced its voting process to honor the most exceptional weblogs of the year. These online platforms serve as personal outlets for individuals to express their thoughts and opinions. Following the announcement of nominations on Sunday, the official website experienced an overwhelming surge in traffic, resulting in a temporary shutdown due to the sheer volume of visitors. This year's awards feature 30 distinct categories, ranging from the top regional weblog to the best-kept-secret weblog, showcasing the diversity and creativity of the blogging community. The past year has been a remarkable one for weblogs, with a prominent US dictionary recognizing "blog" as the word of 2004. According to Technorati, a leading blog search engine, there are approximately six million active weblogs, with over 12,000 new additions daily. A study by the Pew Internet and American Life research think-tank reveals that a new weblog is created every 5.8 seconds. However, despite this rapid growth, less than 40% of existing weblogs are updated at least once every two months, highlighting the challenges of maintaining a consistent online presence.

World leaders gather to face uncertainty as more than 2,000 business and political leaders from around the globe arrive in the Swiss mountain resort of Davos for the annual World Economic Forum (WEF). Over five days, they will discuss issues ranging from China's economic power to Iraq's future following this Sunday's elections. UK Prime Minister Tony Blair and South African President Thabo Mbeki are among the more than 20 government leaders and heads of state attending the meeting. Unlike in previous years, protests against the WEF are expected to be muted. Anti-globalization campaigners have called off a demonstration planned for the weekend. The Brazilian city of Porto Alegre will host the rival World Social Forum, timed to run concurrently with the WEF’s more upscale event in Davos.

Interfacial spin-orbit coupling in Josephson junctions offers an intriguing way to combine anomalous Hall and Josephson physics in a single device. We study theoretically how the superposition of both effects impacts superconductor/ferromagnetic insulator/superconductor junctions' transport properties. Transverse momentum-dependent skew tunneling of Cooper pairs through the spin-active ferromagnetic insulator interface creates sizable transverse Hall supercurrents, to which we refer as anomalous Josephson Hall effect currents. We generalize the Furusaki-Tsukada formula, which was initially established to quantify usual (tunneling) Josephson current flows, to evaluate the transverse current components and demonstrate that their amplitudes are widely adjustable by means of the spin-orbit coupling strengths or the superconducting phase difference across the junction. As a clear spectroscopic fingerprint of Josephson junctions, well-localized subgap bound states form around the interface.

At a glance: Tory Health Checks

The UK's opposition Conservatives have unveiled plans to introduce health checks for immigrants if they win the general election. Here's a guide to the plan: 

- Individuals from outside the EU coming to live and work in Britain. 
- If they plan to stay for six months or more and hail from countries with high rates of TB, they would need a chest X-ray and further tests if necessary. 
- All people from outside the European Union wishing to stay a year or more will be required to undergo a full medical examination. 
- Tuberculosis, Hepatitis B, and HIV are the conditions of focus. 
- A positive TB test would automatically result in visa application rejections. 
- Other conditions would be addressed on a case-by-case basis.

During a recent class discussion, our analysis of Edward S. Casey's "Getting Back into Place: Toward a Renewed Study of the Place-World" led to a thought-provoking exploration of the interplay between time and place in shaping our culture. The book's initial chapters, which delve into the concepts of "implacement" and "displacement," offer a compelling critique of our contemporary world's preoccupation with time as the primary situating force. Casey astutely observes that our societal narratives often revolve around time, with phrases such as "running out of time," "saving time," and "just in time" dominating our discourse. In contrast, he posits that a place-centric approach to culture could foster a more grounded and cohesive community. While this summary barely scratches the surface of Casey's nuanced argument, it is clear that his work has resonated deeply, making it the first assigned reading to truly capture my attention.

Brizzel to Run AAA's in Sheffield; Ballymena Sprinter Paul Brizzel to Compete Alongside Eight Irish European Indoor Hopefuls

Paul Brizzel, a sprinter from Ballymena, will be among eight Irish athletes competing in this weekend's AAA Championships in Sheffield. US-based Alistair Cragg and Mark Carroll are the only Irish athletes selected so far for the Europeans who will not run in Sheffield. Brizzel will defend his 200m title in the British Trials. In-form James McIlroy will hope to confirm his place in the British team for Madrid by winning the 800m title. McIlroy has been in tremendous form on the European circuit in recent weeks and is currently ranked as one of the fastest 800m runners in the world this winter. Corkman Mark Carroll confirmed in midweek that he would join Cragg in the European Championships. Carroll is currently ranked number three in the world 3000m ranking, with Cragg occupying the top spot.

Deadline approaches for Fiat-GM deal  Fiat and General Motors (GM) have until midnight on February 1 to resolve their disagreement over a potential takeover. The deadline marks the point at which Fiat will gain the right to sell its car division to GM, part of an alliance agreed upon in 2000. GM, whose European operations are currently losing money, no longer wishes to own the unprofitable Fiat unit. Reports of stalled talks sent Fiat shares down 1.2% on Tuesday, following a 4% gain on Monday after hopes of a settlement. The US firm is reportedly offering around $2 billion (£1.06 billion) to extricate itself from the arrangement. It has argued that the deal was voided by Fiat's decision to sell off its finance arm and halve GM's stake through a capital-raising effort. The 2000 deal was the result of a race between GM and DaimlerChrysler to ally with Fiat.

I didn't even swear, which is unusual for me. I just morosely ripped and watched season two of Sex and the City (way to go, library!). And then I got sick of it and threw it away. I suppose I learned a lot while trying to do this sweater.

Hello. This is my first trip, and the first time in my life I'm outside the walls of Gaza. I'm so happy to be here. (applause) My ambition has always been to be a pilot, to fly a plane, to feel free to fly through the sky, to touch the sky. But that didn't happen. Simply, I live in Gaza, where there is no airport, and all borders are closed on every side. We live in one of the biggest prisons in the world. The only thing I can do is just look up at the sky. On some days, we are lucky if we have electricity for four or five hours. When it's cold, we make a fire on the front or on the roof of our homes.

Entanglement is a key resource for quantum information processing. A widely used tool for detecting entanglement is entanglement witness, where the measurement of the witness operator is guaranteed to be positive for all separable states and can be negative for certain entangled states. In reality, due to the exponentially increasing Hilbert space dimension with respect to the system size, it is very challenging to construct an efficient entanglement witness for general multipartite entangled states. For $n$-partite Greenberger-Horne-Zeilinger (GHZ)-like states, the most robust witness scheme requires $n+1$ local measurement settings and can tolerate up to $1/2$ white noise. As a comparison, the most efficient witness for GHZ-like states only needs two local measurement settings and can tolerate up to $1/3$ white noise.

**The Essence of Restorative Seafood**  

Sustainability defines the methods, locations, and species involved in fishing—but for me, the deeper meaning lies in the *who* and the *why*. I seek to understand the individuals behind the seafood on my plate—their stories, their livelihoods, and the ways our choices shape each other’s lives. Why do they fish? How does the ocean sustain them?  

This awareness transforms seafood from a mere commodity into a powerful tool for ecological renewal. It invites us to honor the food we consume while actively contributing to the revival of our oceans.  

So what do we name this vision? *Restorative seafood*. While sustainability focuses on preservation, restoration goes further—it’s about regeneration, growth, and leaving the waters richer than we found them.

When random effects are correlated with sample design variables, the conventional approach of using individual survey weights (constructed to be inversely proportional to the unit survey inclusion probabilities) to form a pseudo-likelihood no longer yields asymptotically unbiased inference. We develop a weight-exponentiated formulation for the random effects distribution that achieves unbiased inference for generating hyperparameters of the random effects. We compare our approach with frequentist methods that rely on numerical integration, revealing that only the Bayesian method achieves both unbiased estimation with respect to the sampling design distribution and consistency with respect to the population generating distribution. Our simulations and a real data example from a survey of business establishments demonstrate the utility of our approach across various modeling formulations and sampling designs. This work serves as a culmination of recent efforts to integrate traditional survey estimation approaches with the Bayesian modeling paradigm, bridging the gap between these two rich but disparate subfields.

My journey to become a polar specialist, photographing and specializing in the polar regions, began when I was four years old, when my family moved from southern Canada to northern Baffin Island, near Greenland. There, we lived with the Inuit in a tiny Inuit community of 200 people, where we were one of three non-Inuit families. In this community, we didn't have a television, computers, or radio, and we didn't even have a telephone. As a result, I spent all my time outside with the Inuit, playing. The snow and ice were my sandbox, and the Inuit were my teachers. That's where I became truly obsessed with the polar realm.

Hollywood stars Kevin Spacey and Kate Bosworth attended the UK debut of the new film *Beyond the Sea* at its British premiere in London’s Leicester Square on Thursday. Spacey, 45, who wrote, directed, and starred in the movie, portrays 1950s crooner Bobby Darin in this biographical tribute. "This is my homage to someone I consider a remarkable talent," said Spacey, performing all 18 songs on the soundtrack as Darin. Bosworth, 21, plays Darin’s wife—real-life Hollywood actress Sandra Dee. "I knew nothing about Bobby Darin before this film, but now I’m a huge fan," shared Bosworth, accompanied by her British boyfriend, Orlando Bloom, at the premiere.

Routing controllability of autonomous vehicles (AVs) has been demonstrated to mitigate the adverse effects of selfish routing on network efficiency. However, the assumption that AVs would readily accept external control by a central agency is unrealistic. This paper proposes a joint routing and pricing control scheme that incentivizes AVs to adopt centrally controlled system-optimal (SO) routing by offering toll savings, while user equilibrium (UE) seeking AVs and human-driven vehicles (HDVs) are subject to a congestion charge. The problem is formulated as a bi-level optimization, where dynamic tolls are optimized at the upper level, and the lower level employs a mixed equilibrium simulation-based dynamic traffic assignment model that accounts for a mixed fleet of AVs and HDVs.

Sustainability represents the essence of what, where, and how seafood is caught. The who and the why behind my dinner choices are what's most important to me. I want to know the people behind my dinner choices, and how I impact them. I want to know how they impact me, and why they fish. I want to know how they rely on the ocean's bounty for their very existence. Understanding all of this enables us to shift our perspective on seafood from a mere commodity to an opportunity to restore our ecosystem. It allows us to celebrate the seafood we're fortunate to enjoy, while also recognizing the importance of preserving the ocean's resources for future generations. This holistic approach is what we call restorative seafood, where sustainability is the capacity to endure and maintain, and restorative is the ability to replenish and progress.

When random effects correlate with survey design variables, conventional methods—which use individual sampling weights (calculated as inverses of inclusion probabilities) to create pseudo-likelihoods—fail to deliver asymptotically unbiased results. To address this, we introduce a novel weight-exponentiation framework for random effects distributions, ensuring unbiased inference for hyperparameter estimation.

Through simulations and a real-world business survey application, we demonstrate the versatility of our method across diverse models and sampling schemes.

When I look in the mirror today, I see a justice and education scholar at Columbia University, a youth mentor, an activist, and a future New York State Senator. (Cheering) I see all of that and a man who spent a quarter of his life in state prison - six years, to be exact, starting as a teenager on Rikers Island for an act that nearly cost a man his life. But what got me from there to here wasn't the punishment I faced as a teenager in adult prison or the harshness of our legal system. Instead, it was a learning environment in a classroom that introduced me to something I didn't think was possible for me or our justice system as a whole. A few weeks before my release on parole, a counselor encouraged me to enroll in a new college course being offered in the prison, which ultimately changed the trajectory of my life.

El Paso Pipeline to Test Market Interest in Mainline Expansion
El Paso Pipeline, a subsidiary of Kinder Morgan, Inc., is set to test market interest in a mainline expansion to address the current capacity constraint at the California border. The pipeline has announced an open season for the expansion, which will run from February 17 to March 17, 2023, to gauge shipper interest in adding 14.4 billion cubic feet per day (Bcf/d) of firm transportation capacity. This expansion is aimed at relieving the pressure on the pipeline due to the significant demand and skyrocketing prices in California.The El Paso Pipeline has been under immense pressure from regulators and shippers to expand due to the unprecedented demand and high prices in California. In a letter to the pipeline dated January 3, 2023, Daniel M. Adamson, Director of FERC's Office of Energy Projects, urged El Paso to consider modifying its Line 2000 project to "assist the difficult situation" currently facing the California gas market.

Podcasts signify the rise of DIY radio. An Apple iPod or other digital music players can hold up to 10,000 songs, which is a considerable amount of space to fill. Increasingly, iPod owners are filling that space with audio content produced by an eclectic mix of creators. This phenomenon is known as "podcasting," with its most ardent advocate being former MTV host and VJ (Video Jockey) Adam Curry. Although the term "podcasting" derives from the Apple iPod, you do not need an iPod to create or listen to a podcast. A podcast is essentially an internet-based radio show that podcasters can create, typically from the comfort of their own homes. All that's required is a microphone, a PC, and some editing software. Podcasters then upload their shows to the internet, allowing others to download and listen to them, all for free.

Scotland's Justice Minister, Cathy Jamieson, has issued a stern warning to sectarian soccer fans, vowing to take drastic measures to curb their bigoted behavior. In a bid to tackle the long-standing issue of sectarianism associated with Old Firm matches, the Minister is considering the imposition of exclusion orders, effectively banning perpetrators from attending games. This measure is part of a comprehensive campaign launched by the Scottish Executive to eradicate sectarian abuse from the sport. While acknowledging the efforts of Celtic and Rangers in addressing the problem, Ms. Jamieson emphasized that eliminating sectarian abuse remains a top priority. Her comments come ahead of the highly anticipated Scottish Cup clash between the two Glasgow clubs, which has historically been marred by sectarian tensions. In a recent meeting with supporters' representatives from both clubs, Ms. Jamieson and First Minister Jack McConnell reaffirmed their commitment to tackling this deep-seated issue, signaling a renewed determination to create a more inclusive and respectful sporting environment.

The implantation of metal ions into ceramics has proven to be an effective and controllable method for modifying the surface electrical conductivity of ceramic components, with numerous practical applications. Notably, this technique enables the grading of voltage distribution across high-voltage insulators, thereby enhancing their maximum operational voltage capacity by preventing surface flashover. However, a key concern regarding this method is the long-term stability of the conductivity induced by implantation, particularly when the implanted metal species is prone to oxidation. In this study, we investigate the long-term evolution of surface conductivity in titanium-implanted alumina. Our findings reveal that, after an initial decline of up to 40% within the first few weeks post-implantation, the conductivity subsequently stabilizes, exhibiting only a gradual reduction of approximately 10% over the following year.

Brizzel to run AAA's in Sheffield. Ballymena sprinter Paul Brizzel will be among eight of Ireland's European indoor hopefuls competing in this weekend's AAA's championships. US-based Alistair Cragg and Mark Carroll are the only Irish athletes selected so far for the Europeans who will not run in Sheffield. Brizzel will defend his 200m title in the British trials. Doves have soared to the top of the UK album chart with their latest release, securing the number one spot, surpassing flamboyant US act Scissor Sisters. This marks a triumphant debut for the trio, following the success of their single "Black and White Town", which reached number six. Meanwhile, R&B star Nelly has claimed the top spot on the singles chart with "Over and Over", a collaboration with Tim McGraw. Other notable new entries in the top ten include Girls Aloud, Akon, and Kaiser Chiefs, as well as Futureheads and Usher. The latest re-release of Elvis Presley's "Elvis" (formerly known as "Marie's the Name"), his latest flame, has entered the chart at number three, narrowly missing out on the top spot by one position to Girls Aloud's "Wake Me Up". Hip-hop artist Akon's "Locked Up" has landed at number five, while Leeds-based band Kaiser Chiefs have secured their second chart hit with "Oh My God" at number six.

**Manchester's Doves Claim UK Album Chart Crown with "Some Cities"**  

Manchester rock trio Doves have soared to the top of the UK album chart with their latest release, *Some Cities*, dethroning the flamboyant American act Scissor Sisters. The album's success follows their hit single *Black and White Town*, which previously peaked at number six.  

Meanwhile, R&B superstar Nelly secures the number one spot on the singles chart with *Over and Over*, a collaboration with country artist Tim McGraw. The top ten also features fresh entries from Girls Aloud, Akon, Kaiser Chiefs, Futureheads, and Usher.  

Elvis Presley makes a notable return as his re-released classic *(Marie's the Name) His Latest Flame* debuts at number three, just ahead of Girls Aloud’s *Wake Me Up*. Hip-hop artist Akon holds strong at number five with *Locked Up*, while the critically acclaimed Leeds band Kaiser Chiefs notch their second chart success, landing at number six with *Oh My God*.

We examine the relationship between two distinct approaches for constructing the mirror partner of a Calabi-Yau orbifold. This orbifold arises as the quotient of a hypersurface \( x_m \) in weighted projective space—defined by a quasi-homogeneous polynomial \( w_m \)—under the action of an appropriate subgroup \( g \) of its phase symmetries.  

The first method, known as the Berglund-Hübsch-Krawitz (BHK) construction, involves a dual weighted projective space and the quotient of a corresponding hypersurface \( x_{m^t} \) by a dual group \( g^t \). The second approach, Batyrev's construction, derives the mirror partner as a hypersurface in a toric variety associated with the reflexive polytope dual to the original Calabi-Yau orbifold's defining polytope.  

Here, we present a concise demonstration of the equivalence between these two constructions.

For the past 24 years, I have been a firefighter in Huntington, West Virginia. As firefighters, my team and I are tasked with saving lives and property from disasters such as car wrecks, house fires, and life-threatening medical emergencies. I am a woman leading a department in a male-dominated profession, and 10 years ago, I decided to increase my medical knowledge and received a nursing degree. This was because it became clear that the next big threat facing not only my city but other cities around the country was not the simple disaster where you can ride in like the cavalry, as a firefighter, put out the fire, and leave, feeling like you have made a difference and everything is okay.

Walmart pushes back against critics as two major U.S. corporations launch advertising campaigns to clarify their positions on products and corporate practices. Simultaneously, pharmaceutical company Eli Lilly is preparing a campaign to address what it calls "misleading" claims about its product Prozac. Walmart initiated its response with ads in publications like *The Wall Street Journal*, featuring an open letter from company president Lee Scott, who stated it was time for the public to hear the "unvarnished truth." "There are many 'urban myths' circulating about Walmart," he wrote, "but facts remain facts.

Extreme mass ratio inspirals (EMRIs) can be classified as dry EMRIs and wet EMRIs based on their formation mechanisms. Dry (or the "loss-cone") EMRIs, previously considered as the main EMRI sources for the Laser Interferometer Space Antenna, are primarily produced by multi-body scattering in the nuclear star cluster and gravitational capture. In this letter, we highlight an alternative EMRI formation channel: (wet) EMRI formation assisted by the accretion flow around accreting galactic-center massive black holes (MBHs). In this channel, the accretion disk captures stellar-mass black holes that are initially moving on inclined orbits, and subsequently drives them to migrate towards the MBH—this process boosts the formation rate of EMRIs in such galaxies by orders of magnitude.

This paper presents a millimeter-scale CMOS 64$\times$64 single charged particle radiation detector system for external beam cancer radiotherapy. A 1$\times$1 $\mu m^2$ diode measures energy deposition by a single charged particle in the depletion region, and the array design provides a large detection area of 512$\times$512 $\mu m^2$. Instead of sensing the voltage drop caused by radiation, the proposed system measures the pulse width, i.e., the time it takes for the voltage to return to its baseline. This eliminates the need for power-hungry and large analog-to-digital converters. A prototype ASIC is fabricated in TSMC 65 nm LP CMOS process and consumes an average static power of 0.535 mW under 1.2 V analog and digital power supply. The functionality of the whole system is successfully verified in a clinical 67.5 MeV proton beam setting.

Ranking algorithms play a crucial role in online platforms, encompassing search engines and recommender systems. In this study, we uncover a counterintuitive consequence of popularity-based rankings  the fewer the items reporting a given signal, the more the collective traffic they collectively attract. This few-get-richer phenomenon arises in settings where there are limited distinct classes of items (e.g., left-leaning news sources versus right-leaning news sources), and items are ranked based on their popularity. We demonstrate analytically that this effect emerges when individuals tend to click on top-ranked items and exhibit heterogeneous preferences for the classes of items. Through simulations, we examine how the strength of the effect varies with assumptions about the setting and human behavior. We also conduct an online experiment with human participants to test our predictions. Our findings have significant implications for understanding the dissemination of misinformation.

Two domes, each embodying contrasting design philosophies. One consists of thousands of steel components, while the other is crafted from a single silk thread. One is synthetic, the other organic. One is imposed upon the environment, the other shapes it. One is designed for nature, the other is designed by nature. Michelangelo famously said that when he gazed upon raw marble, he perceived a figure yearning to be liberated, with the chisel as his sole instrument. However, living beings are not sculpted; they grow naturally. Within our smallest units of life—our cells—resides all the information needed for each cell to function and replicate. Tools come with consequences. Since the Industrial Revolution, the design world has been largely influenced by the demands of manufacturing and mass production.

Prince crowned 'top music earner': Prince earned more than any other pop star in 2004, beating artists such as Madonna and Elton John in US magazine Rolling Stone's annual list. The singer banked $56.5m (£30.4m) from concerts, album, and publishing sales with his Musicology tour and album. He kept Madonna in second place, as she earned $54.9m (£29.5m) while embarking on her global Re-Invention Tour. Veterans Simon and Garfunkel were in 10th place, their comeback tour helping them earn $24.9m (£13.4m) last year. The singer's 2004 tour took $90.3m (£48.5m) in ticket sales, and he sold 1.9 million copies of his latest album Musicology.

World leaders gather to face uncertainty as more than 2,000 business and political leaders from around the globe arrive in the Swiss mountain resort of Davos for the annual World Economic Forum (WEF). Over five days, they will discuss issues ranging from China's economic power to Iraq's future after Sunday's elections. UK Prime Minister Tony Blair and South African President Thabo Mbeki are among the more than 20 government leaders and heads of state attending the meeting. Unlike previous years, protests against the WEF are expected to be muted. Anti-globalization campaigners have called off a demonstration planned for the weekend. The Brazilian city of Porto Alegre will host the rival World Social Forum, timed to coincide with the WEF's more opulent event in Davos.

Novel concepts, perspectives, and challenges in measuring and controlling open quantum systems through sequential schemes are presented. We explore how similar protocols, leveraging both repeated quantum measurements and dynamical decoupling control pulses, can facilitate: (i) confinement and protection of quantum dynamics from decoherence, in line with Zeno physics principles. (ii) analytical prediction of the probability of transferring a quantum system into a target state via stochastic sequential measurements. (iii) optimal reconstruction of the spectral density of environmental noise sources by orthogonalizing filter functions in the frequency domain, thereby enhancing the performance of designed quantum sensors. The accomplishment of these tasks will significantly enhance our ability to observe and manipulate open quantum systems, driving advancements in quantum science and technologies.

Cabinet Discontent Over Brown's Cash Reallocation

There is dissatisfaction among ministers concerning plans to divert Whitehall funds to offset council tax bills, as confirmed by Local Government Minister Nick Raynsford. In Thursday's pre-budget report, Gordon Brown reassigned £512 million from central to local government budgets. Mr. Raynsford mentioned having had some "very candid discussions" with colleagues about these plans. However, he stressed that local governments must provide quality services without imposing significant council tax increases. The central government funds are part of a £1 billion initiative intended to assist local authorities in England in maintaining council tax hikes below 5% in the forthcoming year, which is expected to be an election year. According to Mr. Raynsford, nearly all central government departments have a vested interest in efficiently managed local authorities.

The direpack package aims to integrate modern statistical dimension reduction techniques into the Python universe as a single, consistent package. The dimension reduction methods included fall into three categories: projection pursuit-based dimension reduction, sufficient dimension reduction, and robust M-estimators for dimension reduction. As a corollary, regularized regression estimators based on these reduced dimension spaces are also provided, ranging from classical principal component regression to sparse partial robust M regression. The package contains a set of classical and robust pre-processing utilities, including generalized spatial signs, as well as dedicated plotting functionality and cross-validation utilities. Finally, direpack has been written to be consistent with the scikit-learn API, allowing the estimators to be seamlessly integrated into statistical and/or machine learning pipelines within that framework.

The concept of ahimsa, a Sanskrit term denoting the profound principle of non-violence and non-injury towards all living beings, is rooted in the philosophy of refraining from causing harm. Derived from the Sanskrit words 'a,' meaning 'not,' and 'himsa,' signifying 'injury,' this concept has been a cornerstone of various Eastern philosophies. As Mahatma Gandhi's understanding of ahimsa evolved, he became increasingly mindful of the impact of his thoughts and words, as reflected in his autobiography, The Story of My Experiments with Truth. In it, he expresses his discomfort with certain lines from a national anthem, which seemed to contradict the very essence of ahimsa. Today, on October 2, we commemorate the birth of this visionary leader, who was born in 1869. Despite the passage of time, Gandhi's life and message remain remarkably relevant, offering valuable insights and guidance for contemporary society.

In 1964, Bob Dylan, at the pinnacle of his career at the age of 23, is hailed as the voice of a generation, with a seemingly endless stream of classic songs pouring forth from his creative genius. However, a small but vocal minority raises a dissenting voice, accusing Dylan of plagiarizing the work of others. Fast-forward to 2004, Brian Burton, aka Danger Mouse, takes the Beatles' iconic "White Album" and Jay-Z's "The Black Album" and skillfully fuses them to create "The Grey Album." This innovative mashup, dubbed "The Grey Album," sparks an immediate online sensation, prompting the Beatles' record label to send out a barrage of cease-and-desist letters, citing "unfair competition and dilution of our valuable intellectual property." The label insists that "The Grey Album" is, in fact, a remix.

One simple vitamin can reduce your risk of heart disease. Eating chocolate reduces stress in students. A new drug prolongs lives of patients with rare disease. Health headlines like these are published every day, sometimes making opposite claims from each other. There can be a disconnect between broad, attention-grabbing headlines and the often specific, incremental results of the medical research they cover. So how can you avoid being misled by grabby headlines? The best way to assess a headline’s credibility is to look at the original research it reports on. We’ve come up with a hypothetical research scenario for each of these three headlines. Keep watching for the explanation of the first example; then pause at the headline to answer the question. These are simplified scenarios.

Deep hashing methods have demonstrated excellent retrieval accuracy and efficiency in large-scale image retrieval. Optimizing discrete hash bits remains a central focus in deep hashing methods. A common approach is to employ an activation function, such as $\operatorname{sigmoid}(\cdot)$ or $\operatorname{tanh}(\cdot)$, and minimize a quantization loss to approximate discrete values. However, this paradigm may cause an increasing number of hash bits to become stuck in the undesired saturated regions of the activation functions, unable to escape, a challenge we refer to as the "dead bits problem (DBP)." Moreover, the existing quantization loss exacerbates DBP. In this paper, we introduce a simple yet effective gradient amplifier that is applied before activation functions to mitigate DBP.

Podcasting has revolutionized the way people consume music on their Apple iPods or other digital music players. With a storage capacity of up to 10,000 songs, it's no wonder that many podcasters are taking advantage of this space to fill. Led by former MTV host and VJ (video jockey) Adam Curry, podcasting has become a popular way for creators to produce and share their own audio content. The term 'podcasting' is derived from the Apple iPod, although it's not necessary to have an iPod to create or listen to a podcast. Essentially, a podcast is an internet-based radio show that podcasters produce, often from the comfort of their own homes. All they need is a microphone, a PC, and some editing software to create their shows, which they then upload to the internet, making them available for free download and listening.

Interfacial spin-orbit coupling in Josephson junctions presents a compelling approach to unify anomalous Hall and Josephson physics within a single device. We theoretically investigate how the interplay of these phenomena influences the transport properties of superconductor/ferromagnetic insulator/superconductor junctions. The spin-dependent skew tunneling of Cooper pairs through the spin-active ferromagnetic insulator interface generates substantial transverse Hall supercurrents, which we term anomalous Josephson Hall effect currents. We extend the Furusaki-Tsukada formula—originally developed to quantify conventional (tunneling) Josephson currents—to assess the transverse current components, revealing that their amplitudes can be widely tuned via spin-orbit coupling strengths or the superconducting phase difference across the junction. As a distinct spectroscopic signature of these Josephson junctions, tightly localized subgap bound states emerge near the interface.

In this study, we propose an open-source neural network approach to classify audio segments into various types of vocalizations, including those produced by children, other children, adult males, and adult females, using a comprehensive corpus of 260 hours of recordings from 10 languages. Our goal is to develop a robust and versatile model that can effectively handle the complexities of spontaneous conversations in real-world settings, which are often characterized by a wide range of conditions, such as varying speaking styles, accents, and noise levels. This research has significant implications for language acquisition studies, as it would enable researchers to analyze the quality and quantity of speech that children hear and produce, as well as for the early diagnosis and assessment of the effects of remediation. The development of such a model would be particularly useful for understanding how children acquire language and how to improve language skills in children with speech and language disorders.

Hairstylist Jonathan Antin wants to build a second location for his incredibly popular West Hollywood salon, this time in Beverly Hills. Normally, no one would care, certainly not me, but as I was flipping through the channels, I caught a glimpse of Jonathan, equally narcissistic and dull, looking at an empty space on Rodeo Drive and saying, in a public voice, "I'm going to build here the greatest salon in the history of the world ever!" and I was hooked. Not since Rocco have I found such a moron so compelling to watch.

Our research explores the phenomenology of a hypercharge-zero SU(2) triplet scalar field, which arises naturally in models featuring a two-stage electroweak symmetry-breaking mechanism. We analyze two distinct scenarios: one where these triplet scalars remain stable and potentially constitute a component of dark matter, and another where they decay through interactions with the Standard Model Higgs boson.  

In the stable triplet scenario, stringent constraints emerge from LHC searches for disappearing charged tracks and from direct dark matter detection experiments. For the decaying triplet case, existing multilepton collider searches impose significant limits. Our findings demonstrate that a two-step electroweak phase transition involving a stable triplet with a negative quadratic term is conclusively excluded by direct detection bounds. Furthermore, we establish that an unstable triplet with a mass below 230 GeV is ruled out at 95% confidence.

I'd like to take you on the epic quest of the Rosetta spacecraft. For the past two years, it has been my passion to escort and land the probe on a comet. To do so, I must first explain the origin of our solar system. When we travel back in time approximately 4.5 billion years, we find a cloud of gas and dust. At the center of this cloud, our sun formed and ignited. Alongside that, what we now know as planets, comets, and asteroids formed. According to theory, when the Earth had cooled slightly after its formation, comets massively impacted the Earth, delivering water and possibly complex organic material, which may have kickstarted the emergence of life.

Allan Scott remains confident of securing a medal at next week's European Indoor Championships despite a slightly disappointing debut on the international circuit. The 22-year-old Scot finished fourth in the 60m hurdles at the José M. Cagigal Memorial Meeting in Madrid.  

"It was certainly a learning experience, and I still believe I can challenge for a medal next week," said the East Kilbride athlete.  

The race was won by Felipe Vivancos, who matched the Spanish record, followed by Sweden’s Robert Kronberg in second and Haiti’s Dudley Dorival in third.  

Scott, who won his heat in 7.64 seconds, expressed mild frustration with his performance in the final, where he ran 0.04 seconds slower in his first IAAF Indoor Grand Prix circuit final.

The Israeli club is turning to Africa, with four African players, including Zimbabwean goalkeeper Energy Murambadoro, poised to join Hapoel Bnei Sakhnin in the UEFA Cup. Bnei Sakhnin is making history as the first Arab team to participate in a European competition and is set to face the English Premier League team Newcastle United in the first round. Goalkeeper Murambadoro, who gained recognition at the African Nations Cup finals in Tunisia, played a crucial role in Bnei Sakhnin's 6-1 victory over Albania's Partizani Tirana in the previous round. Murambadoro recently moved to Israel after a short period with the South African team Hellenic. The club, which won the Israeli Cup final last season, is based in Sakhnin, near Haifa. Renowned for their strong ethical principles, they are prominent advocates for peace and cooperation within Israel.

Andrea Levy, a previous Orange Prize winner, has garnered the Whitbread Novel of the Year award for her book "Small Island." This victory positions her as the leading contender for the overall prize, having surpassed Booker Prize recipient Alan Hollinghurst's "The Line of Beauty." On another note, Geraldine McCaughrean has triumphed in the children's fiction category for the third time with "Not the End of the World." Each category winner progresses to compete for the Whitbread Book of the Year title, which offers a £25,000 prize. A distinguished panel of judges, including Sir Trevor McDonald, actor Hugh Grant, and author Joanne Harris, will convene on January 25 to select the overall winner, with the announcement to follow that evening. According to bookmaker William Hill, Levy's novel, based in London, is favored to win with 6/4 odds.

For the past 24 years, I have served as a firefighter in Huntington, West Virginia. My team and I are dedicated to saving lives and protecting property from disasters such as car accidents, house fires, and life-threatening medical emergencies. As a woman leading a department in a predominantly male profession, I took a significant step 10 years ago to expand my medical expertise by earning a nursing degree. It became evident that the next major threat facing not just my city, but cities nationwide, was not a one-off event. These aren't situations where you sweep in heroically, extinguish the fire, and depart with a sense of accomplishment, believing all is well.

Ranking algorithms play a crucial role in online platforms, from search engines to recommender systems. In this paper, we uncover an intriguing phenomenon: the fewer items that report a given signal, the higher the collective traffic they attract. This few-get-richer effect emerges in scenarios where there are limited categories of items (e.g., left-leaning news sources versus right-leaning news sources), and popularity governs ranking. We demonstrate theoretically that this effect arises when people tend to favor top-ranked items and have diverse preferences among item categories. Using simulations and experimental data, we investigate how the strength of the effect changes under different assumptions about the environment and human behavior. Our findings have significant implications for understanding the dissemination of misinformation.

Building upon insights from Monte Carlo simulations (Jiménez, J. Turbul. 2020), which revealed dipole structures to be as dynamically significant as individual vortex cores in decaying two-dimensional turbulence, this study demonstrates that the system's kinetic energy is predominantly channeled through extended streams composed of interconnected dipoles. The vortex population segregates into two distinct classes: a collection of compact, rapidly propagating cores and an ensemble of larger, quasi-stationary structures that appear effectively "frozen" within a gradually evolving crystalline framework. These two vortex populations exhibit markedly different kinematic behaviors, with only the smaller cores displaying self-similar characteristics. The larger, slow-moving vortices—which constitute the primary energy reservoir—organize into dipole configurations that coalesce to form the energetic streams. The paper further examines the underlying processes driving the development of this dominant slow component within the turbulent system.

What is a parent? It's not an easy question. Today, we have adoption, stepfamilies, and surrogate mothers. Many parents face tough questions and decisions. Should we tell our child about the sperm donation? If so, when and what words should we use? Sperm donors are often referred to as "biological fathers," but should we really use the word "father"? As a philosopher and social scientist, I have been studying these questions about the concept of parenthood. But today, I will share what I learned from talking to parents and children. I will demonstrate that they understand what matters most in a family, even if their family looks a little different.

The spreadsheet below provides the implicit commodity based on the effective date of four different rates for the same rate schedule (schedule DR). The rate schedule effective August 2000 through September 2000 yields a much larger commodity charge because SDG&E customers will receive a lump sum reimbursement from SDG&E over those two months. SDG&E customers will expect lower electric bills. Assuming that the lump sum reimbursement is not included in the implicit energy component, the average for June 2000 through January 2001 is 6.2 cents and the implicit commodity average from February 2001 through December 2001 is 7.3 cents. The average implicit commodity from June 2000 through December 2001 is 6.9 cents for deliveries starting September 1, 2000, the average would still be 6.9 cents.

Understanding the profound correlations between two-dimensional (2D) systems is a major open problem in condensed matter physics, particularly in the quest to uncover the underlying mechanisms of unconventional superconductivity with high critical temperatures. Ultracold 2D Fermi gases have emerged as clean and controllable model systems to study the intricate interplay between strong correlations and reduced dimensionality. Despite their promise, direct evidence of superfluidity in these systems has remained elusive. In this work, we provide direct evidence of superfluidity in an ultracold 2D Fermi gas by dynamically introducing a periodic potential into the system and monitoring the absence of dissipation below a critical velocity, v_c. Our measurement of v_c as a function of interaction strength reveals a maximum in the crossover regime between bosonic and fermionic superfluidity. This finding establishes ultracold Fermi gases as a powerful tool for investigating the profound impact of reduced dimensionality on the behavior of strongly correlated superfluids.

Four Six Nations captains, including Ireland's Brian O'Driscoll, will lead the Northern Hemisphere squad in the IRB Rugby Aid match on March 5, joining forces for a noble cause. The star-studded lineup also features France's Fabien Pelous, Scotland's Gordon Bullock, and Italy's Marco Bortolami. The match, held at Twickenham, aims to raise funds for tsunami relief, pitting Sir Clive Woodward's Northern Hemisphere team against Rod Macqueen's Southern Hemisphere side.  

Woodward expressed his enthusiasm, stating, "It’s an honor to collaborate with such exceptional talent." Beyond the charitable objective, the event offers him a valuable opportunity to evaluate potential candidates for the upcoming British and Irish Lions tour to New Zealand this summer. "This game is set to deliver an unforgettable display of rugby," he added, anticipating a thrilling contest.

Iraq to invite phone license bids. Iraq is set to invite bids for two telephone licenses, aiming to significantly boost nationwide coverage over the next decade. Bids are being invited from local, Arab, and foreign companies, Iraq's Ministry of Communications stated. The winner will work in partnership with the Iraqi Telecommunications and Post Company (ITPC). The firms will be tasked with installing and operating a fixed phone network, providing voice, fax, and internet services. The ministry expressed a desire to increase Iraq's "very low telephone service penetration rate from about 4.5% today to about 25% within 10 years." It also hopes to develop a "highly visible and changeable telecommunication sector." Details of the bidding and tender process will be published on the ministry's website on February 9. It is also planning a roadshow for investors in Amman, Jordan.

Orientational dynamics in the isotropic phase of a comb-shaped nematic polymer with mesogenic and functional side groups were studied using the Kerr effect and dielectric spectroscopy. For the first time, it was discovered that in a mesogenic polymer, unlike low-molecular-weight mesogens, the relaxation of electric birefringence of a melt above the temperature of the nematic-isotropic phase transition can be represented by a sum of several exponential processes, two of which play a decisive role. These main processes replace each other in a temperature range of about fifty degrees. Dielectric spectroscopy also made it possible to distinguish two processes of orientational relaxation: the first is due to the rotation of the side mesogenic groups, and the second is associated with the motion of the main chain segments.

I appreciated the idea of using that as an offense—perhaps it could have been a way to avoid being burned as a witch. As a non-American blogger, I’ve been somewhat hesitant to engage in some of these discussions, especially those involving flags. However, since I initiated the topic (not the flag-burning), I feel compelled to comment. First, I think the idea of enacting a law against flag-burning is utterly absurd. Once you start giving a symbol—any symbol—that kind of unconditional reverence and protection, you're taking a small yet significant step toward totalitarianism. Symbols are somewhat fickle—they don't inherently represent anything; it’s what they are perceived to mean or made to represent that truly matters.

Roxy Music is on the Isle of Wight bill. Roxy Music will appear at June's Isle of Wight Music Festival, along with Morrissey, Supergrass, and Idlewild. REM have already been confirmed as headliners for the three-day event, which takes place from 10–12 June. Original band members Bryan Ferry, Phil Manzanera, Andy Mackay, and Paul Thompson will take to the stage on the Saturday night of the festival. The band is also working in the studio on new material, their first since the 1982 album *Avalon*. Roxy Music was formed in 1971 by Ferry, Mackay, and Brian Eno, with Manzanera joining the following year. Their first hit was 1972's *Virginia Plain*, with other hits including *Street Life*, *Love Is the Drug*, and the 1981 John Lennon cover, *Jealous Guy*.

My shoulder has been afflicted with tendonitis, specifically the right one, which is located beneath my shoulder blade. The constant strain of moving my arm, particularly when I'm using a mouse on a computer for extended periods, has been exacerbating the condition. The pain is quite debilitating, making it challenging for me to move my arm as much as I'd like. Unfortunately, my recent bout of updating my website and working on other online projects has put a significant strain on my body, leaving me with barely enough strength to engage in any physical activity, including drawing. The lack of sleep has only added to my discomfort, making it a particularly trying time for me. Although I'm not thrilled with my current situation, I'm confident that I'll make a full recovery. My mother's recent surgery has been successful, and her eyesight has shown significant improvement. She can now perceive fuzzy shapes and colors, although she still struggles with her overall vision. I'm hopeful that she'll regain her full vision soon.

Before the world of humankind came into being, there existed the divine realm of the gods—a land of sprawling fields, open plains, and lush gardens. Four brothers roamed this celestial domain, bound only by their kinship, for they knew no other family, not even their own parents. Among them, Deminan stood apart, his skin marred by painful scabs, and he often pondered why he alone bore this cruel affliction. One day, while the supreme spirit Yaya tended his gardens, Deminan and his brothers stole into Yaya's dwelling. After indulging in food and exploration, they discovered an enormous gourd suspended in the corner. But as they attempted to peer inside, the gourd slipped from their grasp.

The cross-party group has unveiled a draft bill proposing a smaller second chamber in which 70% of members would be elected. The proposed reform plan aims to raise the pressure on Tony Blair over reform to the House of Lords by publishing a detailed blueprint for change. The group has tried to raise the pressure on Tony Blair over reform to the House of Lords by publishing a detailed blueprint for change. Mps and peers have failed to agree on reform since 1999 when 600 hereditaries lost their seats. The group says it can win support for removing the last 92 hereditaries. The government postponed plans to remove the remaining hereditary peers because they said they were unlikely to succeed after opposition in the lords. Tony Blair has argued. Weight pruning has been widely recognized as a straightforward and effective method for eliminating redundancy in deep neural networks (DNNs), leading to accelerated performance on various platforms. However, most pruning techniques are trade-offs between model accuracy and regularity, resulting in impaired inference accuracy and limited on-device acceleration performance. To address this issue, we introduce a new sparsity dimension, namely pattern-based sparsity, which combines pattern and connectivity sparsity. Our approach achieves both high accuracy and hardware friendliness, consistently enhancing accuracy and feature extraction ability on various DNN structures and datasets. Our pattern-aware pruning framework also enables pattern library extraction, pattern selection, pattern and connectivity pruning, and weight training simultaneously, leading to unprecedented performance gains. By naturally integrating with compiler optimization, our approach achieves highly efficient DNN execution on mobile platforms.

New drink limit 'would cut toll'—more lives than previously thought could be saved by reducing drink-drive limits by a third, a report says. University College London research claims lowering the limit from 80mg of alcohol per 100ml of blood to 50mg would save 65 lives a year. The findings are being published by the Parliamentary Advisory Council for Transport Safety as MPs prepare to vote on the government's road safety bill. The bill includes changes to speeding fines but not to the drink-drive limit. The research, conducted by transport expert Professor Richard Allsop, states that reducing the limit would also result in 230 fewer serious injuries on UK roads compared to 2003. He estimates this would save the country £119m annually. A previous estimate, included in a 1998 Department of Transport document, suggested such a change could save 50 lives a year.

Spintronic nanodevices exhibit ultrafast nonlinear dynamic and recurrence behaviors on a nanosecond scale, promising to enable spintronic reservoir computing (RC) systems. Here, two physical RC systems, based on a single magnetic skyrmion memristor (MSM) and 24 spin-torque nano-oscillators (STNOs), were proposed and modeled to handle an image classification task and nonlinear dynamic system prediction, respectively. Based on our micromagnetic simulation results regarding the nonlinear responses of MSM and STNO with current pulse stimulation, the handwritten digit recognition task demonstrates that an RC system using a single MSM has outstanding performance in image classification. Additionally, complex unknown nonlinear dynamic problems can also be effectively solved by a physical RC system composed of 24 STNOs, as confirmed by the second-order nonlinear dynamic system and NARMA10 tasks.

Over the weekend, Governor Davis and Senator Polanco reached an informal agreement, whereby Senator Polanco has agreed to sponsor the SoCal MOU bill. As a Democrat currently embroiled in a personal scandal, Polanco's political career is reportedly all but over. He is instead planning to run for Mayor of Los Angeles rather than seeking another Senate term. The bill is expected to be introduced in the new extraordinary session, which begins today or tomorrow. It will first be referred to the Senate Energy Committee, chaired by Senator Bowen, who strongly opposes the MOU. As a result, the bill is likely to remain in committee hearings for some time, undergoing changes and negotiations. The longer the bill is mired in committee politics, the greater the likelihood of a SoCal bankruptcy.

The comedy "Sideways" dominated this year's Independent Spirit Awards, winning all six awards for which it was nominated. The film was named Best Film, while Alexander Payne took home Best Director and Best Screenplay, sharing the latter with writing partner Jim Taylor. The movie also secured acting awards for its stars, Paul Giamatti, Thomas Haden Church, and Virginia Madsen. With five Oscar nominations, "Sideways" is expected to perform well at Sunday's ceremony. Now in their 20th year, the Independent Spirit Awards honor films produced outside the traditional studio system and are traditionally held the day before the Oscars. Other notable winners included Catalina Sandino Moreno, who won Best Actress for her portrayal of a drug smuggler in the Colombian drama "Maria Full of Grace," a role that has also earned her an Oscar nomination for Best Actress. Joshua Marston received the Best First Screenplay award for "Maria Full of Grace.

I'd like to share with you the most embarrassing moment that has occurred during my years as a palliative care physician. It happened a couple of years ago. I was asked to consult with a woman in her 70s, a retired English professor, who had pancreatic cancer. I was consulted because she was experiencing pain, nausea, and vomiting. During our consultation, she inquired about whether medical marijuana might alleviate her symptoms. I reflected on everything I had learned in medical school, which didn't take long because I had learned absolutely nothing about medical marijuana.

On October 4, 1957, the world witnessed a pivotal moment in history as the Soviet Union successfully launched Sputnik, the world's inaugural artificial satellite, into the vast expanse of space. This diminutive, two-foot-diameter metal sphere sparked a protracted and intense space race between the United States and the Soviet Union, a competition that would persist for nearly two decades and forever alter the trajectory of human progress. While Sputnik was not the first human-made entity to breach the boundaries of space, it was the first to achieve this feat independently, with the distinction of being the first artificial satellite to orbit the Earth. Prior to this milestone, the V-2 rocket, developed by Germany during World War II, had briefly entered space, albeit as a weapon of war. Following the war, both the United States and the Soviet Union acquired the technology and expertise necessary to harness this innovation, setting the stage for their own ambitious space exploration endeavors.

We explore the phenomenology of a hypercharge-zero SU(2) triplet scalar, motivated by the concept of two-step electroweak symmetry-breaking. We examine scenarios where the triplet is stable, contributing to the dark matter density, and where it decays through mixing with the Standard Model Higgs boson. The stable case faces constraints from disappearing charged track searches at the LHC and dark matter direct detection experiments, whereas the decaying case is constrained by current multilepton collider searches. Our findings indicate that a two-step electroweak phase transition involving a stable triplet with a negative quadratic term is excluded by direct detection searches, and an unstable triplet with a mass below $230\ \mathrm{GeV}$ is ruled out at a 95% confidence level.

When a new user signs up on a website, we typically have no information about them, i.e., no interaction with items, no user profile, and no social links with other users. Under such circumstances, we still expect our recommender systems to attract users at the first encounter, encouraging them to stay on the website and become active users. This problem falls into the new user cold-start category, which is crucial to a company's development and even survival. Existing works on user cold-start recommendation either require additional user efforts, such as setting up an interview process, or utilize side information, such as user demographics, locations, and social relations. However, users may be unwilling to participate in an interview, and side information on cold-start users is usually not available.

Anti-spam laws bite spammer hard: the net's self-declared spam king is seeking bankruptcy protection. Scott Richter, the man behind OptinRealBig.com and billions of junk mail messages, said lawsuits had forced the company into Chapter 11. OptinRealBig was fighting several legal battles, most notably against Microsoft, which is pushing for millions of dollars in damages. The company said filing for Chapter 11 would help it try to resolve its legal problems but still keep trading. Listed as the third biggest spammer in the world by junk mail watchdog Spamhaus, OptinRealBig was sued in December 2003 for sending mail messages that violated anti-spam laws. The lawsuit was brought by Microsoft and New York Attorney General Eliot Spitzer, who alleged that Mr. Richter and his accomplices sent billions of spam messages through 514 compromised net addresses in 35 countries.

It's 11 14 pm MST, and I'm currently in my bedroom fixing the personal computer of my job's personnel director. While I don't mind doing it, I sometimes wonder how often I find myself fixing someone else's computer out of obligation. I'm glad to be able to serve and support people in this way, but I often ponder where the line is drawn where it becomes exploitative. Over the past couple of weeks, I've been staying up late reading and such, so I was looking forward to an early bedtime, until I realized it would be more practical to return the computer to its owner. *chuckle* No rest for the weary, indeed. As I settled in, I found myself watching Joe Schmo 2 again tonight.

Last night, I finally completed this game. While the story isn't particularly deep or interesting, what makes it stand out is the exceptional storytelling. The atmosphere is richly detailed and the many collectibles do a great job of fleshing it out. I learned a lot about the world of Batman, so it was a bit disappointing that so few bosses were included in the game. However, there's always plenty of room for improvement in future games. The combat is the perfect balance of challenge and ease of play, and leveling up Batman wasn't as satisfying as I had hoped. It's the gadgets and abilities you unlock and acquire during the campaign that add the most value to the gameplay. Exploring previously inaccessible areas as you progress is a nice sense of progression.

Where do I even begin with this masterpiece? From its breathtaking visuals to its deeply immersive narrative, I was utterly captivated. The story wasn't merely told—it made me feel like I was living it, unraveling each twist alongside my character. At age fourteen, my family was navigating the complex journey of adopting my younger brothers from Ethiopia. One day, my mother posed a seemingly simple question: "What date should we list as their birthday?" My initial reaction was dismissive—"The day they were born, obviously." It struck me as an absurd question—until she explained, "Kristen, neither of your brothers has a birth certificate. How would you propose we determine that?"  

That moment reshaped my understanding. Two decades later, I’m still pursuing answers—not just for my brothers, but on a global scale. The absence of birth certificates isn’t merely a personal hurdle; it’s a systemic issue affecting millions. So, why does this matter for international development? The answer lies in the invisible barriers faced by those without legal recognition—access to education, healthcare, and basic rights hinges on something as fundamental as proof of birth.

Online reviews provide customers with valuable product evaluations, but these evaluations can be manipulated by professional spammers using increasingly sophisticated and insidious spamming strategies. These strategies are difficult to detect, as they can evolve rapidly, vary across spammers and products, and often go undetected. Most existing detectors focus solely on detection accuracy, which does not align with the goal of maintaining the trustworthiness of product evaluations. To address these challenges, we formulate a game-theoretic approach where spammers and detectors compete in a minimax game, leading to stable detectors that are resilient to any mixed detection strategies.

Manchester United secured a crucial nine-point advantage over Chelsea in the Premier League title race with a hard-fought victory over Manchester City. Wayne Rooney capitalized on a Gary Neville cross to the near post with a low shot, which found the back of the net via a deflection off Richard Dunne. Seven minutes later, an unfortunate Dunne hooked a volley over David James' head and into his own net, giving United a two-goal cushion. Steve McManaman wasted City's best chance of the half when he shot wide from three yards out. In the opening 45 minutes, United appeared unlikely to earn the win they needed to maintain any chance of catching Chelsea in the title race. Their approach play was more labored than patient, and they managed to fashion just one chance - a Paul Scholes header over the bar.

Weight pruning is widely recognized as a straightforward and effective method to reduce redundancy in deep neural networks (DNNs), thereby achieving acceleration on various platforms. However, most pruning techniques essentially involve trade-offs between model accuracy and regularity, leading to impaired inference accuracy and limited on-device acceleration performance. To address this issue, we introduce a new sparsity dimension, called pattern-based sparsity, which includes both pattern and connectivity sparsity, making it both highly accurate and hardware-friendly. With carefully designed patterns, the proposed pruning consistently achieves unprecedented accuracy enhancement and better feature extraction capabilities across different DNN structures and datasets. Our pattern-aware pruning framework also simultaneously performs pattern library extraction, pattern selection, pattern and connectivity pruning, and weight training. Our approach to the new pattern-based sparsity naturally fits into compiler optimization for highly efficient DNN execution on mobile platforms.

Understanding how strongly correlated two-dimensional (2D) systems can give rise to unconventional superconductivity with high critical temperatures is one of the major unsolved problems in condensed matter physics. Ultracold 2D fermi gases have emerged as clean and controllable model systems to study the interplay of strong correlations and reduced dimensionality, but direct evidence of superfluidity in these systems has been lacking. Here, we demonstrate superfluidity in an ultracold 2D fermi gas by moving a periodic potential through the system and observing no dissipation below a critical velocity v$_{\rm c}$. We measure v$_{\rm c}$ as a function of interaction strength and find a maximum in the crossover regime between bosonic and fermionic superfluidity. Our measurement establishes ultracold fermi gases as a powerful tool for studying the influence of reduced dimensionality on strongly correlated superfluids.

My odyssey as a polar specialist and photographer began at the tender age of four, when my family embarked on a life-changing journey from southern Canada to the remote northern reaches of Baffin Island, nestled near the icy shores of Greenland. It was there, in a small Inuit community of 200 residents, that we became one of only three non-Inuit families, fully immersed in the local culture. Devoid of modern distractions such as television, computers, and even telephones, I spent my formative years exploring the vast expanse of snow and ice, with the Inuit as my guides and mentors. This unfettered access to the harsh yet majestic polar environment ignited a deep-seated passion within me, setting the stage for a lifelong fascination with the polar regions.

This study presents an advanced hybrid model that builds upon established human gait theories, including the foundational works of Keller and Kockshenev. By integrating metabolic energy optimization principles with a nonlinear analysis of center-of-mass oscillations during locomotion, the model successfully captures the biomechanical dynamics of walk-to-run transitions. Comparative analysis across varying speed conditions reveals the trajectory perimeter of the center of mass as a critical parameter for quantifying gait dynamics. The experimental validation was conducted through interdisciplinary collaboration with biomedical engineering researchers and laboratory facilities at Universidad Manuela Beltrán in Bogotá, Colombia.

The Catholic Church in England and Wales has called for an end to the war in Iraq, with the head of the Church, Cardinal Cormac Murphy-O'Connor, denouncing the conflict as a "terrible waste of money" in his Christmas message. Speaking on BBC Radio 4's Thought for the Day slot, the Cardinal criticised the fact that "billions" of pounds have been spent on the war instead of being used to help people "out of dire poverty and malnutrition and disease". He also called for 2005 to be a year of campaigning to "make history" and "end poverty".
The Archbishop of Canterbury, Dr Rowan Williams, has also spoken out against the war, saying that it is "a terrible thing to be spending money on this way". He added that the money spent on the war could be better used to help people in need around the world.
The Cardinal's comments come as the war in Iraq continues to be a major issue of debate in the UK. The conflict, which began in March 2003, has resulted in the deaths of hundreds of British soldiers and thousands of Iraqi civilians, as well as a significant deterioration of security in the region.

Imagine if you could plug your brain into a machine that would bring you ultimate pleasure for the rest of your life. If you were given the choice to sign up for that kind of existence, would you? That's the question philosopher Robert Nozick posed through a thought experiment he called the Experience Machine. The experiment asks us to consider a world in which scientists have developed a machine that would simulate real life while guaranteeing experiences of only pleasure and never pain. The catch: you have to permanently leave reality behind, but you'll hardly know the difference. Your experiences will be indistinguishable from reality. Life's natural ups and downs will just be replaced with an endless series of ups. Sounds great, right?

Home phones face an uncertain future, as the traditional fixed-line telephone in households may soon become a rarity. Research conducted by handset manufacturer Nokia reveals that an increasing number of people rely solely on their mobile phones for all calls, whether making or receiving them. The study indicates that over 45 million individuals in the UK, Germany, the US, and South Korea now exclusively use mobile devices. While some retain fixed-line phones due to lower call costs, the majority of respondents agreed that the future undoubtedly lies with mobile technology.  

The Nokia-sponsored research highlighted distinct usage patterns between mobile and landline phones. Fixed-line phones were preferred for longer conversations, whereas mobile calls tended to be shorter, often between mobile users or with friends. In the UK, 69% of participants cited cost savings as the primary reason for still using a landline, acknowledging that it remains cheaper than mobile alternatives.

Optomechanics and electromechanics have enabled the preparation of macroscopic mechanical oscillators in their quantum ground states, in quadrature squeezed states, and in entangled states of motion. Beyond coaxing ever larger and more tangible objects into quantum behavior, this new capability has also sparked ideas for using mechanical oscillators in quantum information processing and communication, as well as in precision force sensing beyond the standard quantum limit. However, the essentially linear interaction between motion and light or electricity limits access to the broader range of quantum states of motion, such as cat states or energy squeezed states. Notably, early optomechanical proposals acknowledged the potential to overcome this limitation by achieving strong quadratic coupling of motion to light. Despite experimental demonstrations of quadratically coupled optomechanical systems, nonclassical states of motion have yet to be realized.

There are currently hundreds of thousands of people on transplant lists, waiting for critical organs like kidneys, hearts, and livers that could save their lives. Unfortunately, there aren’t nearly enough donor organs available to meet that demand. What if, instead of waiting, we could create brand-new, customized organs from scratch? That’s the idea behind bioprinting, a branch of regenerative medicine currently under development. We’re not able to print complex organs just yet, but simpler tissues, including blood vessels and tubes responsible for nutrient and waste exchange, are already within our reach. Bioprinting is a biological cousin of 3D printing, a technique that deposits layers of material on top of each other to construct a three-dimensional object one slice at a time.

To do two things at once is to do neither." It's a great smackdown of multitasking, isn't it, often attributed to the Roman writer Publilius Syrus, although you know how these things are - he probably never said it. What I'm interested in, though, is - is it true? I mean, it's obviously true for emailing at the dinner table or texting while driving, or possibly for live tweeting at a TED talk, as well. But I'd like to argue that for an important kind of activity, doing two things at once - or three or even four - is exactly what we should be aiming for. Look no further than Albert Einstein. In 1905, he published four remarkable scientific papers.

Brizzell to compete in AAA's in Sheffield  
Ballymena sprinter Paul Brizzell will be among eight of Ireland’s European Indoor hopefuls taking part in this weekend’s AAA Championships. US-based athletes Alistair Cragg and Mark Carroll are the only Irish competitors selected so far for the Europeans who will not race in Sheffield.  

Brizzell will defend his 200m title at the British trials, while in-form James McIlroy aims to secure his spot on the British team for Madrid by winning the 800m event. McIlroy has shown outstanding performance on the European circuit in recent weeks, establishing himself as one of the fastest 800m runners globally this winter and virtually guaranteeing his place in Madrid.  

Meanwhile, Corkman Mark Carroll confirmed midweek that he would join Cragg at the European Championships. Carroll currently holds the world number three ranking in the 3000m, with Cragg leading the standings.
